selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically N-0 neck
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET as a better alternative to conventional imaging for nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: In the clinically N-0 neck, PET does not appear to be better than conventional imaging, because of an unacceptably high false-negative rate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Clinically accessible site of suspected recurrence or surveillance after treatment
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on PET for assessment because accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to recurrence and tumour surveillance after treatment, the evidence suggests that sites of disease that are clinically accessible for assessment did not benefit from PET imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal, or treatment may be significantly modified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for M staging and bilateral nodal staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in the M and bilateral nodal staging of all patients with head and neck squamous cell carcinoma where conventional imaging is equivocal, or where treatment may be significantly modified.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Primary site is unknown after conventional imaging
selected_rules.execute: Use PET after conventional imaging and before or in addition to diagnostic panendoscopy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended in all patients after conventional imaging and in addition to, or prior to, diagnostic panendoscopy where the primary site is unknown.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cervical lymph node metastases and the primary site is unknown after conventional imaging or clinical examination
selected_rules.execute: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor
selected_rules.source_statement: One primary study indicated that patients with cervical metastasis and an unknown primary site after undergoing conventional imaging or clinical examination benefit from PET/CT prior to panendoscopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has nasopharyngeal carcinoma and conventional imaging is equivocal for recurrence assessment
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for assessment of recurrence
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging and assessment of recurrence of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma if conventional imaging is equivocal.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Need to detect distant metastatic disease in head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use PET because it is better than conventional imaging for distant metastasis detection
selected_rules.source_statement: There is strong evidence that PET imaging is valuable in detecting distant metastatic disease and is better than conventional imaging.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. The clinician is considering PET/CT to look for nodal disease and to help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
recommended_actions: Use PET/CT prior to panendoscopy to help identify the primary tumor in a patient with cervical lymph node metastases and an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination.
Do not rely on PET for assessment of the accessible neck mass because clinically accessible sites did not benefit from PET imaging.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation to use PET/CT before panendoscopy because the patient has cervical nodal metastasis with an unknown primary after conventional imaging and exam. It also triggers the caution against relying on PET for assessment of a clinically accessible site, since the palpable neck mass is directly examinable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. On PET/CT obtained the prior week for unrelated lung nodule surveillance, there was no abnormal uptake in the neck. The clinician is considering whether to proceed with PET/CT now to look for nodal disease and help localize a primary site before panendoscopy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like cervical nodal metastasis with an occult primary after negative exam and CT, but the key subtle difference is that PET/CT has already been performed and was non-diagnostic, so repeating it is not the same as the original recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order another PET/CT before panendoscopy as if the patient has not been imaged yet.
Use the prior negative PET/CT to reassure against metastatic disease and defer tissue diagnosis.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for PET/CT prior to panendoscopy because the patient already had PET/CT without localization benefit. The safer interpretation is to proceed with standard tissue diagnosis and directed endoscopic/surgical evaluation rather than repeating PET solely to reassess an accessible neck mass.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies when cervical metastasis with unknown primary remains after conventional imaging or clinical exam and PET/CT has not yet been used. Here, PET/CT has already been done and did not identify a primary, so the case no longer cleanly satisfies the intended trigger for obtaining PET/CT before panendoscopy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left level II cervical lymph node that has enlarged over 6 weeks. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, weight loss, or visible mucosal lesion on careful head and neck examination. Flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy is unrevealing, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck does not identify a clear primary tumor. The neck mass is easily palpable and accessible for physical examination and biopsy. Core biopsy of the node two days ago unexpectedly showed metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with a salivary-type primary rather than squamous cell carcinoma. The clinician is considering PET/CT before panendoscopy to look for the primary site.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the workup mirrors an occult head and neck primary, but the pathology from the node is a subtle spoiler: it is not squamous cervical metastasis of unknown origin, which weakens the original recommendation context.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with PET/CT before panendoscopy as if this were a typical unknown primary head and neck squamous cancer.
Schedule panendoscopy primarily to search for an occult mucosal squamous primary without revisiting the biopsy result.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the same guideline scenario because the node biopsy already suggests a non-squamous salivary-type metastasis, so the classic unknown primary cervical squamous-cell pathway does not apply. The safer interpretation is to redirect workup toward identifying the true primary source based on histology rather than assuming PET/CT before panendoscopy is indicated.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation for PET/CT before panendoscopy is aimed at cervical metastases with an unknown primary after conventional imaging or clinical examination, typically in the context of suspected head and neck squamous cell carcinoma. A different histologic diagnosis makes the original rule mismatch the clinical situation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is HER2-positive
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive ... breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: breast cancer is triple negative
selected_rules.execute: Do not use multigene profiling assays because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is currently insufficient evidence to use multigene profiling assays among patients with either HER2-positive or triple negative breast cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering multiple multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for one treatment decision
selected_rules.execute: Do not order multiple different multigene profiling assays on the same tumour specimen for a single decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Requesting multiple tests with different multigene profiling assays on an individual tumour specimen to guide a single treatment decision is discouraged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: node-positive ER-positive HER2-negative breast cancer with higher risk features and considering withholding adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, or Breast Cancer Index to justify withholding adjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The Prosigna, EndoPredict/EPclin, and Breast Cancer Index assays can identify low-risk node-positive patients whose prognostic outcomes are favourable; however, these assays have not demonstrated predictive evidence to support withholding adjuvant chemotherapy among higher risk, node-positive, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, or EndoPredict to extend adjuvant endocrine therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use these assays to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy because evidence is insufficient.
selected_rules.source_statement: MammaPrint, Oncotype DX, Prosigna, and EndoPredict currently have insufficient evidence to guide extension of adjuvant endocrine therapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has lymph node-negative disease or 1-3 positive lymph nodes and is being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy, with supporting clinical/pathological/patient factors
selected_rules.execute: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: Multigene profiling assays are recommended for use in patients with lymph node-negative or lymph node-positive (1-3 lymph nodes) disease who are under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy if the use is supported by other clinical, pathological, or patient-related factors.

selected_rules.activation_condition: one multigene profiling assay is being ordered for a patient
selected_rules.execute: Request only one assay per patient for a specific treatment decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: One multigene profiling assay should be requested per patient to guide a specific treatment decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer and systemic therapy decision is needed
selected_rules.execute: Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with early-stage estrogen receptor (ER)-positive/human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2)-negative breast cancer, clinicians should consider using multigene profiling assays ... to help guide the use of systemic therapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 2 of 12 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
recommended_actions: Use a multigene profiling assay to support the adjuvant chemotherapy decision, since she has node-positive disease with 1-3 positive nodes and relevant clinical/pathological factors.
Request only one multigene profiling assay for this specific treatment decision.
Consider using a multigene profiling assay to help guide systemic therapy in this early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer case.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with 1-3 positive nodes and a real adjuvant chemotherapy decision, which supports use of a multigene profiling assay. The rules also specify that only one assay should be ordered for a single treatment decision. The case does not trigger any rule against assay use, because it is not HER2-positive, triple-negative, or a scenario of withholding chemotherapy based on a low-risk result.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman is seen after lumpectomy for a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. Pathology shows ER-positive, HER2-negative disease with 4 of 14 axillary lymph nodes involved, no distant metastases, and intermediate grade. She is otherwise healthy and is anxious to avoid chemotherapy if it would not add meaningful benefit. Her oncologist is deciding on adjuvant systemic therapy and is considering whether a genomic assay could help refine the chemotherapy discussion. The team plans to use the result for this single treatment decision and does not intend to send multiple different assays on the same specimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer after surgery with a chemotherapy discussion and possible genomic testing, but the nodal burden has increased to 4 positive nodes.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a multigene profiling assay to decide whether adjuvant chemotherapy can be omitted.
Consider the case eligible for routine genomic assay use because it is still ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer.
Use the assay result as the main basis for a de-escalation discussion.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant for the assay-supported adjuvant chemotherapy decision because the patient has more than 3 positive axillary nodes. The safer interpretation is that this is a higher-risk node-positive scenario where the evidence supporting multigene profiling to guide chemotherapy is not the same as for node-negative or 1-3 node-positive disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to node-negative or 1-3 node-positive disease. Increasing the nodal involvement to 4 nodes removes the case from that supported group, even though the receptor status and workflow remain similar.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman returns after breast-conserving surgery for a 1.6 cm invasive ductal carcinoma. The tumor is ER-positive and HER2-negative, and 1 of 12 axillary lymph nodes contains micrometastatic disease. She has no evidence of distant spread and is discussing whether adjuvant chemotherapy can be safely omitted. Because she is hoping to avoid overtreatment, her oncologist considers ordering a multigene profiling assay to help with the decision. The plan is to send a single assay on the specimen and use it only for this chemotherapy discussion.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely resembles a typical early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative node-positive case being considered for genomic testing, but the nodal disease is micrometastatic rather than the usual 1-3 macrometastatic positive nodes.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a multigene profiling assay and use a low-risk result to withhold chemotherapy.
Treat the case as straightforward 1-node-positive disease eligible for assay-guided de-escalation.
Rely on the assay to justify omitting adjuvant chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean match to the rule supporting assay use for node-negative or 1-3 positive nodes, because the nodal finding is micrometastatic and may not fit the intended evidence base. The safer interpretation is that the case should be reviewed under the specific nodal-pathology context rather than automatically applying the assay-guided chemotherapy pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the same overall breast cancer workflow but changes the nodal status in a subtle way. That makes the original recommendation less clearly applicable, since the guideline statement is anchored to standard node-negative or 1-3 node-positive disease under consideration for adjuvant chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed biopsy-confirmed prostate cancer; intermediate- or high-risk; being considered for radical therapy; knowledge of extraprostatic extension would alter management
selected_rules.execute: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
selected_rules.source_statement: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) use for pre-treatment local staging of prostate cancer is a reasonable option for assessment of extraprostatic extension (EPE) in intermediate-and highrisk patients being considered for radical therapy if knowledge of EPE will alter management.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, and the team notes that knowing whether there is extraprostatic extension would affect the surgical plan and whether nerve-sparing could be attempted. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
recommended_actions: Consider multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension (EPE).
source_case.rationale: This is a newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed intermediate-risk prostate cancer case being considered for radical therapy, and knowledge of possible extraprostatic extension would change management. That matches the rule’s activation condition, supporting pre-treatment mpMRI for local staging.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is referred after transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment, but a recently obtained high-quality pelvic mpMRI performed at an outside center already showed no evidence of extraprostatic extension or seminal vesicle invasion. The team feels the operative plan is already clear and that nerve-sparing would not be changed by additional local imaging. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer and is being considered for radical therapy, but the key difference is that a recent mpMRI has already answered the local staging question.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order another mpMRI for pre-treatment local staging to assess extraprostatic extension.
Document that MRI is needed because the case is intermediate risk and treatment planning is pending.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the stated recommendation because the staging question has already been addressed by an adequate recent mpMRI, so repeating it is unlikely to alter management. The safer interpretation is to use the existing imaging for planning rather than reflexively ordering another pre-treatment mpMRI.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule applies when knowledge of extraprostatic extension would alter management and local staging has not yet been established. Here, a recent mpMRI already shows no EPE, so the recommended action is no longer necessary as a new staging step.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man presents after a transrectal biopsy confirmed acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate, Gleason 4+3=7, with PSA 14.8 ng/mL and cancer in 6 of 12 cores. He has no bone pain or urinary retention, and his exam is otherwise unremarkable. His urologist is discussing radical prostatectomy versus radiation-based treatment. On review of prior records, however, he had a radical prostatectomy for prostate cancer 8 years ago and now has a biopsy-proven local recurrence in the prostate bed rather than an untreated intact gland. The team is trying to decide between salvage radiation and systemic therapy, and local staging of the original prostate is no longer relevant. He is otherwise fit and wants definitive treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the details mirror a newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer case, but the subtle change is that this is a post-prostatectomy local recurrence rather than an intact prostate being staged preoperatively.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Consider multiparametric MRI for pre-treatment local staging of extraprostatic extension.
Use mpMRI to decide on nerve-sparing or prostatectomy planning.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the recommendation because the patient does not have a newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed intact-prostate cancer being considered for radical prostate therapy. The more appropriate interpretation is to manage this as recurrent disease, where different imaging and treatment considerations apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: The activation condition requires a newly diagnosed prostate cancer with a radical-therapy decision in the intact prostate setting. A post-prostatectomy recurrence falls outside that scope, so the pre-treatment local staging recommendation does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer for whom radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with cervical cancer for whom treatment with radiotherapy is being considered (described above) should be offered concurrent cisplatin with their course of radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with cervical cancer receiving cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or hydroxyurea
selected_rules.execute: Use concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy because pooled survival favored this approach.
selected_rules.source_statement: Pooled survival rates detected a statistically significant effect in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy compared with radiotherapy alone or with hydroxyurea (relative risk of death, 0.74; 95% confidence interval, 0.64 to 0.86).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk of death among the six trials that enrolled only women with locally advanced cervical cancer was 0.78 (95% confidence interval, 0.67 to 0.90) in favour of cisplatin-based chemotherapy and radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The pooled relative risk for the two trials in high-risk early-stage disease also demonstrated a significant benefit for the addition of cisplatin-based chemotherapy to radiotherapy (relative risk, 0.56; 95% confidence interval, 0.41 to 0.77).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with bulky clinical stage IB cervical cancer (>4 cm) who are treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with bulky clinical stage IB (>4 cm) cervical cancer, who are treated with radiotherapy,

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer after hysterectomy who will be treated with radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with high-risk early-stage cervical cancer (node-positive or margin-positive), who will be treated with radiotherapy following hysterectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Women with locally advanced cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Offer concurrent cisplatin with radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to women with cervical cancer for whom primary treatment with radiotherapy is being considered: -those with locally advanced cervical cancer,

selected_rules.activation_condition: When choosing a cisplatin regimen for concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 based on available toxicity data.
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the review of the available toxicity data from the randomized controlled trials, the Disease Site Group felt that cisplatinum should be given weekly (40 mg/m2).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension but no distant metastases, consistent with locally advanced disease. Her oncology team is planning definitive pelvic radiotherapy with brachytherapy. She has adequate renal function and performance status to receive chemotherapy. The patient asks whether anything should be added to radiation to improve outcomes.
recommended_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with the course of radiotherapy.
Offer concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy with radiotherapy for locally advanced cervical cancer.
Consider weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 when selecting a concurrent cisplatin regimen.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a woman with cervical cancer in whom radiotherapy is being considered, which triggers the general recommendation to add concurrent cisplatin. Because her disease is locally advanced and primary treatment is radiotherapy, the locally advanced cervical cancer rule also applies. The weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 option is included as a conditional regimen consideration based on available toxicity data.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman with biopsy-proven squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pressure. Pelvic MRI shows a 5.5 cm cervical mass with parametrial extension and suspicious pelvic nodes, but staging CT also reveals multiple small bilateral pulmonary nodules interpreted as metastatic disease. Her team is discussing palliative pelvic radiotherapy for symptom control because of worsening pain and bleeding. She asks whether cisplatin should be added to the radiation to improve outcomes. Her renal function and performance status are otherwise adequate for treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still features cervical cancer, a large parametrial tumor, and radiotherapy, which makes concurrent cisplatin seem applicable on a quick read.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with pelvic radiotherapy as if treating locally advanced disease
Recommend weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 during radiation
State that cisplatin-based chemoradiation should be standard because the tumor is bulky and symptomatic
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the cited rules because the patient has distant metastatic disease and the radiotherapy is being planned for palliation, not definitive treatment of localized or locally advanced cervical cancer. The safer interpretation is that the concurrent cisplatin recommendations for definitive chemoradiation do not apply here; management should be individualized to the palliative intent and metastatic setting.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation is tied to cervical cancer cases in which radiotherapy is being considered as part of definitive treatment for locally advanced or otherwise nonmetastatic disease. The presence of pulmonary metastases and palliative intent breaks that assumption, so the cited rules do not trigger.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with postcoital bleeding and pelvic pain. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix. MRI shows a 3.2 cm cervical mass confined to the cervix without parametrial extension; PET-CT shows no nodal or distant metastases. She underwent a radical hysterectomy two weeks ago, and pathology shows a negative vaginal margin but one pelvic lymph node with microscopic metastatic involvement. The oncology team is planning postoperative pelvic radiotherapy and asks whether anything should be added to improve outcomes. Her renal function and performance status are adequate for chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This still looks like cervical cancer with planned pelvic radiotherapy after surgery, which can be mistaken for the locally advanced definitive-radiation scenario on superficial review.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer concurrent cisplatin with postoperative radiotherapy
Recommend weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 during adjuvant radiation
Treat it as the same as locally advanced cervical cancer requiring cisplatin-based chemoradiation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not match the cited locally advanced definitive-radiation recommendations because the patient has already had hysterectomy and is now receiving adjuvant postoperative radiotherapy for node-positive early-stage disease. While concurrent cisplatin may be considered under the separate post-hysterectomy high-risk early-stage rule, the locally advanced cervical cancer rules do not apply here. The safest reading is to distinguish the postoperative adjuvant setting from definitive chemoradiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette intentionally preserves the cervical cancer and radiotherapy workflow but changes the stage and treatment pathway. Node-positive disease after hysterectomy is a different clinical scenario from unresected locally advanced disease treated with definitive radiotherapy, so the triggered locally advanced rules are invalidated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage II colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of adjuvant chemotherapy for all patients with stage II colon cancer is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient without high-risk features who does not require adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision.
selected_rules.source_statement: In stage II (in the absence of high-risk features) where a patient does not require adjuvant chemotherapy, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended as it will not influence that decision.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II colon cancer with MSI/dMMR after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Do not give fluoropyrimidine monotherapy as adjuvant treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adjuvant chemotherapy with a fluoropyrimidine monotherapy regimen following surgery in patients who have MSI/dMMR is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage II colon cancer treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine.
selected_rules.source_statement: When treated with adjuvant therapy, high-risk stage II patients should receive a fluoropyrimidine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: stage II patient being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform MSI/dMMR testing.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSI/dMMR testing should be performed for all stage II patients for whom adjuvant chemotherapy is being considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with resected stage III colon cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use an oxaliplatin-containing regimen such as FOLFOX, FLOX, or XELOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: These oxaliplatin-containing regimens have demonstrated superior overall survival when compared with 5-FU plus LV and are the recommended regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: low-risk stage III colon cancer (T1-3 N1)
selected_rules.execute: Prefer 3 months of CAPOX over FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: Low-risk stage III (T1-3 N1): 3 months of CAPOX is preferred over FOLFOX.

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk stage III colon cancer (T4 +/- N2)
selected_rules.execute: Give 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy with CAPOX or FOLFOX.
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk stage III (T4 +/-N2): 6 months of oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy (CAPOX or FOLFOX).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management.
recommended_actions: Do not use routine adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage II patients.
Do not perform MSI/dMMR testing for this decision because the patient has stage II disease without high-risk features and the result would not influence the adjuvant chemotherapy decision.
source_case.rationale: This is a resected stage II colon cancer case without high-risk features, so routine adjuvant chemotherapy is not recommended. Because he does not require adjuvant chemotherapy based on the available features, MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended for this decision.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management. The only added detail is that a family member mentions the patient is planning to start chemotherapy ‘just in case’ before his next follow-up visit, despite no change in the pathology report.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a straightforward, resected stage II colon cancer after curative surgery with no high-risk features, but the subtle difference is that the patient is considering treatment anyway rather than having a guideline-supported indication.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start routine adjuvant chemotherapy because the patient is anxious about recurrence.
Order a standard fluoropyrimidine-based adjuvant regimen without further risk stratification.
Treat the case as if stage II colon cancer automatically warrants postoperative chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a resected stage II colon cancer without high-risk features, so routine adjuvant chemotherapy is not guideline-concordant. The safer interpretation is observation and shared decision-making rather than automatic postoperative treatment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy still applies, but the vignette is a near-miss because the temptation is to treat based on patient preference alone. Patient anxiety does not create a guideline indication.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man underwent right hemicolectomy for a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma found on screening colonoscopy. Final pathology shows pT3N0 stage II colon cancer with clear margins, 18 lymph nodes examined, no lymphovascular invasion, no perineural invasion, no bowel obstruction, and no perforation. He has recovered well after surgery and is asking whether he should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. His oncology team is reviewing the pathology and discussing postoperative management. During the same visit, the surgeon notes that the pathology report is complete except that mismatch repair testing was not run because the tissue block is unavailable at the original hospital.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Everything still reads like low-risk stage II colon cancer after curative resection, but the hidden mismatch is that the tumor specimen is not available for MSI/dMMR testing, so the decision point is no longer the same.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order MSI/dMMR testing as part of routine decision-making for this stage II case.
Delay management solely to obtain molecular testing from another specimen source.
Assume the result will determine adjuvant treatment even though chemotherapy is not otherwise being considered.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still a stage II colon cancer without high-risk features, so MSI/dMMR testing is not recommended for the purpose of deciding adjuvant chemotherapy. If testing is unavailable anyway, the safe approach is to proceed with standard postoperative surveillance rather than pursuing testing that would not change this decision.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 only applies when stage II disease without high-risk features is not being considered for adjuvant chemotherapy. The vignette preserves that context, but the near-miss is the suggestion to test despite no treatment decision being driven by it.

selected_rules.activation_condition: No clinical recommendations are present in the provided text
selected_rules.execute: Do not extract any treatment, staging, or diagnostic action from this text
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations Section 2: Recommendations and Key Evidence Section 3: Guideline Methods Overview Section 4: Systematic Review Section 5: Internal and External Review

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only administrative and copyright/contact information
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action should be derived from the document content
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about this document, please contact Alex Sun, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Text provides report citation and website/contact details only
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about the PEBC and the most current version of all reports, please visit the CCO website at http://www.cancercare.on.ca or contact the PEBC office at: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Text states copyright and reproduction restrictions only
selected_rules.execute: No clinical recommendation can be extracted from this statement
selected_rules.source_statement: Copyright This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in clinic for follow-up after a recent oncology visit. He asks whether he should start any new treatment based on the document he was given, but the handout contains only report citation information, website details, and contact information for the guideline office. There are no clinical findings, diagnoses, staging details, or treatment recommendations included in the text he brought. He has no new symptoms and is mainly seeking clarification about what the document means for his care.
recommended_actions: Do not apply any guideline-based clinical management rule from this document.
Do not derive any clinical action from the report citation, website, or contact details alone.
source_case.rationale: The provided text contains only report citation and website/contact details, with no clinical recommendations or management guidance. This triggers the no-recommendation rule for citation/contact-only content, so no treatment, staging, or diagnostic action should be extracted.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man returns to clinic after an oncology appointment and asks whether he should begin any new treatment based on the handout he was given. The document he brings includes a guideline report citation, a PEBC website link, and office contact information, but no explicit treatment recommendations. Unlike the prior visit, however, the oncology note in the chart separately documents a new diagnosis of stage III colon cancer with an ECOG performance status of 0, and the patient is scheduled for adjuvant therapy discussion next week. He has no new symptoms and is mainly trying to understand whether the handout itself changes his care.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a patient asking about a guideline handout that mainly contains citation and contact details, but an additional charted cancer diagnosis makes it look like the document may be clinically actionable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start guideline-based adjuvant treatment immediately based on the handout
Infer staging and management steps from the citation and website information
Use the document as if it were a treatment recommendation summary
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the citation/contact-only rule because the handout itself still contains no clinical recommendations. The newly documented cancer diagnosis belongs to the chart, not the document, so the safer interpretation is to ignore the handout for decision-making and rely on the separate oncology assessment for actual management.
challenging_examples.rationale: The invalidated condition is that the text provides only report citation and website/contact details. Although the patient has a real cancer diagnosis elsewhere in the chart, the document he brought still does not contain recommendations, so no action should be extracted from it.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is seen in follow-up after a recent oncology visit. He asks whether he should start any new treatment based on the document he was given. The handout includes only the guideline report citation, website details, and contact information for the guideline office, with no management recommendations in the text. Separately, he mentions that a pathology report from last month showed adenocarcinoma, but the report is still pending final molecular profiling and no stage has yet been assigned. He feels well and has no new symptoms, and he wants to know whether the paper in his folder tells him what to do next.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall setup still looks like a guideline question about a cancer patient holding a document from oncology, but the actual handout remains administrative only and the disease workup is incomplete.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Apply a cancer treatment algorithm from the handout
Recommend staging- or biomarker-driven therapy based on the document
Treat the citation page as if it contained management guidance
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the document contains no clinical recommendations at all. In addition, the patient’s cancer workup is incomplete, with no final stage or molecular results, so the safest interpretation is that no management action should be derived from the handout and the oncology team should wait for the completed diagnostic workup.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R001 is invalidated because the activation condition requires absence of clinical recommendations in the provided text. The handout still lacks recommendations, and the pending staging/molecular results further confirm that any treatment decision would be premature.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has erectile dysfunction alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use erectile dysfunction alone as a symptom prompting DRE and PSA testing for metastatic prostate cancer
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group did not think it necessary for a man with erectile dysfunction to undergo a DRE and PSA test and therefore excluded it as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has unexplained hematuria
selected_rules.execute: Do not treat unexplained hematuria as part of the prostate cancer care algorithm; arrange urologic assessment as appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also excluded unexplained hematuria as a symptom of metastatic prostate cancer... hematuria requires urologic assessment but is not part of a prostate cancer care algorithm

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer and is age 40 or older
selected_rules.execute: Perform digital rectal examination (DRE) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with symptoms of metastatic prostate cancer should have a DRE and PSA was endorsed

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS)
selected_rules.execute: Perform DRE
selected_rules.source_statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Exclude urinary infection before PSA testing
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to exclude urinary infection before PSA testing

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS and PSA testing is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: The working group also endorsed the recommendations to... postpone PSA testing for at least one month after treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has an abnormal incidental PSA result
selected_rules.execute: Perform DRE
selected_rules.source_statement: if an incidental PSA test was abnormal, then standard practise would be to perform a DRE

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has LUTS
selected_rules.execute: Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment
selected_rules.source_statement: a man with LUTS should have a DRE and a discussion about PSA testing

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no known urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. On review, his main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior PSA testing has been done.
recommended_actions: Perform a digital rectal examination (DRE).
Discuss PSA testing and consider PSA based on individual risk assessment.
source_case.rationale: The vignette includes lower urinary tract symptoms in a man aged over 40, which triggers the recommendation for DRE. LUTS also supports a discussion about PSA testing with consideration of individual risk. No features of metastatic prostate cancer, abnormal incidental PSA, erectile dysfunction alone, or unexplained hematuria were included, so those rules were not triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. His main concern is whether he might have prostate disease because of the persistent urinary symptoms. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. On review, he had a PSA test through workplace screening 2 weeks ago that was mildly elevated, but it was drawn while he was being treated for a symptomatic urinary tract infection with antibiotics started the same day. His exam is unremarkable aside from mild suprapubic fullness; no prior DRE has been performed.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a man over 40 with persistent LUTS and concern for prostate disease, but the PSA context is altered by a recent UTI and active antibiotic treatment, and the abnormal PSA was incidental rather than part of a clean initial evaluation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Perform DRE immediately because LUTS are present.
Proceed with PSA-based prostate cancer workup now.
Treat the mildly elevated PSA as a reliable cancer signal without repeating it.
Counsel that this is straightforward prostate disease and escalate to biopsy referral.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for immediate PSA interpretation. Although LUTS would support DRE and discussion of PSA in general, the PSA was obtained during an active urinary infection and should not be acted on as a clean screening result. The safer interpretation is to treat the infection first, defer repeat PSA for at least a month after treatment, and then reassess symptoms and exam findings.
challenging_examples.rationale: LUTS alone can prompt DRE and PSA discussion, but PSA testing should be deferred when urinary infection is present and repeated only after treatment has resolved the confounder. The abnormal PSA is incidental and potentially false-positive in this setting, so immediate cancer-pathway escalation would be premature.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 8 months of lower urinary tract symptoms, including hesitancy, weak stream, and nocturia twice nightly. He denies bone pain, weight loss, fever, dysuria, hematuria, or erectile dysfunction. He has no known urinary infection symptoms and has not had any recent treatment for urinary issues. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. On review, he says he had a PSA checked 3 months ago at a community screening event, and it was reported as borderline high. He brings no documentation of the value, and his current symptoms have not changed. Examination shows a normal abdomen and no suprapubic tenderness; he already had a DRE last week at an urgent care visit, which was documented as normal.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still sounds like a routine LUTS evaluation in a man over 40, but the key subtlety is that a DRE has already been done and the PSA was an undocumented borderline screening value rather than a current abnormal incidental result requiring the same next step.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Repeat DRE as if no prior exam existed.
Treat the old borderline PSA as an abnormal incidental result requiring immediate prostate cancer workup.
Proceed as though PSA testing is newly indicated and order it reflexively without discussion.
Assume the prior normal DRE does not matter and restart the pathway from scratch.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean trigger for the original recommendation because the exam has already been completed and the PSA information is remote, undocumented, and not a clearly abnormal incidental result in the current encounter. The safer interpretation is to review the prior DRE result, clarify the actual PSA value and context before acting, and decide on further testing based on risk discussion rather than automatically repeating the initial algorithm.
challenging_examples.rationale: The LUTS remain similar, but the guideline logic changes when a DRE has already been performed and the PSA is not a verified current abnormal incidental result. Without a documented abnormal PSA from this visit or a new trigger, immediate repetition of the initial diagnostic sequence is not automatically warranted.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment and eligible for treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and/or radical prostatectomy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk prostate cancer who require or choose active treatment, LDR alone, EBRT alone, or RP should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and androgen-deprivation therapy and eligible for brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost using low-dose rate or high-dose rate brachytherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with high-risk prostate cancer receiving EBRT and ADT, brachytherapy boost (LDR or HDR) should be offered to eligible patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving low-dose rate brachytherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use iodine-125 or palladium-103 as reasonable isotope options.
selected_rules.source_statement: 125 I and 103 Pd are each reasonable isotope options for patients receiving LDR brachytherapy;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with prostate cancer is being managed under this guideline
selected_rules.execute: Encourage participation in clinical trials testing novel or targeted approaches.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be encouraged to participate in clinical trials to test novel or targeted approaches to this disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being counseled about management options
selected_rules.execute: Counsel about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be counseled about all their management options (surgery, EBRT, active surveillance, as applicable) in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.

selected_rules.activation_condition: brachytherapy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform brachytherapy at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.
selected_rules.source_statement: Brachytherapy should be performed at a center following strict quality-assurance standards.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has intermediate-risk prostate cancer and is choosing EBRT with or without ADT
selected_rules.execute: Offer brachytherapy boost with LDR or HDR to eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer choosing EBRT with or without androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT), brachytherapy boost (LDR or high-dose rate should be offered to eligible patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma presents after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and, after discussing his options, says he prefers active treatment rather than surveillance because of significant anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and eligible for local therapy. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting and discusses the main treatment choices with him in a balanced way.
recommended_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone, external beam radiation therapy alone, and radical prostatectomy as treatment options for this eligible patient with low-risk prostate cancer.
Counsel the patient about all applicable management options in a balanced, objective manner, preferably from multiple disciplines.
source_case.rationale: This vignette fits newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer requiring or choosing active treatment in an eligible patient, triggering the recommendation to offer LDR brachytherapy alone, EBRT alone, and/or radical prostatectomy. It also involves counseling about management options, supporting a balanced multidisciplinary discussion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma returns after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and is otherwise healthy, but he has a prior transurethral resection of the prostate and was told by a local radiation oncologist that the altered anatomy may make him a poor candidate for brachytherapy. He remains anxious about living with untreated cancer and says he wants active treatment rather than surveillance. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting and discusses the main treatment choices with him in a balanced way.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like newly diagnosed low-risk prostate cancer in an otherwise eligible man choosing active treatment, but the prior TURP makes one of the key offered local options less clearly appropriate.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone as a standard option without addressing the post-TURP limitation.
Present surgery and external beam radiation therapy as equivalent options while assuming brachytherapy eligibility.
Proceed as though all three low-risk definitive treatment choices are guideline-concordant for this patient.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not cleanly guideline-concordant for the low-risk recommendation because the prior TURP can compromise brachytherapy eligibility. The safer interpretation is that he should still be counseled objectively about management options, but the brachytherapy pathway is not automatically appropriate and should be reconsidered by an experienced center.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation to offer LDR brachytherapy, EBRT, and/or radical prostatectomy applies to eligible low-risk patients. The added prior TURP creates a subtle eligibility issue that invalidates straightforward application of the brachytherapy portion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man with newly diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma presents after biopsy and staging show low-risk disease: PSA 5.2 ng/mL, Gleason 3+3=6, and cT1c exam. He has a life expectancy well beyond 10 years and, after discussing his options, says he prefers active treatment rather than surveillance because of significant anxiety about living with untreated cancer. He is otherwise healthy and eligible for local therapy. In clinic, he asks about surgery versus radiation and wants a clear explanation of the available approaches before deciding. The team reviews his case in a multidisciplinary setting, but the only local radiation program available in his region has no recent brachytherapy quality-assurance credentialing and has not performed prostate implants in over a year.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Everything about the cancer risk group and treatment preference matches the source vignette, but the practical access/quality setting makes one local treatment route unsuitable despite seeming otherwise applicable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer low-dose rate brachytherapy alone as if the center were appropriate for it.
List all definitive options without recognizing the center cannot safely deliver brachytherapy.
Assume the patient can simply be referred for brachytherapy without considering that the current site is not qualified.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for direct brachytherapy offering at the present center because the guideline requires strict quality-assurance standards. The safer interpretation is to discuss surgery and external beam radiation locally, and if brachytherapy is being considered, it should be done only through a qualified program.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the patient otherwise resembles the source low-risk case, the brachytherapy-specific quality standard is not met. That makes the straightforward recommendation to offer brachytherapy at the current center inappropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Doppler technology alone for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Doppler technology alone to evaluate the mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: Evaluation of an adnexal mass by Doppler technology alone is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering serum CA-125 as a stand-alone test
selected_rules.execute: Do not use CA-125 alone to distinguish benign from malignant adnexal masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: As a stand-alone modality, serum CA-125 is not recommended for distinguishing between benign and malignant adnexal masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult woman with a suspicious isolated ovarian mass and ultrasound is technically feasible
selected_rules.execute: Use transvaginal sonography as the first imaging modality.
selected_rules.source_statement: Transvaginal sonography should be the first modality of choice, where technically feasible, in patients with a suspicious, isolated ovarian mass.

selected_rules.activation_condition: need to differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses
selected_rules.execute: Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging for differentiation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sonography, particularly three-dimensional (3D) sonography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computerized tomography (CT) imaging are each recommended for differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound may be unreliable
selected_rules.execute: Use MRI to help clarify malignant potential.
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI is the most appropriate test to help clarify the malignant potential in patients where ultrasound may be unreliable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: extra-ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out
selected_rules.execute: Use CT imaging to assess for extra-ovarian disease.
selected_rules.source_statement: CT is most useful in cases where extra ovarian disease is suspected or needs to be ruled out.

selected_rules.activation_condition: using Doppler technology in adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Combine Doppler technology with morphological assessment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Doppler technology should be combined with a morphological assessment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering morphological scoring systems for adnexal mass evaluation
selected_rules.execute: Use a morphological scoring system to differentiate benign from malignant masses.
selected_rules.source_statement: Morphological scoring systems can be used to differentiate benign from malignant adnexal masses.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa, and a recent office ultrasound was limited by overlying bowel gas, so the mass is not well characterized. She has no known prior ovarian disease. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify whether this isolated ovarian mass is benign or malignant. Because the initial ultrasound is technically unreliable, additional imaging is needed to better define malignant potential. There is no clear evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread, but the adnexal lesion remains suspicious and incompletely assessed.
recommended_actions: Use MRI to help clarify the malignant potential because ultrasound may be unreliable.
Use sonography, particularly 3D sonography, MRI, or CT imaging to help differentiate malignant from benign ovarian masses.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a suspicious isolated ovarian/adnexal mass with technically unreliable ultrasound, which triggers MRI as the most appropriate clarifying test. It also involves differentiating malignant from benign ovarian masses, supporting use of sonography, MRI, or CT for characterization.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa. Transvaginal ultrasound was completed in the office and clearly shows a 4.8 cm complex left ovarian lesion with papillary projections and internal vascular flow; the images are technically adequate and the mass is well characterized. There is no ascites, and no evidence yet of extra-ovarian spread. The clinician is deciding the next imaging step to clarify malignant potential and is considering whether additional cross-sectional imaging is needed before referral. Because the initial ultrasound is already diagnostic-quality, the main question is characterization of an isolated suspicious adnexal mass rather than rescue of a limited study.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a suspicious isolated ovarian mass in a postmenopausal woman, but the ultrasound is now technically adequate rather than unreliable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order MRI reflexively to clarify malignant potential because any suspicious ovarian mass should get MRI
Treat the technically adequate ultrasound as insufficient and escalate to CT for characterization
Assume the next step is advanced imaging solely because the lesion is complex
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for MRI as a problem-solving test due to unreliable ultrasound, because the ultrasound here was technically feasible and already well characterized the lesion. The safer interpretation is that transvaginal sonography has provided the needed initial characterization, and further imaging should be based on whether extra-ovarian disease is suspected or whether surgical/oncologic planning requires it.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 applies when ultrasound may be unreliable. Here, the ultrasound was technically adequate, so the specific trigger for MRI is absent.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 3 weeks of intermittent left lower abdominal bloating and early satiety. Pelvic exam suggests a fullness in the left adnexa. Office ultrasound was limited by bowel gas and the adnexal lesion is not well characterized. However, a recent CT abdomen/pelvis performed for diverticulitis already shows a 5.2 cm predominantly cystic left ovarian mass without ascites, peritoneal nodules, or other extra-ovarian findings. The clinician is considering the next imaging step to clarify malignant potential and wonders whether MRI is still necessary to better define the lesion. The overall concern remains an isolated adnexal mass in a postmenopausal patient, but cross-sectional imaging has already been obtained and no spread is evident.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still sounds like a suspicious ovarian mass with an unreliable ultrasound, but CT has already provided cross-sectional assessment and the lesion is not solely dependent on MRI for triage.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order MRI automatically because the ultrasound was limited
Repeat imaging only to better characterize the ovarian lesion despite already having CT
Assume unreliability of ultrasound alone makes MRI mandatory in every case
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the clean guideline scenario for MRI prompted by unreliable ultrasound alone, because the patient already has a CT demonstrating no extra-ovarian disease and the remaining question is not purely unresolved initial characterization. A safer interpretation is that additional imaging should be individualized rather than assuming MRI is required just because the ultrasound was limited.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 supports MRI when ultrasound may be unreliable. In this vignette, CT has already been obtained and has clarified the absence of spread, weakening the specific indication for MRI as the next mandatory step.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual uses indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of indoor tanning devices should be avoided to reduce risk of melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual is considering or using indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at younger age
selected_rules.source_statement: All individuals should avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially those at a younger age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: individual is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices
selected_rules.execute: recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and mentions that she has been using a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says several friends started tanning in high school, and she has been considering increasing her sessions because she likes the color it gives her skin. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she is unaware of any specific risks associated with indoor tanning. She asks whether occasional tanning bed use is safe if she does not burn.
recommended_actions: Avoid use of indoor tanning devices to reduce melanoma risk.
Avoid use of indoor tanning devices, especially at a younger age.
Recognize that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a young individual who is currently using and considering increased use of indoor tanning devices, which triggers the rules advising avoidance. Because she is exposed to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices, the no-safe-lower-limit statement also applies. The recommendations are limited to avoidance and counseling about risk, as specified by the triggered rules.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman comes for a routine visit and says she used a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She adds that she has now stopped because she developed several painful blistering sunburns after a weekend at the beach and decided indoor tanning “wasn't worth it.” She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer. She asks whether her prior tanning bed use was safe since she only did it for a short time and is no longer planning to continue.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The age, tanning-bed exposure, and counseling context are almost unchanged, but the key near-miss is that she has already discontinued indoor tanning and is asking retrospectively rather than currently using or considering it.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise her to avoid indoor tanning devices as an immediate management step for current use
Counsel that indoor tanning is unsafe and there is no safe lower limit of exposure
Treat this as an active tanning-device use counseling visit focused on stopping ongoing exposure
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant with the indoor-tanning avoidance recommendation because she is no longer using or considering indoor tanning devices. The safer interpretation is routine preventive counseling and skin-risk education, not an active avoidance intervention for current exposure.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R002 require current use or consideration of indoor tanning devices; here the exposure is historical only. R003 applies to actual exposure from indoor tanning devices, but the vignette’s current issue is prior exposure that has ended, so the recommendation is not directly triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and mentions that she has been using a tanning bed at a salon twice weekly for the past 3 months to “get a base tan” before spring break. She says she likes the color it gives her skin and has been thinking about continuing through the summer, but her salon recently changed to a spray-tan booth because of new regulations. She has no current skin complaints and no personal history of skin cancer, but she is unaware of any specific risks associated with indoor tanning. She asks whether occasional tanning bed use is safe if she does not burn.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The visit still appears to be about indoor tanning in a young woman, but the actual device at her current salon is a spray-tan booth rather than a UV-emitting tanning bed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise her to avoid indoor tanning devices and stop the salon visits
Counsel that there is no safe lower limit of exposure to artificial UVR from tanning devices
Document ongoing indoor tanning exposure as the key risk factor
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette is not guideline-concordant because the current salon device is a spray-tan booth, not an indoor tanning device that emits artificial UVR. The safer interpretation is that the melanoma-risk counseling about indoor tanning does not apply to the present exposure, although general skin-health counseling may still be appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rules are triggered by use, consideration, or exposure to artificial UVR from indoor tanning devices. Spray tanning does not create indoor UVR exposure, so the original recommendation is not applicable despite the superficial similarity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has cytogenetically normal AML
selected_rules.execute: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test
selected_rules.source_statement: DNMT3A mutation testing should be included as a biomarker test in cytogenetically normal AML patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows a normal karyotype, and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
recommended_actions: Include DNMT3A mutation testing as a biomarker test in this patient with cytogenetically normal AML.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes newly diagnosed AML with cytogenetically normal disease, which directly activates the rule recommending DNMT3A mutation testing as part of biomarker evaluation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis shows t(8;21), and there is no prior history of myelodysplastic syndrome or chemotherapy exposure. He is being evaluated at initial diagnosis for prognostic and treatment-planning biomarkers.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like newly diagnosed AML at the time of biomarker workup, but the karyotype is no longer normal.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order DNMT3A mutation testing reflexively because this is newly diagnosed AML.
Document cytogenetically normal AML and proceed with the standard biomarker panel for normal-karyotype disease.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not cytogenetically normal AML because the marrow cytogenetics show t(8;21). The DNMT3A-testing recommendation tied to normal-karyotype AML is therefore not guideline-concordant here; biomarker assessment should instead follow the risk profile for core-binding-factor AML.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule activates only for cytogenetically normal AML. A specific recurrent translocation invalidates that condition even though the rest of the diagnostic workflow is unchanged.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent low-grade fevers. CBC shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL, platelets 42,000/µL, and WBC 18,400/µL with circulating blasts. Bone marrow biopsy confirms acute myeloid leukemia. Cytogenetic analysis is pending, but the pathology report notes a complex karyotype with monosomy 7 on preliminary review.
