challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves metastatic breast cancer, palliative chemotherapy, low mood, and a request for an antidepressant, but the mood symptoms are now more than mild/subthreshold and her cancer-related physical symptoms are already controlled.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start an antidepressant immediately for presumed mild depression
Focus the visit on symptom control adjustments for pain, constipation, or nausea despite those being stable
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original recommendation because the depressive symptoms are no longer clearly subthreshold or mild; they sound more persistent and clinically significant. In addition, the physical symptom burden that would prompt optimization is already well controlled. The safer interpretation is to assess for a clearer depressive disorder and consider a more formal mental health evaluation rather than applying the ‘do not routinely use antidepressants for mild symptoms’ recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule applies to subthreshold depressive symptoms or mild depression. Here, the symptom description is more consistent with a higher-severity depressive presentation. Also, the supportive-care trigger is weakened because pain, constipation, and nausea are controlled.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on palliative chemotherapy comes to clinic for worsening fatigue and low mood over the past 3 weeks. She reports feeling “down and overwhelmed,” with reduced enjoyment, poor sleep, and less appetite, but denies suicidal thoughts. Her symptoms are mild and she remains able to manage most daily activities, though she has been missing some social visits. She wonders if she should start an antidepressant right away because she feels emotionally drained by treatment. On review, her pain from bone metastases is not well controlled, and she also notes constipation and nausea. Recent labs show mild anemia and a normal TSH; B12 and folate were checked last month and were normal, but her ferritin was low and she recently started iron supplementation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely mirrors the source case, including the same cancer setting, mild mood complaints, and uncontrolled physical symptoms, but one subtle lab/treatment change suggests a medical contributor has already been found and addressed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Avoid antidepressants because the symptoms are mild and simply wait after iron repletion
Assume the depression workup is complete and focus only on cancer symptom control
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is still not suitable for the exact original recommendation because the depressive symptoms are described as mild/subthreshold, but the medical-contributor workup is not fully unrevealing: iron deficiency has been identified and treatment has begun. The safer interpretation is to continue evaluating the mood symptoms in context of the anemia/iron deficiency and reassess after medical optimization, rather than reflexively treating as primary depression or assuming the original rule cleanly applies.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation includes investigating reversible medical contributors such as iron deficiency. Here, that contributor has already been found, so the vignette no longer fits the same unmet-workup context, even though the mood severity remains mild.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering NaF PET or choline PET
selected_rules.execute: Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of NaF and choline PET should be considered experimental

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering dietary advice for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: No diet plan can be recommended because no diet plan or food supplement has been associated with improved cancer outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A man is not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits and develops symptoms
selected_rules.execute: Perform a PSA test and consider symptom-specific diagnostic imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: In men who are not being evaluated through regularly scheduled clinical visits, a PSA test should be performed if the following symptoms develop. Additionally, diagnostic imaging specific to the patient's symptom(s) may be indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Men after primary therapy with long-lasting treatment effects
selected_rules.execute: Ensure follow-up providers are aware of quality-of-life domains affected and available management options
selected_rules.source_statement: Follow-up healthcare providers should be aware of the domains of quality of life potentially affected by treatment for prostate cancer and the management options available to combat them.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Following prostate cancer treatment when assessing quality of life
selected_rules.execute: Measure individual patient-reported outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: To ensure optimal quality of life in these men, individual patient-reported outcomes should be measured.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Prostate cancer survivor after completing curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide ongoing surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: For prostate cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any healthcare practitioner provides PSA surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Manage PSA according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway
selected_rules.source_statement: All healthcare practitioners that provide PSA surveillance should manage PSA as per the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Planning survivorship care models for prostate cancer survivors
selected_rules.execute: Include family physicians in survivorship care models
selected_rules.source_statement: Expert opinion supports family physicians being involved in all survivorship care models.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and has been followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits.
recommended_actions: Provide ongoing surveillance after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Manage PSA surveillance according to the current CCO Prostate Cancer Pathway.
Measure individual patient-reported outcomes to assess quality of life.
Ensure follow-up providers are aware of the quality-of-life domains affected by treatment and the available management options.
Include the family physician in the survivorship care model.
Treat NaF PET and choline PET as experimental if considering them for recurrence/restaging.
Do not recommend any specific diet plan or food supplement for improved cancer outcomes.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a prostate cancer survivor after curative-intent therapy, triggering the need for ongoing surveillance, PSA management per the CCO pathway, patient-reported outcome measurement, survivorship follow-up awareness of quality-of-life domains, and inclusion of the family physician. The patient also asks about recurrence imaging with PET and dietary supplements, which triggers the rules stating NaF and choline PET are experimental and that no specific diet or supplement should be recommended for improved cancer outcomes.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man returns to a survivorship clinic 14 months after completing curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer. His PSA has remained undetectable on routine surveillance, and he reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that are affecting sleep and daily activities. He is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and asks what should prompt further review. He also asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and whether he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. He has, however, recently transferred his routine follow-up entirely to a private oncology clinic and no family physician is involved in his survivorship care.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a standard post-curative prostate cancer survivorship visit with symptom review, PSA surveillance, and questions about PET imaging and diet, but the family physician is no longer part of the survivorship model.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Include the family physician in the survivorship care plan as if this were still guideline-concordant
Document routine survivorship care without addressing the absence of primary care involvement
Proceed as though all recommended survivorship partners are already engaged
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the rule supporting family-physician involvement in survivorship care models because the current care structure excludes the family physician. The safer interpretation is that survivorship follow-up should be restructured to involve primary care, rather than assuming the guideline condition is met.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss preserves the prostate cancer survivorship context and most follow-up elements, but the key discrepancy is that the family physician is not involved. That makes the recommendation to include primary care in survivorship care not currently satisfied.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man completed curative-intent external beam radiotherapy for localized prostate cancer 14 months ago and is being followed in a survivorship clinic. He reports worsening fatigue, mild urinary urgency, and intermittent hot flashes that have affected sleep and daily activities. His PSA surveillance has been done through the clinic, but he is unsure which symptoms are expected long-term effects and which should prompt further review. He asks whether a special PET scan would help if his PSA rises again and also asks if he should start a specific “prostate cancer diet” or supplement to reduce recurrence risk. He has no new bone pain, weight loss, or neurologic deficits. On review, his recent PSA is 0.3 ng/mL, then 0.4 ng/mL six weeks later, and he has no documented biochemical recurrence or rising PSA trend beyond this borderline fluctuation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This looks almost identical to a routine post-radiotherapy survivorship visit, including the same PET scan question and PSA follow-up, but there is no true recurrence or restaging indication yet.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a NaF PET or choline PET to look for recurrent disease
Reassure the patient that advanced PET imaging is appropriate for any PSA increase
Treat the imaging request as guideline-concordant recurrence/restaging workup
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for NaF or choline PET because the patient does not have established recurrence or a clear restaging indication; the PSA change is minimal and does not by itself justify those experimental scans. The safer interpretation is continued surveillance and PSA trend monitoring per the prostate cancer pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette intentionally keeps the same post-treatment setting and imaging question, but the PSA pattern is only borderline and does not establish recurrence. That removes the clinical context needed to apply the rule about NaF and choline PET in recurrence/restaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine monitoring or surveillance of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is not recommended for the routine monitoring and surveillance of lymphoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important and anatomical imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case and functional imaging is important
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal, and/or in potentially curable cases, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: functional imaging is important, anatomical imaging is equivocal, and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT for staging.
selected_rules.source_statement: When functional imaging is considered to be important in situations where anatomical imaging is equivocal and treatment choices may be affected in limited stage indolent lymphomas, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: early stage (I or II) Hodgkin lymphoma after 2 or 3 cycles of chemotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered as definitive single-modality therapy
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to assess early response and inform whether to complete therapy or give more therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the assessment of early response in early stage (I or II) HL following two or three cycles of chemotherapy when chemotherapy is being considered as the definitive single-modality therapy, to inform completion of therapy or if more therapy is warranted.

selected_rules.activation_condition: potentially curable case with suspected recurrence and conventional imaging is equivocal
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to investigate recurrence of Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
selected_rules.source_statement: In potentially curable cases, when functional imaging is considered to be important and conventional imaging is equivocal, a FDG PET/CT scan is recommended to investigate recurrence of HL or NHL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: residual mass after chemotherapy in Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma and further potentially curative therapy is being considered and biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed
selected_rules.execute: Perform FDG PET/CT to evaluate the residual mass.
selected_rules.source_statement: An FDG PET/CT scan is recommended for the evaluation of residual mass(es) following chemotherapy in a patient with HL or NHL when further, potentially curative, therapy (such as radiation or stem cell transplantation) is being considered and when biopsy cannot be safely or readily performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: FDG PET is abnormal during initial diagnosis of lymphoma
selected_rules.execute: Use clinical follow-up or biopsy to confirm findings because false positives can occur.
selected_rules.source_statement: FDG PET may disclose higher rates of systemic disease; however, due to false-positive results, FDG PET scans should be subject to clinical follow-up or biopsy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP eight months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She now returns for a routine follow-up visit without B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, or abnormal laboratory findings. Her oncologist notes that she feels well and asks whether she should have an FDG PET/CT scan to “make sure nothing is coming back.” There is no new clinical concern, and prior post-treatment imaging was reassuring. The main question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test.
recommended_actions: Do not use FDG PET/CT for routine surveillance of lymphoma.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes routine monitoring/surveillance after lymphoma treatment without new symptoms or equivocal findings, which matches the rule against routine FDG PET/CT surveillance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with previously treated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completed six cycles of R-CHOP nine months ago and achieved a complete metabolic response. She returns for a routine follow-up visit and remains free of B symptoms, palpable adenopathy, and laboratory abnormalities. Her oncologist mentions that a prior CT done for unrelated abdominal pain showed a tiny 8 mm prevascular mediastinal node that has been unchanged on two scans and asks whether an FDG PET/CT should be ordered now to “make sure it isn’t lymphoma.” There is no new exam finding, no rising LDH, and no other clinical concern. The main question is whether to continue scheduled surveillance imaging or use PET/CT as a routine monitoring test.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like post-treatment lymphoma follow-up, but the only imaging issue is a stable incidental node without symptoms or progression, making this routine surveillance rather than a true workup for suspected recurrence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order an FDG PET/CT now to evaluate the stable node
Continue serial PET/CT scans as routine surveillance after remission
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for routine surveillance PET/CT. The patient is asymptomatic with no clinical evidence of relapse, and the incidental mediastinal node is stable on CT, so the safer interpretation is continued clinical follow-up rather than routine FDG PET/CT monitoring.
challenging_examples.rationale: Routine surveillance imaging is not recommended in lymphoma remission without new symptoms or concerning findings; a stable incidental node alone does not convert this into a guideline-supported PET/CT indication.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with follicular lymphoma completed bendamustine-rituximab seven months ago and had a complete response on end-of-treatment imaging. He is seen for routine follow-up and feels well, with no fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or palpable adenopathy. His blood counts and LDH are normal. He asks whether he should undergo an FDG PET/CT at this visit because his original disease was “the kind that can come back quietly.” The oncologist notes that there is no new symptom, no equivocal exam finding, and no prior suspicious post-treatment scan. The question is whether to obtain PET/CT for surveillance or simply continue standard follow-up.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a classic remission follow-up visit for lymphoma, but the subtype is indolent follicular lymphoma with no new concern, which still does not justify routine PET/CT surveillance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Schedule FDG PET/CT for reassurance during routine follow-up
Add PET/CT to ongoing surveillance imaging because the lymphoma can recur silently
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant routine surveillance PET/CT. Even though the lymphoma subtype differs from the first case, the key issue is the absence of symptoms or suspicious findings; the safer interpretation is routine clinical follow-up without PET/CT unless new clinical concern develops.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation against routine FDG PET/CT surveillance applies here because the visit is purely follow-up and there is no evidence of relapse or an equivocal lesion requiring functional imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When providing palliative care across the disease course
selected_rules.execute: Use a team-based approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms and supports patients and families.
selected_rules.source_statement: Palliative care focuses on care that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms; it can be delivered throughout the course of the disease; it involves a team of healthcare providers and supports patients as well as their families.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting palliative care guidelines for review
selected_rules.execute: Include only papers that provide recommendations on palliative care and exclude papers that only describe guideline or consensus methods without recommendations.
selected_rules.source_statement: Papers were included if they provided any recommendations on palliative care. Papers were excluded if they only provided guideline or consensus methods and no recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When screening palliative care guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Restrict inclusion to guidelines published from 2011 to 2016.
selected_rules.source_statement: Due to the large number of guidelines, the Working Group decided to restrict the review to include only guidelines published in the past five years (2011 to 2016).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When selecting higher-quality guidelines for this review
selected_rules.execute: Include only guidelines with an AGREE Rigour of Development subsection score of 28 or higher.
selected_rules.source_statement: To identify the best-quality guidelines, the AGREE Instrument Rigour of Development scale was used and only guidelines that scored 28 or above were included in this review.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using the broad palliative care guideline for adults
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline covering the palliative care path from first diagnosis of serious illness to death.
selected_rules.source_statement: The "Palliative Care for Adults" guideline by McCusker et al. ... covers the palliative care path from the patient's first diagnosis of a serious illness to death.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When caring for patients in the last days and hours of life
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, decision support, care and management, education, and organization/policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This is a Canadian guideline about patient care in the last days and hours of life, and the recommendations in this guideline were specific to nurses. The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment for end of life, Decision support at end of life, Care and management at end of life, Education, and Organization and policy recommendations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When assessing or preventing pressure ulcers in palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario guideline for nurse-specific recommendations on assessment, planning, interventions, discharge or transfer/care arrangements, and education/organization-policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: The topic of this guideline was the assessment and prevention of pressure ulcers. This guideline does not focus solely on palliative care, but the treatment and assessment of pressure ulcers in palliative care is a part of the guideline. ... The guideline makes recommendations in the following areas: Assessment, Planning, Interventions, Discharge or transfer or the care arrangements, and Education and organization/policy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When managing chronic cough in patients receiving palliative care
selected_rules.execute: Use simple linctus, then a therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, then an opioid or opioid derivative such as dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine.
selected_rules.source_statement: The recommendations include use of simple linctus, therapeutic trial of sodium cromoglycate, and then prescription of an opioid or opioid derivative (dextromethorphan, morphine, or codeine).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities.
recommended_actions: Use a team-based palliative care approach that addresses both physical and non-physical symptoms.
Provide support for both the patient and his family, including caregiver support and help with coping.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline across the disease course, from first diagnosis of serious illness through death.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because palliative care is being provided across the disease course and should address physical symptoms, emotional needs, and family support. It also triggers R005 because the patient has a serious illness and is being managed along the palliative care path from diagnosis onward. No other rules are directly relevant because he is not in the last days of life, and no pressure ulcer, chronic cough-specific, or guideline-selection scenario is described.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He asks for help managing symptoms at home, but the oncology note states he is due for brain metastasis treatment next week and the plan is to focus on disease-directed therapy only; the team is not yet discussing palliative care beyond basic symptom checks. His spouse remains overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities, and he has been told that supportive services will be revisited after the next scan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer type, advanced stage, symptom burden, and caregiver distress closely mirror the source case, but the workflow has shifted to short-term disease-directed management rather than a palliative care approach across the illness course.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start a team-based palliative care plan addressing physical symptoms, emotional distress, and caregiver support now.
Apply the broad adult palliative care guideline as if this were an established palliative-care encounter from diagnosis to death.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the broad palliative-care recommendations because the case frames the visit as deferred supportive care while pursuing disease-directed treatment only, rather than an active palliative care approach across the disease course. A safer interpretation is that symptom needs are present, but the vignette does not clearly establish the palliative-care workflow required by the rule.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires an actual palliative care approach across the disease course, including team-based symptom and family support. R005 requires using the broad palliative care guideline when the patient is on that palliative path from diagnosis to death. Here, the note explicitly defers palliative care and limits the current plan to cancer-directed therapy, making the recommendation not directly applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer was diagnosed 9 months ago and is now on second-line treatment after progression. He reports increasing fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, poor appetite, and intermittent anxiety about his family’s future. His wife says he has become more withdrawn and is struggling to talk about goals of care. He is not in the final days of life, but his symptoms are worsening and he asks for help managing them at home while continuing cancer-directed therapy. The oncology team notes that his physical symptoms are only part of the problem; he also needs emotional support and help for his wife, who is overwhelmed by caregiving responsibilities. However, the patient is enrolled in an outpatient palliative care study that excludes family support interventions and limits visits to medication review only.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette is unchanged and still sounds like a classic palliative-care referral, but the care setting has been narrowed to a medication-only study protocol that excludes the broader team-based and family-support elements of the rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend a comprehensive team-based palliative care plan including caregiver support and coping resources.
Treat this as a standard broad palliative-care pathway case from diagnosis through death.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not concordant with the broad palliative-care recommendation because the current service arrangement is explicitly restricted to medication review and excludes the team-based, non-physical, and family-support components described in the rule. The safer interpretation is that the patient has palliative needs, but the described care model does not meet the guideline’s scope.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 calls for team-based palliative care addressing both physical and non-physical symptoms and supporting patients and families. R005 applies to the broad palliative-care guideline across the disease course. A medication-only study protocol with no family-support interventions is too narrow to satisfy those conditions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IIIB cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVA cervical cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not apply the bevacizumab recommendation from GOG-0240 eligibility
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, GOG-0240 did not include patients with stage IIIB cancer (local extension to pelvic sidewall) or IVA cancer (invasion into bladder or rectum).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and randomized clinical trials are available
selected_rules.execute: offer participation in randomized clinical trials evaluating systemic therapy regimens
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that all patients with recurrent, metastatic or persistent cervical cancer be offered the opportunity to participate in randomized clinical trials, if available, that evaluate the efficacy of and adverse effects of systemic therapy regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: use cisplatin with paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin with paclitaxel is recommended for this patient population

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and a cisplatin combination is being used
selected_rules.execute: substitute carboplatin for cisplatin when appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: The substitution of carboplatin for cisplatin in these combinations is also recommended for this target population because carboplatin is associated with fewer adverse effects and greater ease of administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage IVB, recurrent, or persistent cervical cancer not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy, performance status 0 to 1, adequate renal hepatic and bone marrow function, and no prior chemotherapy for recurrence, nonhealing wounds, active bleeding, or inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism
selected_rules.execute: use bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel
selected_rules.source_statement: Bevacizumab in combination with cisplatin-paclitaxel is recommended for a specific subset of the target population, which includes only patients that match the characteristics of the GOG-0240 study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and carboplatin-paclitaxel is feasible to administer
selected_rules.execute: use carboplatin-paclitaxel as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on these results, and on the feasibility of administration, carboplatin-paclitaxel is recommended as a treatment option for recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has recurrent, metastatic, or persistent cervical cancer and systemic therapy is indicated
selected_rules.execute: consider cisplatin-vinorelbine
selected_rules.source_statement: cisplatin in other combinations, including cisplatin-vinorelbine, cisplatin-gemcitabine, and cisplatin-topotecan may also be considered.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain, hydronephrosis, and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
recommended_actions: Offer participation in a randomized clinical trial evaluating systemic therapy regimens, if the patient is interested and eligible.
Use cisplatin with paclitaxel as systemic therapy.
Carboplatin-paclitaxel is also a feasible treatment option if administration considerations favor carboplatin.
source_case.rationale: The patient has recurrent cervical cancer with systemic therapy indicated, so she meets criteria for cisplatin-paclitaxel and for consideration of carboplatin-paclitaxel as an alternative option. Because randomized clinical trials are available, she should also be offered trial participation. Bevacizumab is not triggered because the vignette does not establish the specific GOG-0240-eligible subset, and no stage IIIB/IVA exclusion is needed here.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain, hydronephrosis, and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center, and she is interested in discussing enrollment. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. However, her tumor board notes the PET/CT also shows a new rectal wall invasion consistent with stage IVA disease. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical to a GOG-0240-eligible recurrent cervical cancer case, but the new rectal invasion changes the stage to IVA and breaks the bevacizumab eligibility subset.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add bevacizumab to cisplatin-paclitaxel because she otherwise looks like the trial population.
Treat her as fully eligible for the bevacizumab-containing regimen since performance status and labs are acceptable.
Proceed with bevacizumab without re-checking the staging exclusion.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for bevacizumab with cisplatin-paclitaxel because stage IVA disease was excluded from the relevant evidence base. A safer interpretation is recurrent cervical cancer requiring systemic therapy, with trial participation and non-bevacizumab systemic options still appropriate.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation for bevacizumab applies only to the specific GOG-0240-like subset. Rectal invasion makes the patient stage IVA, so the bevacizumab recommendation should not be applied.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman with biopsy-proven cervical squamous cell carcinoma previously treated with definitive chemoradiation now has progressive pelvic pain and enlarging para-aortic nodes on imaging. Her disease is deemed recurrent and not amenable to curative surgery or radiotherapy. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate marrow counts. She has not received chemotherapy for recurrence. A randomized trial is available at her center, but after reviewing the protocol she declines because she wants standard treatment immediately. She has no active bleeding, no nonhealing wounds, and no history of inadequately anticoagulated thromboembolism. Her oncology team is discussing first-line systemic therapy options for recurrent disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a standard recurrent cervical cancer visit with trial availability, but the patient explicitly refuses enrollment.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer or document trial participation as if it were recommended despite her refusal.
Pursue trial consent again as the primary next step.
Record that she should be entered into the randomized study because one is available locally.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the trial-offer recommendation as an actionable plan, because the patient has declined participation. The safer interpretation is to proceed with standard systemic therapy discussions rather than attempt to assign trial enrollment.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although trials are available, the recommendation is to offer participation to eligible patients; once refused, enrollment is no longer appropriate. Standard recurrent-disease treatment options remain the next step.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease is being diagnosed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for diagnosis
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma stage I, IIa, or IIb
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is not recommended for the diagnosis of sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging of I, IIa, or IIb melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has suspected brain metastases from melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to diagnose brain metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET or PET/CT is not recommended for the diagnosis of brain metastases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: Do not routinely use PET to detect primary uveal malignant melanoma
selected_rules.source_statement: The routine use of PET is not recommended for the detection of primary uveal malignant melanoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has melanoma and is high-risk with potentially resectable disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PET for staging
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for staging of high-risk patients with potentially resectable disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, an isolated metastasis is identified
selected_rules.execute: Use PET
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At recurrence, metastectomy is being contemplated
selected_rules.execute: Use PET before metastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: PET is recommended for isolated metastases at time of recurrence or when contemplating metastectomy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Assessing treatment response in malignant melanoma
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation for or against PET
selected_rules.source_statement: A recommendation cannot be made for or against the use of PET for the assessment of treatment response in malignant melanoma due to insufficient evidence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and his primary tumor was otherwise consistent with stage II disease. His oncology team is discussing whether PET imaging should be ordered to further characterize the nodal finding before deciding on additional management.
recommended_actions: Do not use PET for diagnosis of the sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease.
Do not use PET for staging of this melanoma presentation.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule for melanoma with sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease being diagnosed. The guideline states PET is not recommended for diagnosing sentinel lymph node micrometastatic disease or for staging stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma. No other rule is clearly triggered because the case does not describe high-risk potentially resectable disease, recurrence, isolated metastasis, or treatment-response assessment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. The sentinel node shows a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit, and the oncology note describes the primary as a thin lesion with otherwise stage IIa features. He has no neurologic symptoms, no known distant disease, and no palpable adenopathy. The team is considering whether PET imaging should be ordered now to clarify the extent of disease before deciding on adjuvant management.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical melanoma post-operative staging scenario, but the primary tumor is now explicitly stage IIa rather than simply stage II disease, making the PET question look reasonable while still falling into a non-recommended category.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET to stage the melanoma before adjuvant treatment decisions.
Use PET to further characterize the sentinel node micrometastatic finding.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still not guideline-concordant for PET. The case is stage IIa melanoma with sentinel node micrometastatic disease being diagnosed, and PET is not recommended for diagnosing sentinel lymph node micrometastases or for staging stage I, IIa, or IIb melanoma. A safer interpretation is to proceed with standard melanoma management and reserve imaging for symptoms or other higher-risk indications.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle stage change preserves the deceptive staging workflow but keeps the patient within a group where PET is not recommended. The micrometastatic sentinel node finding does not override the staging restriction.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with cutaneous melanoma of the left upper back underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology now shows a single sentinel node with a 0.4 mm micrometastatic deposit. He has no neurologic symptoms and no known distant disease, but his oncology team is also reviewing a new 7 mm enhancing lesion in the left frontal lobe seen on MRI after he reported brief word-finding difficulty. The question is whether PET should be ordered to better define the brain lesion before additional management is chosen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The melanoma context and PET discussion are preserved, but a subtle new neurologic symptom and MRI-confirmed intracranial lesion shift the scenario from sentinel-node staging to suspected brain metastasis.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order PET to characterize the suspected brain metastasis.
Use PET/CT as the next diagnostic step for the intracranial lesion.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a situation for routine PET diagnosis of brain metastases. The lesion is already seen on MRI, and PET is not recommended to diagnose melanoma brain metastases. The safer interpretation is to manage this as suspected intracranial metastatic disease with MRI-based assessment and specialist-directed neuro-oncologic planning rather than using PET to clarify the brain finding.
challenging_examples.rationale: The presence of a brain lesion and focal neurologic symptom makes the case look like advanced melanoma staging, but the relevant rule specifically says PET is not recommended for diagnosing brain metastases. The MRI finding also means PET would not be the preferred test to characterize the intracranial disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surgery plus radiation therapy as the initial local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone and surgery is planned
selected_rules.execute: Take both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: Both pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans should be taken into consideration for surgical planning.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider surgery alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as a local treatment option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Either surgery alone or RT alone is a reasonable treatment option; the combination of surgery plus RT is not recommended as an initial treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Decide local treatment with a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, considering age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
selected_rules.source_statement: The local treatment for an individual patient should be decided by a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient after consideration of the following: 1) patient characteristics (e.g., age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and existing comorbidities), 2) the potential benefit compared with the potential complications from surgery and/or toxicities associated with RT, and 3) patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: complete tumour resection is impossible
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy alone as the optimal local treatment.
selected_rules.source_statement: If complete tumour resection is impossible, RT alone may be the optimal choice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: postoperative residual tumour or positive margins
selected_rules.execute: Consider postoperative radiation therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: RT may be a treatment option postoperatively in patients who have residual tumours or positive margins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: localized Ewing's sarcoma of bone, surgery is planned, and there is good chemotherapy response in certain anatomic locations
selected_rules.execute: Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: In certain anatomic locations with good chemotherapy response, the post-chemotherapy MRI may be the appropriate imaging modality to plan surgical resection margins.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 14-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a marked radiographic and symptomatic response. Repeat MRI shows substantial shrinkage of the intramedullary and soft-tissue components, while the pre-chemotherapy MRI demonstrated more extensive marrow involvement. The orthopedic oncologist is planning limb-sparing surgery, but the lesion’s location near the metaphysis raises concern about achieving clear margins without excessive functional loss. The case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with the patient and family, who prefer an approach that preserves function if oncologically safe. The team notes that complete resection appears feasible, though the final extent of surgery will depend on careful comparison of the pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI studies.
recommended_actions: Take both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRI scans into consideration for surgical planning.
Use the post-chemotherapy MRI to help plan surgical resection margins when appropriate.

Decide local treatment at a multidisciplinary tumour board together with the patient, weighing age, tumour location, tumour size, response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, comorbidities, expected benefit versus surgical/RT harms, and patient preference.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches localized Ewing’s sarcoma of bone with surgery planned after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, triggering preoperative staging and planning rules. The good chemotherapy response and an anatomic location where margin planning is important support using the post-chemotherapy MRI for resection margins. The multidisciplinary decision-making rule is also triggered because local treatment should be individualized with the patient and team.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old boy with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the proximal tibia completed neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has had a clear symptomatic improvement. Restaging MRI shows shrinkage of the soft-tissue mass, but the orthopedic oncologist believes limb-sparing surgery would leave a close anterior margin because the tumor abuts the neurovascular bundle. At tumor board, the team discusses local control options with the family, who strongly prefer to avoid a major amputation if possible. Because the surgeon is worried about microscopic residual disease after resection, the initial plan being discussed is combined limb-sparing surgery followed by radiation therapy. The pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRIs are both available for review, and the post-treatment scan is being used to estimate how much tissue remains to be removed.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Localized Ewing’s sarcoma after neoadjuvant chemotherapy with surgery being planned, close attention to MRI-based margin planning, and multidisciplinary discussion all mirror the source case, but the proposed local treatment shifts to surgery plus radiation rather than a single local modality.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with limb-sparing surgery and add postoperative radiation as the initial local treatment plan.
Assume combined surgery plus RT is preferred because the margin will likely be close.
Treat the scenario as guideline-concordant because both MRI scans are available and the family wants function preservation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the initial local treatment choice. The presence of localized Ewing’s sarcoma after neoadjuvant chemotherapy does not justify routine upfront surgery plus RT; surgery alone or RT alone are the reasonable initial local options. The safer interpretation is to individualize local control at tumor board and reserve postoperative RT only if final pathology shows residual tumor or positive margins.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle mismatch is the addition of planned combined surgery and radiation as the initial local strategy. That combination is the part that conflicts with the recommendation, even though the rest of the case resembles a standard post-chemotherapy surgical planning discussion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 13-year-old girl with localized Ewing’s sarcoma of the distal femur finished neoadjuvant chemotherapy and has shown a marked radiographic response. Her orthopedic surgeon is considering limb-sparing resection, and both the pre-chemotherapy and post-chemotherapy MRIs are available because the team wants to compare the original marrow extent with the current residual lesion. At multidisciplinary conference, the family requests the least morbid operation that still offers cure, and the team agrees the final operation should be individualized based on tumor size, location, and chemotherapy response. One detail complicates planning: the post-treatment MRI shows that the soft-tissue component has nearly resolved, but the tumor still extends farther proximally within the medullary canal than expected on the latest scan alone. Because of this discrepancy, the surgeon is relying primarily on the initial MRI to define the full bony extent before making a final resection plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely matches a post-neoadjuvant, localized Ewing’s sarcoma surgical-planning scenario with both MRI studies and tumor-board decision-making, but the key imaging nuance is that the tumor’s most relevant extent is not well represented by the post-chemotherapy MRI alone.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use only the post-chemotherapy MRI to set resection margins because the mass has responded well.
Plan margins from the latest scan alone and ignore the original marrow extent.
Assume the good radiographic response makes post-treatment imaging sufficient for surgical planning in this location.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean match for the recommendation to use the post-chemotherapy MRI to plan surgical margins when appropriate. Here, the post-treatment MRI underestimates persistent intramedullary disease relative to the baseline scan, so the safer interpretation is that both studies must be integrated and the pre-chemotherapy extent remains essential for planning. The post-chemotherapy MRI alone would be misleading.
challenging_examples.rationale: The invalidating detail is that the post-chemotherapy MRI does not adequately reflect the true residual intramedullary extent, so relying on it alone would be inappropriate. The recommendation applies only when post-treatment MRI is the appropriate modality in that anatomic setting and response pattern.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent
selected_rules.execute: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are both acceptable standards of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose treatment case by case based on patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy, and patient preference
selected_rules.source_statement: Therefore, choice of treatment should be made on a caseby-case basis based on considerations that include patient and tumour characteristics, toxicity of each strategy and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use sequential chemotherapy as a strategy to provide access to all effective cytotoxic drugs
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the results of this systematic review, patients should have access to all effective cytotoxic drugs using a sequential strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening visceral disease
selected_rules.execute: Consider upfront combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy may be more appropriate for patients with rapidly progressing, very symptomatic or bulky life-threatening visceral disease given their higher overall response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: A meta-analysis of five trials (1-5) demonstrates a survival advantage for combination chemotherapy (HR, 0.92; 95%CI, 0.86-0.99, p=0.02).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that the survival advantage of combination chemotherapy is likely very small and may not be clinically significant
selected_rules.source_statement: Median survival advantage in most trials is 3 to 6 weeks (range <1 week to 12 weeks). Therefore, any survival advantage that exists is likely to be very small and not clinically significant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia
selected_rules.source_statement: Hematological toxicities include significantly more neutropenia (1,4), febrile neutropenia (1) ... with upfront combination chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, but he is anxious to start treatment quickly. His creatinine and liver tests are acceptable, and he has no major comorbidities other than well-controlled hypertension. After discussing goals of care, he states he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that both sequential chemotherapy and upfront combination chemotherapy are reasonable options in this palliative setting, and notes that the survival difference between approaches is likely small. They also discuss that combination therapy may increase the risk of neutropenia and febrile neutropenia.
recommended_actions: Consider either planned sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as acceptable standards of care.
Consider that combination chemotherapy has a small survival advantage over sequential chemotherapy.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more neutropenia.
Consider that upfront combination chemotherapy causes more febrile neutropenia.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent, triggering R001. The case also supports the general metastatic setting for R005, R007, and R008. No rule about rapidly progressing, very symptomatic, or bulky life-threatening disease was used, since those features were not present.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status presents with newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma involving multiple bilobar liver metastases and several small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or other major symptoms, and his creatinine and liver tests are acceptable. He wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The oncology team reviews that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy could be considered in this palliative setting. However, the team also notes that his tumor is now being worked up with curative-intent liver-directed therapy because the liver disease has been deemed potentially resectable after response to treatment. The patient is eager to start systemic therapy quickly while planning for surgery if feasible.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like metastatic colorectal cancer with good performance status and palliative discussions, but the disease is no longer clearly unresectable and the intent has shifted toward possible curative liver-directed therapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat this as unresectable metastatic disease and present sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy as equally acceptable palliative standards.
Recommend a palliative systemic strategy without clarifying the possibility of conversion to resectability and surgical planning.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the unresectable metastatic/palliative recommendation because the liver metastases have been described as potentially resectable with curative-intent planning. The safer interpretation is to treat this as a potentially convertible or resectable metastatic scenario that needs multidisciplinary reassessment rather than automatically applying the unresectable palliative chemotherapy recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires adult unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent. The subtle change to potentially resectable liver-dominant disease removes that condition.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with ECOG 1 performance status has newly diagnosed unresectable metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with multiple bilobar liver metastases and small pulmonary nodules. He has no bowel obstruction or severe cancer-related symptoms, and his creatinine and liver tests are acceptable. After discussing goals of care, he says he wants treatment aimed at prolonging life while preserving quality of life. The team reviews that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy can be considered, but he is enrolled in a first-line trial that randomizes patients to a maintenance-based sequential strategy versus an oxaliplatin-containing doublet. Although the metastatic disease remains unresectable, the oncology team is waiting for protocol assignment before choosing the exact regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly all disease and goals-of-care details match the source case, but the immediate workflow is constrained by a clinical trial protocol rather than an open choice between the two standard approaches.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: State that either sequential chemotherapy or upfront combination chemotherapy should be offered as standard-of-care options right away.
Ignore the trial enrollment and proceed as if the regimen choice were fully discretionary in routine practice.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a straightforward guideline-concordant application of the recommendation because treatment selection is governed by a trial protocol, so the clinician is not simply choosing between standard sequential and upfront combination strategies in routine care. The safer interpretation is to follow the study protocol and avoid presenting the standard recommendation as the immediate management decision.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies to unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer treated with palliative intent in routine care. Trial enrollment changes the decision framework and makes the direct recommendation inapplicable at the point of choice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation outside a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous stem cell transplantation is not recommended for patients with MDS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with MDS
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend autologous SCT outside of a clinical trial.
selected_rules.source_statement: One systematic review comprising a total of 22 studies did not detect any benefit associated with autologous SCT (ASCT), and does not recommend it outside of a clinical trial (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: ASCT is not recommended for patients with AML in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
selected_rules.source_statement: While associated with more favourable treatment-related mortality (TRM) rates, if longterm survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in first complete remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT as supported therapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of ASCT for patients with AML in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in second or subsequent remission and long-term survival is the primary outcome
selected_rules.execute: Do not use autologous SCT for long-term survival benefit.
selected_rules.source_statement: If long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest, then there is no evidence to support the use of ASCT in second or subsequent remission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML in CR1 and intermediate-risk or poor-risk disease
selected_rules.execute: Use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: Significant benefits in favour of alloSCT for both intermediate and poor risk patients (p<0.01) were detected, but no difference was detected with good risk patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with AML who achieves a second or subsequent remission
selected_rules.execute: Recommend allogeneic transplantation.
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the recommended option for patients with AML who achieve a second or subsequent remission.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control.
recommended_actions: Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred post-remission strategy.
Do not use autologous stem cell transplantation when long-term survival is the primary outcome of interest.
If transplantation is being considered for this intermediate-risk AML in first complete remission, use allogeneic SCT rather than autologous SCT or chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult patient with AML in first complete remission and intermediate-risk disease, triggering the rules against autologous SCT in CR1 and the recommendation favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy when transplant is being considered. The patient’s concern about long-term disease control also aligns with the rule stating there is no evidence to support autologous SCT when long-term survival is the primary outcome.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status remains good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is weighing post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control. Cytogenetic testing, however, places him in a good-risk category rather than intermediate-risk disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow, remission status, transplant discussion, and concern about long-term control all mirror the source case, but the risk group is now good-risk instead of intermediate-risk.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation as the preferred consolidation strategy.
Choose allogeneic SCT over chemotherapy because the patient is in CR1 and wants maximal disease control.
Assume the original AML-in-CR1 transplant recommendation applies unchanged.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still AML in first complete remission, but the disease is good-risk rather than intermediate-risk. The rule favoring allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy does not apply here, because that recommendation is limited to intermediate- or poor-risk disease. Autologous SCT is also not the preferred post-remission strategy in CR1, and the lack of a donor should prompt individualized discussion of non-transplant consolidation options rather than automatic transplant escalation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key mismatch is the favorable risk category. That invalidates the rule that specifically recommends allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT or chemotherapy for intermediate- or poor-risk AML in CR1. The case remains close enough to tempt a superficial application of the source recommendation, but the guideline-concordant choice is no longer the same.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with intermediate-risk acute myeloid leukemia was treated with induction chemotherapy and is now in first complete remission. His performance status is good, and he asks whether an autologous stem cell transplant would be the best way to reduce relapse risk. The team is considering post-remission options, including transplant versus chemotherapy. He has no matched sibling donor identified yet, and he is particularly focused on maximizing long-term disease control. On review, however, he has already undergone a prior autologous stem cell transplant for lymphoma several years ago, which was complicated by prolonged cytopenias.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The leukemia scenario, remission status, and transplant decision point are identical to the source vignette, but the patient has already had a prior autologous transplant, making repeat ASCT a materially different situation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend autologous stem cell transplantation again because he is in CR1.
Frame autologous SCT as the preferred post-remission strategy since he has no donor.
Assume the intermediate-risk AML recommendation directly applies without considering prior transplant history.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the AML-specific language closely matches the source case, the patient’s prior autologous transplant makes this no longer the standard CR1 post-remission scenario addressed by the rule set. The original recommendation against autologous SCT in CR1 and the preference for allogeneic SCT in intermediate-risk disease do not straightforwardly apply to a patient with prior ASCT history and prior transplant-related complications. This should be interpreted as a case requiring individualized transplant review, not automatic repetition of autologous SCT.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle disqualifier is prior autologous transplantation, which changes the clinical context enough that the source recommendation cannot be applied directly. The vignette remains deceptive because all the AML/CR1 transplant cues are preserved, but the patient is no longer the uncomplicated candidate assumed by the rule.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario
selected_rules.execute: do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Also, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy has not been approved for patients with thymic epithelial tumours in Ontario.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all adult patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: use the TNM staging system
selected_rules.source_statement: The TNM staging system should be used for all patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours undergoing surgery
selected_rules.execute: aim to achieve complete resection
selected_rules.source_statement: The aim of surgery in all cases is to achieve a complete resection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: discuss the patient at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, including higher-volume centres when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Discussion of all patients at multidisciplinary cancer conference (MCC) is strongly recommended, not just at local MCC but also with higher-volume centres.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with thymic epithelial tumours
selected_rules.execute: consider presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board
selected_rules.source_statement: Presentation at the International Thymic Malignancies Interest Group tumour board should be considered.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment.
recommended_actions: Use the TNM staging system for this patient.
Discuss the case at a multidisciplinary cancer conference, and include a higher-volume centre when possible.
Do not use peptide receptor radionuclide therapy as an approved treatment for this thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the general thymic epithelial tumour rules for all adult patients and the Ontario-specific restriction. The patient has a confirmed thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario, so TNM staging applies, multidisciplinary review is strongly recommended, and peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not an approved treatment.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass. Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment. A PET scan obtained for staging also shows multiple small liver lesions and diffuse bone uptake concerning for metastatic disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a newly diagnosed thymic epithelial tumour in Ontario being reviewed by the same multidisciplinary team, but the added metastatic findings make the intent no longer potentially curative/resectable, and the workflow is no longer the same as the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the TNM staging system as though this were a potentially resectable localized tumour and proceed with the same surgical planning.
Push the case to a higher-volume thoracic surgery centre for resection-focused management without reassessing the metastatic burden.
Treat the radionuclide therapy question as if it were an appropriate alternative to standard staging and metastatic workup.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer guideline-concordant with the source recommendation set because the imaging now suggests disseminated metastatic disease rather than a localized, potentially resectable thymic epithelial tumour. The safer interpretation is that management should shift to metastatic staging confirmation and non-surgical treatment planning rather than assuming the same resectable-disease pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation set assumed a new thymic epithelial tumour appropriate for formal staging and multidisciplinary review with possible higher-volume surgical input. Here, the new liver and bone lesions change the clinical context substantially: the tumour is no longer simply a potentially resectable mediastinal mass. That makes the source workflow misleading if applied unchanged, even though the diagnosis and Ontario context remain similar.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man in Ontario is referred after a CT chest done for persistent cough shows an anterior mediastinal mass.
