Biopsy confirms a thymic epithelial tumour. He is otherwise well, with no myasthenic symptoms and normal baseline bloodwork. The case is being reviewed before any treatment decision is made. The oncology team plans formal staging and wants input from thoracic surgery, medical oncology, and radiation oncology, with consideration of consultation from a higher-volume centre because the tumour appears potentially resectable. The patient asks whether a specialized radionuclide therapy could be used instead of surgery or standard systemic treatment. On review, the tumour is actually a metastatic deposit from a previously resected testicular seminoma, and the thymic lesion is not the primary site of disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Everything about the presentation sounds identical at first, including the Ontario setting, anterior mediastinal mass, and biopsy language, but the pathology context is altered so this is not truly a thymic epithelial tumour case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Apply thymic epithelial tumour staging and management pathways exactly as in the source vignette.
Conclude that peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is not approved for this diagnosis in Ontario and treat that as the key decision point.
Send the case to thymic surgery and thymic malignancy board review as if the mediastinal lesion were a primary thymic cancer.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for thymic epithelial tumour recommendations because the lesion is a metastasis from seminoma, not a primary thymic epithelial tumour. The safer interpretation is that management should follow the primary germ cell tumour pathway, and the thymic-specific recommendations do not apply.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation requires a true thymic epithelial tumour. By changing the biopsy context so the mediastinal lesion is metastatic seminoma, the case superficially resembles a thymic tumour referral but no longer meets the activation condition for the thymic-specific staging and Ontario treatment restriction rules.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with previously untreated, symptomatic, endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) alone is more effective for palliation of respiratory symptoms than HDREB alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease
selected_rules.execute: Use routine HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence does not provide conclusive results to suggest that routine use of HDREB and EBRT would provide improved symptom relief over EBRT alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction
selected_rules.execute: Use a surgical core out procedure before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB
selected_rules.source_statement: December 2022: the recommendation "For patients with complete collapse of the lung due to endobronchial obstruction, a surgical core out procedure may be needed before EBRT or EBRT with HDREB" is no longer endorsed because the updated evidence does not show a benefit of this procedure.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient previously treated by EBRT who is symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use HDREB if technically feasible
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients previously treated by EBRT who are symptomatic with endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease, HDREB is recommended, providing that endobronchial brachytherapy is technically feasible.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer and previously untreated disease where selected combined treatment is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider HDREB plus EBRT in selected patients
selected_rules.source_statement: June 2018: It is the opinion of the Lung Cancer Disease Site Group that HDREB and EBRT might be suitable in selected patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The oncology team is considering whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation, but there is no prior EBRT history and no clear reason to prefer a combined approach over radiation alone.
recommended_actions: Use external beam radiation therapy alone for palliation of respiratory symptoms rather than HDREB alone.
Do not routinely use HDREB plus EBRT to improve symptom relief over EBRT alone.
Consider HDREB plus EBRT only in selected patients, recognizing this is an opinion-based option rather than a routine recommendation.
source_case.rationale: The patient has previously untreated, symptomatic endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer, which triggers the rules addressing initial palliation. EBRT alone is favored over HDREB alone, and routine combined HDREB plus EBRT is not supported by conclusive evidence. A combined approach may be considered only selectively.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 3 weeks of worsening cough, intermittent hemoptysis, and dyspnea. CT chest shows a centrally located right upper lobe non-small cell lung cancer causing partial endobronchial obstruction of the segmental bronchus with post-obstructive atelectasis, but not complete lung collapse. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable endobronchial lesion narrowing the airway. He is being evaluated for palliative treatment to relieve respiratory symptoms. The radiation oncologist notes that the obstruction is more distal than initially thought and that the endobronchial segment can be accessed only with difficulty, but the team is still discussing whether to add endobronchial brachytherapy to external beam radiation because symptoms are significant.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC with prior radiation history being considered for brachytherapy, but the key difference is that he has not previously received EBRT, so the recurrent-disease indication for HDREB does not apply.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with HDREB because the lesion is symptomatic and endobronchial.
Recommend brachytherapy as the preferred salvage option for the obstructing lesion.
Assume technical feasibility alone is enough to justify HDREB despite no prior EBRT.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the recurrent-disease HDREB indication because the patient is radiation-naïve. The safer interpretation is that he should be managed under the initial palliation recommendations, where EBRT alone is generally favored and routine HDREB is not supported.
challenging_examples.rationale: R004 requires symptomatic endobronchial obstruction due to recurrent disease after prior EBRT. That prerequisite is absent here, so the salvage brachytherapy recommendation does not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old woman with no prior thoracic radiation presents with 4 weeks of progressive cough, scant hemoptysis, and shortness of breath. CT chest demonstrates a centrally located left lower lobe non-small cell lung cancer with partial endobronchial obstruction and associated post-obstructive atelectasis. Bronchoscopy confirms a friable lesion narrowing the airway, but the airway remains patent and there is no complete collapse. She is referred for palliative radiation to relieve respiratory symptoms. Because the lesion is centrally located and the family is asking for the most aggressive local approach, the team considers delivering endobronchial brachytherapy together with EBRT as the initial plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation closely matches symptomatic, previously untreated endobronchial NSCLC being considered for palliative radiation, but the subtle mismatch is that the case is framed as routine combined HDREB plus EBRT rather than a carefully selected exception.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order routine HDREB plus EBRT to maximize symptom relief.
Assume combined local radiation is standard for all symptomatic endobronchial tumors.
Choose brachytherapy plus EBRT without noting that evidence does not support routine superiority over EBRT alone.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant as a routine combined-treatment case. For previously untreated symptomatic endobronchial NSCLC, EBRT alone is generally favored for palliation, and HDREB plus EBRT should only be considered selectively rather than used routinely.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 specifically states that evidence does not support routine HDREB plus EBRT for improved symptom relief over EBRT alone. The patient fits the general disease context, but the proposed combined approach is not routinely recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Ipilimumab is not recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of interferon alpha (IFN-α) for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma outside of a clinical trial is no longer recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma
selected_rules.execute: do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, and isolated limb perfusion are not recommended for adjuvant treatment of cutaneous melanoma except as part of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and no BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab or pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma without BRAF V600E or V600K mutations with high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with completely resected cutaneous melanoma at high risk of recurrence and BRAF V600E or V600K mutation
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib plus trametinib is recommended as adjuvant therapy for patients with completely resected cutaneous melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations and high risk of recurrence (stage IIIA to IIID, IV).

selected_rules.activation_condition: high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.execute: perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to guide treatment decisions
selected_rules.source_statement: Molecular testing of high-risk melanoma patients to characterize mutations should be conducted to help guide appropriate treatment decisions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with clinically positive lymph nodes before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: perform dissection of lymph nodes in the involved area before adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These trials and recommendations regarding axillary resection do not apply to patients with clinically positive lymph nodes (by palpation or radiologic investigation), and the standard of care is dissection of lymph nodes in that area (axillary, groin, or head and neck) prior to adjuvant therapy or adjuvant radiotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: use nivolumab or pembrolizumab, or targeted therapy if an actionable mutation is identified, as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy (in patients with identified mutations) are recommended for adjuvant therapy of mucosal melanoma with high risk of recurrence.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence (stage III). He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. He has no known BRAF V600E or V600K mutation status yet, and no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. He is being seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not use ipilimumab as adjuvant therapy.
Do not use interferon alpha for adjuvant treatment outside a clinical trial.
Do not use chemotherapy regimens, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, Bacillus Calmette-Guérin, or isolated limb perfusion as adjuvant treatment except in a clinical trial.
Perform molecular testing to characterize mutations to help guide adjuvant treatment decisions.
source_case.rationale: The patient has completely resected cutaneous melanoma with high-risk features, which triggers the non-recommendations against ipilimumab, interferon alpha, and other older adjuvant approaches. Because he is a high-risk melanoma patient being considered for adjuvant therapy, molecular testing is also recommended to determine mutation status and guide treatment selection.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man returns after wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows clear margins, but one of the sentinel nodes is positive and a postoperative PET/CT now shows a small FDG-avid right axillary node, raising concern for clinically evident residual nodal disease. He is recovering well and asks about older immune shots he read about online. He has not yet had BRAF V600E/V600K testing. He is being seen to discuss postoperative treatment options after surgery, including whether any adjuvant therapy can start right away.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like completely resected high-risk melanoma after surgery, but the new imaging evidence of a clinically positive node means it is not the same adjuvant-therapy scenario.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start adjuvant nivolumab or pembrolizumab as if this were fully resected stage III disease.
Skip additional nodal management and proceed directly to postoperative systemic therapy.
Order molecular testing only after choosing adjuvant treatment.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for routine adjuvant recommendations because the postoperative PET/CT suggests clinically positive nodal disease rather than a fully resected nodal basin. The safer interpretation is that he needs nodal dissection or other management of the involved basin before applying adjuvant melanoma guidance, and mutation testing remains appropriate to guide later systemic decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rules for adjuvant systemic therapy and mutation-guided selection assume completely resected high-risk melanoma. Clinically positive nodes on imaging invalidate that premise, so the case should be managed as persistent nodal disease rather than straightforward adjuvant therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man underwent wide local excision and sentinel node biopsy for a 2.1-mm ulcerated cutaneous melanoma on the upper back. Final pathology shows complete resection with two involved regional lymph nodes, placing him at high risk of recurrence. He is recovering well and asks about “older immune shots” he read about online. BRAF V600E/V600K testing was sent on the primary tumor last week and returned negative. He has no clinical evidence of persistent nodal disease on exam or imaging. At today’s visit, he is seen to discuss adjuvant treatment options after surgery, including whether he can start one of the newer immunotherapies.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall setup is almost identical to the source case, but the key difference is that the tumor is BRAF wild-type, which changes the set of preferred adjuvant options and makes the older agents still non-recommended.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend ipilimumab because the patient is high risk after resection.
Offer interferon alpha or an older chemotherapy-based adjuvant regimen.
Suggest vaccines or bevacizumab as adjuvant therapy because mutation testing is negative.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a completely resected high-risk melanoma case, so the older adjuvant approaches are still not guideline-concordant. The negative BRAF result does not create an indication for ipilimumab, interferon, chemotherapy, vaccines, levamisole, bevacizumab, BCG, or isolated limb perfusion. The safer interpretation is to avoid those agents and use the result only to guide consideration of standard modern adjuvant options.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss is subtle because the case preserves high-risk resected melanoma but adds a negative mutation result. That does not justify obsolete adjuvant therapies; it only narrows modern systemic choices. The prohibited older treatments remain not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has undergone initial complete resection of GIST
selected_rules.execute: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of imatinib as an adjuvant therapy in patients who have undergone initial complete resection of disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering neoadjuvant treatment with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Do not use neoadjuvant imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, the use of neoadjuvant imatinib is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Use imatinib as therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST, treatment with imatinib is a recommended therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: starting imatinib for KIT-expressing (CD117+) unresectable or metastatic GIST
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe imatinib 400 mg daily as the initial dose
selected_rules.source_statement: Until additional data become available, the initial dose of imatinib should be prescribed at a dose of 400 mg daily.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with stable disease on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib until disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with stable disease should be treated until disease progression.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient develops serious toxicity on imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Discontinue imatinib
selected_rules.source_statement: Treatment should be discontinued if serious toxicity develops.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient achieves complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib
selected_rules.execute: Continue imatinib indefinitely until further data define optimal duration
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who achieve a complete clinical response and radiologic remission with imatinib, treatment should be continued indefinitely until further data is available regarding the optimum duration of therapy in such patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+)
selected_rules.execute: Apply these recommendations to this population
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with unresectable or metastatic GIST expressing KIT (CD117+).

source_case.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual or metastatic disease on postoperative imaging, and the tumor was not being treated as unresectable or metastatic disease. He feels well and is eager to do anything that might lower recurrence risk, but he has not started any systemic therapy yet.
recommended_actions: Do not use imatinib as adjuvant therapy after initial complete resection of GIST.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for patients who have undergone initial complete resection of GIST. The guideline states there is insufficient evidence to support adjuvant imatinib in this setting, so imatinib should not be used postoperatively.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. However, the pathology report also notes a tiny 6-mm peritoneal implant sampled during surgery that was positive for metastatic GIST, though the primary gastric mass itself was fully resected. Postoperative imaging shows no measurable residual disease, and he has not started systemic therapy yet.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still sounds like a routine post-resection gastric GIST follow-up, but the small peritoneal implant changes it from a purely localized, completely resected tumor to metastatic disease.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start imatinib as adjuvant therapy after resection
Treat this as a standard low-risk post-op GIST and withhold systemic therapy
Document complete resection and assume recurrence prevention is the only goal
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the straightforward scenario of initial complete resection of localized GIST. The sampled peritoneal implant indicates metastatic disease, so the adjuvant-no-imatinib rule for fully resected localized disease does not apply. The safer interpretation is that management should follow unresectable/metastatic GIST pathways rather than postoperative adjuvant decision-making.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 only applies after initial complete resection of disease without metastatic involvement. The peritoneal implant invalidates that condition, making the case discordant with the rule.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man with biopsy-proven gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor underwent complete surgical resection with negative margins after presenting with melena and iron-deficiency anemia. Final pathology confirmed a low-to-intermediate risk GIST, and postoperative recovery has been uncomplicated. At his oncology follow-up, he asks whether he should start imatinib to reduce the chance of recurrence. He has no evidence of residual disease on imaging, but the operative note documents that the tumor capsule was ruptured intraoperatively before the specimen was removed. He feels well and has not started any systemic therapy yet.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most details match a typical completely resected gastric GIST, but the intraoperative rupture makes the surgical outcome different from an uncomplicated complete resection.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend adjuvant imatinib exactly as for a routine resected GIST
Assure the patient that negative margins mean recurrence risk is handled
Ignore the rupture because postoperative imaging is clear
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the same as an uncomplicated initial complete resection scenario. Tumor rupture changes the risk context and means the case should not be treated as a simple post-resection localized GIST for purposes of the no-adjuvant-imatinib recommendation. The safer interpretation is that he needs risk reassessment and specialist review rather than automatic application of the resected-localized rule.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is triggered by initial complete resection of GIST in the usual localized setting. Intraoperative rupture creates a higher-risk, nonconcordant scenario, so the recommendation against adjuvant imatinib cannot be applied as written.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for allogeneic SCT
selected_rules.execute: Do not use allogeneic stem cell transplantation for AL amyloidosis
selected_rules.source_statement: There is no evidence supporting the use of allogeneic SCT for patients with AL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for treatment including bone marrow or stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation as an option for selected patients, preferably in an investigative setting
selected_rules.source_statement: High-dose chemotherapy (CT) and autologous SCT is an option for selected patients with primary systemic amyloidosis, preferably within an investigative setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for autologous SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use autologous SCT only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals
selected_rules.source_statement: In consideration of the lack of curative treatment options and the limitations of the evidence reviewed, the Expert Panel believes that offering ASCT may be a reasonable option for some patients, depending on performance status, co-morbidities, patient preferences, and ultimate treatment goals.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: The patient selection process and the ultimate decision to perform an SCT should take into account not only disease-related characteristics, but also comorbidities and patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality
selected_rules.source_statement: Careful patient selection based on degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function is an emerging concept in amyloidosis that should be considered to reduce transplant-related mortality.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area and that first relapse transplantation may be considered as an emerging area pending new evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on how newer agents affect the need for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Newer agents are being investigated in the treatment of AL amyloidosis. At this time it is not known how they may impact the need for SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT
selected_rules.execute: No recommendation can be made on the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on transplantation outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Transplantation in amyloidosis is an evolving area. New emerging areas include consideration of transplantation in first relapse and the impact of novel proteasome inhibitors on outcomes.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis presents after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. Because he has AL amyloidosis and is being evaluated for SCT, the team reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
recommended_actions: Use autologous stem cell transplantation only in selected patients after weighing performance status, comorbidities, patient preferences, and treatment goals.
Base the transplant decision on disease-related characteristics, comorbidities, and patient preferences.
Consider careful patient selection based on the degree of light chain amyloidosis involvement and organ function to reduce transplant-related mortality.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with primary systemic AL amyloidosis being considered for SCT, which triggers the rules supporting selective use of autologous SCT and emphasizing individualized assessment. He is clinically stable and functionally well, so the vignette appropriately highlights evaluation of performance status, comorbidities, disease burden, organ function, and patient preferences before proceeding.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with primary systemic AL amyloidosis returns after 5 months of bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. He has persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild peripheral edema, but no symptomatic heart failure. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild concentric thickening, and his creatinine is stable at 1.1 mg/dL. He remains active, works full-time, and wants the most aggressive treatment that offers the best chance of long-term disease control. His hematologist discusses stem cell transplantation as a next step. However, he is currently being evaluated for an unrelated family history of cirrhosis and is found to have probable hereditary hemochromatosis with elevated ferritin and abnormal liver iron studies, prompting concern about additional transplant risk. The team still reviews his organ involvement, overall functional status, comorbidities, and treatment goals before deciding whether he is an appropriate transplant candidate.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a classic AL amyloidosis transplant discussion with good performance status and preserved renal and cardiac function, but an additional significant comorbidity increases transplant risk and changes candidacy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with autologous stem cell transplantation because he remains fit and wants aggressive therapy.
Treat the transplant discussion as guideline-concordant without further concern.
Assume his preserved EF and stable creatinine make him an appropriate SCT candidate.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for routine SCT selection because the added probable hereditary hemochromatosis with liver iron overload introduces a major comorbidity that must be weighed carefully. The safer interpretation is that he remains under evaluation and may not be a transplant candidate until the hepatic issue is clarified and overall risk is reassessed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule support depends on careful individualized selection. Although the amyloidosis features remain similar, the added liver comorbidity makes the transplant decision materially different and prevents simple application of the recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old woman with primary systemic AL amyloidosis has completed 6 months of daratumumab- and bortezomib-based therapy with a partial hematologic response. She has improved edema but still has albuminuria, and she reports fatigue when climbing stairs. Echocardiography shows preserved ejection fraction with mild wall thickening, and her creatinine remains 0.9 mg/dL. She is interested in the next step and asks whether stem cell transplantation could offer the best chance of durable disease control. The transplant team begins review because she appears to have acceptable organ function. On closer assessment, however, she underwent a frailty evaluation and is unable to complete the 6-minute walk without stopping twice; her ECOG performance status is 3 due to progressive neuropathy and deconditioning after therapy. The clinicians discuss supportive options and further stabilization rather than moving directly to transplant.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the same amyloidosis subtype, treatment sequence, and preserved cardiac/renal measurements, but the subtle change is poor functional status, which strongly affects transplant eligibility.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend autologous SCT based on preserved ejection fraction and stable kidney function.
Proceed to transplant referral because she has already had initial therapy and wants aggressive treatment.
Ignore the frailty findings because the organ involvement seems limited.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for SCT selection. Her ECOG 3 status and significant frailty make her a poor candidate despite otherwise similar organ findings. The safer interpretation is that transplant should be deferred and the team should optimize function and reassess candidacy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation requires weighing performance status, comorbidities, and organ involvement. Here, the key mismatch is poor functional status, which undermines the usual selection criteria for autologous SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not use re-irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: However, re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy as equally effective treatment options
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with early (T1) glottic cancer, recommended treatment options include the equally effective endolaryngeal surgery, with or without laser, or radiation therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with previously untreated early (T1) glottic cancer
selected_rules.execute: Choose between surgery and radiation based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition
selected_rules.source_statement: The choice between treatment modalities should be based on patient and clinician preferences and general medical condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and anterior commissure involvement
selected_rules.execute: Consider radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Anterior commissure involvement may be a factor that favours a recommendation of radiotherapy over surgery due to a common opinion that voice outcomes are particularly affected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and tumor localized to the midportion of the vocal fold with uncompromised endoscopic accessibility
selected_rules.execute: Consider endolaryngeal surgery as an ideal candidate treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Tumours localized to the midportion of the vocal fold, and where endoscopic accessibility is uncompromised, may be considered ideal candidates for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot tolerate a general anaesthetic
selected_rules.execute: Prefer radiation therapy over surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Other important practical considerations include the ability for patients to tolerate a general anaesthetic, which is required for surgery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer who cannot cooperate with daily treatment for four to six weeks
selected_rules.execute: Avoid radiation therapy and consider surgery if otherwise appropriate
selected_rules.source_statement: In contrast, radiotherapy requires patient cooperation for daily treatment for four to six weeks.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with early (T1) glottic cancer and local recurrence after surgery
selected_rules.execute: Consider partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery
selected_rules.source_statement: Partial laryngeal surgery, including revision endoscopic surgery, is possible for local recurrence following surgery.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 9 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy and biopsy confirm a small local recurrence confined to the glottis, still staged as early T1 disease. He remains otherwise well and asks whether another course of radiation could treat the recurrence. He is anxious to preserve voice but understands that the lesion is limited and potentially amenable to local salvage treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not use re-irradiation for this recurrence.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with early T1 glottic cancer and recurrence after prior radiation therapy, which triggers the rule that re-irradiation is not an option in cases of recurrence.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old man returns with progressive hoarseness 10 months after definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the left true vocal fold. Follow-up laryngoscopy shows a small recurrent lesion involving the posterior third of the glottis, and biopsy confirms recurrent squamous cell carcinoma. Imaging does not show nodal or distant spread, and he asks whether a second course of radiation could still be used to control the recurrence while preserving his larynx. He is otherwise healthy and strongly prefers nonoperative treatment if possible.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This is very close to the source case: adult male, prior definitive radiation, biopsy-proven small local recurrence, and a question about repeat radiation. The subtle change is posterior third involvement rather than a straightforward early T1 recurrence confined to the original limited glottic site.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend re-irradiation as salvage because the recurrence is small and localized.
Proceed with a second definitive radiation course to preserve the larynx.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original recommendation because the recurrence is no longer the same early T1 glottic scenario assumed by the rule. Posterior glottic extension changes the local anatomy and makes the case a near-miss rather than a simple early T1 post-radiation recurrence. The safer interpretation is that repeat radiation should not be reflexively selected; salvage should be reassessed by a head and neck multidisciplinary team.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule against re-irradiation applies to recurrence after prior radiation in early T1 glottic cancer, but this vignette subtly shifts the recurrence beyond the original limited early-glottic pattern. That makes the original rule application unreliable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents with persistent hoarseness 8 months after completing definitive radiation therapy for a T1 glottic squamous cell carcinoma of the right true vocal fold. Repeat laryngoscopy shows a small recurrent lesion on the vocal fold, and biopsy confirms recurrent squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor remains superficial and there is no nodal disease. He asks whether another course of radiation could be used, since surgery would affect his voice. He is anxious to avoid an operation and notes that his first radiation course was hard but manageable.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case strongly resembles the source: same age range, prior radiation for T1 glottic cancer, biopsy-proven local recurrence, and a request for repeat radiation. The subtle mismatch is that the first radiation course was completed only 8 months ago, making this an extremely short interval for considering any repeat course.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer re-irradiation because the recurrence is local and still small.
Treat the recurrence with another definitive radiation course to preserve the larynx.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although this is a recurrent early glottic lesion after prior radiation, the very short interval since treatment makes the situation especially unfavorable for re-irradiation. It should not be treated as guideline-concordant salvage radiation. The safer interpretation is to avoid assuming repeat radiation is appropriate and to consider alternative salvage strategies after specialist review.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation says re-irradiation is not an option in recurrence after prior radiation. This vignette keeps the same framework but adds an unusually short post-treatment interval, making the temptation to re-treat even stronger while still remaining discordant with the rule.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving conventional fractionation radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not escalate dose beyond 66 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: Dose escalation beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving radiotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use hyperfractionated radiotherapy regimens that do not accelerate the treatment course
selected_rules.source_statement: Hyperformated radiotherapy regimens that do not result in acceleration of the treatment course, even though the total nominal radiotherapy dose may be modestly increased, are not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with unresectable stage III NSCLC is being considered for induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give induction chemotherapy before concurrent chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine use of induction chemotherapy prior to concurrent chemoradiotherapy is not recommended;

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for durvalumab after chemoradiation
selected_rules.execute: do not exclude durvalumab solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 biomarker status
selected_rules.source_statement: At this time, it is inappropriate to exclude patients from therapy based solely on a biomarker such as epidermal growth factor receptor or programmed death-ligand 1 status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC (N2 or N3) and curative-intent treatment is planned
selected_rules.execute: use concurrent chemoradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Concurrent chemoradiation should be used for curative-intent treatment of patients with unresectable, lymph node-positive (N2 or N3) stage III NSCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks
selected_rules.source_statement: A standard dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy given in fractions of 2 Gy once per day over six weeks is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has stage III NSCLC and has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: use durvalumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Durvalumab should be used in patients with stage III NSCLC who have not progressed postchemoradiation and have no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is having chemoradiotherapy and surgery for stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: schedule surgery three to five weeks after chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with stage IIIA/B-N2 NSCLC who are having chemoradiotherapy and surgery, ensure that their surgery is scheduled for three to five weeks after the chemoradiotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions over six weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
recommended_actions: Use concurrent chemoradiation for curative-intent treatment of this unresectable, lymph node-positive stage III NSCLC.
Use standard radiation dose fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy in 2 Gy once-daily fractions over six weeks.
Do not escalate the radiation dose beyond 66 Gy with conventional fractionation.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches unresectable, node-positive stage III NSCLC planned for curative treatment, which triggers concurrent chemoradiation. Because the patient is receiving concurrent chemoradiation, standard fractionation of 60 to 66 Gy once daily is recommended. The question of dose escalation is also addressed by the rules: conventional fractionation should not be escalated beyond 66 Gy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with COPD and a 45-pack-year smoking history is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIA NSCLC with biopsy-proven N2 mediastinal lymph node involvement. He is being treated with curative intent and has started concurrent chemoradiation. The radiation oncology plan specifies 70 Gy in 35 once-daily fractions over seven weeks with weekly on-treatment review. A medical oncologist asks whether the dose should be escalated to improve local control, but the team is otherwise proceeding with standard concurrent therapy. The patient has not yet completed chemoradiation and has no current contraindications to future immunotherapy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves unresectable, node-positive stage IIIA NSCLC with curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation, but the radiation plan is subtly changed to a conventional dose above the recommended ceiling.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Continue the planned concurrent chemoradiation without questioning the radiation dose.
Approve dose escalation to 70 Gy for better local control.
Assume the regimen remains guideline-concordant because it is still once-daily conventional fractionation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because conventional fractionation for unresectable stage III NSCLC should be 60 to 66 Gy, and doses beyond 66 Gy are not recommended. The safer interpretation is to keep the curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation approach but revise the radiation plan to standard-dose treatment rather than escalation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette is intentionally close to the source case, but the total dose has been increased to 70 Gy, which violates the recommendation against escalation beyond 66 Gy in conventional fractionation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 71-year-old woman with well-controlled COPD is diagnosed with unresectable stage IIIB NSCLC involving bulky mediastinal and supraclavicular nodes. She completes concurrent chemoradiation with 60 Gy in 30 once-daily fractions and recovers without significant toxicity. Restaging CT shows no progression, and she is being considered for consolidation therapy. Her oncologist notes that tumor testing from the diagnostic biopsy shows an EGFR exon 19 deletion and PD-L1 expression of 80%, and wonders whether those biomarker results should determine whether she receives immunotherapy. She has no autoimmune disease and no other contraindications to checkpoint blockade.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a standard post-chemoradiation stage III NSCLC consolidation scenario, but biomarker status is introduced as the reason to withhold immunotherapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Exclude durvalumab because the tumor is EGFR-mutated and PD-L1 positive.
Delay consolidation therapy while focusing on biomarker-directed options.
Assume she is not eligible for durvalumab based solely on molecular testing.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant if durvalumab is withheld solely because of EGFR or PD-L1 status. The safer interpretation is that biomarker status alone should not exclude her from consolidation durvalumab, assuming she has not progressed after chemoradiation and has no contraindications to an immune checkpoint inhibitor.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette mirrors the post-chemoradiation setting, but the intended error is biomarker-based exclusion from durvalumab, which is specifically not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering adding gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of gemcitabine or capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is not recommended for adjuvant chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 65 years and capecitabine is being considered instead of adjuvant AC or CMF
selected_rules.execute: do not use capecitabine as the adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of AC or CMF
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients older than 65 years, capecitabine is not recommended as an adjuvant chemotherapy option in lieu of adjuvant AC or CMF (oral cyclophosphamide).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen
selected_rules.execute: do not treat ovarian ablation or suppression plus tamoxifen as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours (treated with or without chemotherapy) the addition of ovarian ablation or suppression to tamoxifen is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has ER-positive cancer and is premenopausal and considering ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor
selected_rules.execute: do not use ovarian ablation or suppression plus 5 years of an aromatase inhibitor as standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In premenopausal patients with ER+ tumours, treated with or without chemotherapy, ovarian ablation or suppression plus five years of an AI is not the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient does not have HER2-positive breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: Only patients with HER2+ breast cancer (IHC 3+, ISH ratio ≥2, or 6+ HER2 gene copies per cell nucleus) should be offered adjuvant trastuzumab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is receiving trastuzumab and has any of the following: LVEF under 55%, documented congestive heart failure, high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias, angina requiring medication, clinically significant valvular disease, transmural infarction on ECG, or poorly controlled hypertension
selected_rules.execute: do not offer trastuzumab
selected_rules.source_statement: trastuzumab should not be offered to patients with any of the following: a left ventricular ejection fraction LVEF of <55%; a history of documented congestive heart failure; high-risk uncontrolled arrhythmias; angina pectoris requiring medication; clinically significant valvular disease; evidence of transmural infarction on electrocardiograph (ECG); poorly controlled hypertension.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering Ki-67 for routine risk stratification in early-stage breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter outside clinical trials
selected_rules.source_statement: It is premature to recommend its use as a standard parameter for patient risk stratification, although it may be evaluated in clinical trials.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a trial
selected_rules.execute: do not use intrinsic subtypes outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: At this point, the use of these subtypes in clinical decision making outside of a clinical trial is not recommended.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer has completed surgery and is being seen to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 1+. She is postmenopausal and has no cardiac history. The oncology team is discussing several options, including adding capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen and offering adjuvant trastuzumab because of her high-risk features. A colleague also suggests using Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype results to refine recurrence risk and guide treatment intensity. The patient asks for the most evidence-based plan and wants to avoid unnecessary treatment.
recommended_actions: Do not add gemcitabine or capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane regimen for adjuvant chemotherapy.
Do not offer adjuvant trastuzumab because the cancer is not HER2-positive.
Do not use Ki-67 as a standard parameter for routine risk stratification outside clinical trials.
Do not use intrinsic breast cancer subtypes for clinical decision making outside a clinical trial.
source_case.rationale: The vignette is designed to trigger rules about adjuvant chemotherapy intensification, HER2-directed therapy, and biomarker use. Because the tumor is HER2-negative, trastuzumab is not recommended. The proposed addition of capecitabine to an anthracycline-taxane regimen is also not recommended. Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype testing are mentioned only as decision aids, but both are not recommended for routine clinical decision making outside trials.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer returns after surgery to finalize adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 2+ with a negative ISH ratio. She is postmenopausal, has no cardiac history, and is otherwise healthy. The oncology team is discussing standard anthracycline-taxane chemotherapy, but one note suggests adding capecitabine because of the nodal involvement and another proposes adjuvant trastuzumab based on the borderline IHC result. A colleague also mentions ordering Ki-67 and intrinsic molecular subtype testing to better estimate recurrence risk before choosing intensity of treatment. The patient wants the most evidence-based plan and is trying to avoid extra toxicity.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like high-risk early breast cancer after surgery, but HER2 is not positive and the biomarker tests are being proposed as decision aids rather than as routine standard care.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add capecitabine to the anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen
Start adjuvant trastuzumab because the tumor is IHC 2+
Use Ki-67 to refine recurrence risk for routine decision making
Use intrinsic subtype testing to select adjuvant treatment intensity
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for trastuzumab because HER2 is not positive: IHC 2+ with a negative ISH ratio does not meet criteria for HER2-directed therapy. The other proposed additions remain nonstandard; capecitabine should not be added to an anthracycline-taxane adjuvant regimen, and Ki-67 or intrinsic subtype testing should not be used for routine clinical decision making outside a trial.
challenging_examples.rationale: The near-miss preserves the same adjuvant breast cancer workflow but changes HER2 status from clearly negative IHC 1+ to equivocal IHC 2+ with negative ISH, which can tempt over-treatment. It also keeps the biomarker discussion present, but these tests remain unsupported for routine treatment selection.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 61-year-old woman with stage II, node-positive breast cancer is seen after lumpectomy to discuss adjuvant therapy. Her tumor is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC 0. She is postmenopausal with no known cardiac disease and normal baseline blood pressure. Her oncologist recommends anthracycline-taxane chemotherapy, but a second opinion suggests adding capecitabine because of the nodal burden and adding trastuzumab as a precaution despite the negative HER2 result. The patient also asks whether a Ki-67 score and intrinsic molecular subtype report she found in her chart should influence how aggressive the treatment should be.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This mirrors a standard post-surgical adjuvant planning visit in node-positive ER-positive disease, but the HER2 result is definitively negative and the extra testing is being used as if it were validated for routine care.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Escalate to capecitabine-containing adjuvant chemotherapy
Offer trastuzumab despite HER2 IHC 0 status
Base recurrence-risk counseling on Ki-67 as a standard measure
Use intrinsic subtype testing to justify intensifying treatment
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for trastuzumab because the tumor is HER2-negative by IHC 0. Capecitabine should also not be added to the anthracycline-taxane regimen in this setting. Ki-67 and intrinsic subtype results should not be used as standard tools for routine risk stratification or treatment selection outside clinical trials.
challenging_examples.rationale: The scenario remains highly similar to the source vignette but subtly changes the HER2 test to a clearly negative result, making trastuzumab inappropriate. The other proposed changes are also nonstandard and should not drive adjuvant treatment decisions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The care of patients with head and neck cancer should be coordinated among members of an experienced Core Team, comprised of a group of physicians and allied healthcare providers who will be responsible for the assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: The Primary Care Provider will be responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient and will offer supportive care after treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: involve an extended team as needed to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation
selected_rules.source_statement: The Extended Team will be called upon by the core team to facilitate treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: members of the care team
selected_rules.execute: ensure team members have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers
selected_rules.source_statement: Members of the Teams must have training or experience managing patients with head and neck cancers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with head and neck cancer
selected_rules.execute: recognize the primary care physician as not involved in day-to-day treatment but important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health
selected_rules.source_statement: The primary care physician is not involved in the day to day treatment of the head and neck cancer patient but plays an important role in post-treatment supportive care and is responsible for the ongoing overall health of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours
selected_rules.execute: use reconstruction expertise from a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of patients with head and neck tumours and necessitates a fellowship-trained microvascular surgeon with specific training in head and neck reconstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting head and neck malignancies
selected_rules.execute: use CAP-CCO standards for pathology reporting
selected_rules.source_statement: Pathologists are advised to use the CAP-CCO standards for reporting head and neck malignancies (5).

selected_rules.activation_condition: reporting nodal dissections
selected_rules.execute: include levels and structures in the specimen, number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of nodes, and presence and location of extracapsular spread
selected_rules.source_statement: The reporting of nodal dissections should include a description of the levels and structures included in the specimen, including number of involved and uninvolved nodes, level of these nodes, and the presence and location of extracapsular spread of tumour.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The team discusses definitive treatment planning, swallowing rehabilitation, nutritional support, and survivorship needs. His family physician is informed of the diagnosis and remains responsible for his general medical care and supportive follow-up after cancer treatment. Because he may need surgery depending on response to therapy, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise would be required if an operation becomes necessary.
recommended_actions: Coordinate care among an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation.
Ensure the primary care provider is responsible for ongoing overall health and post-treatment supportive care.
Recognize that the primary care physician is not involved in day-to-day treatment but remains important for post-treatment supportive care and ongoing overall health.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient with head and neck cancer, which triggers the general care-coordination recommendations. The multidisciplinary clinic and planned follow-up align with the need for an experienced core team and ongoing primary care involvement. No pathology-reporting or surgical-management-specific rule was selected as a triggered rule.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic, but because the center does not have a dedicated speech-language pathologist or dietitian available that week, the initial plan is deferred to the surgeon and radiation oncologist alone. His family physician is updated by letter after the consultation, but the oncology team tells the patient that his primary care doctor should hold off on supportive follow-up until all cancer therapy is complete. The surgeon also notes that if surgery is needed later, reconstruction can be arranged at a separate referral center if a complication arises.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves a typical head and neck cancer referral and multidisciplinary discussion, but the core team is incomplete and the PCP is positioned as inactive rather than responsible for ongoing supportive care.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed as though the required experienced core team has already coordinated assessment, planning, and rehabilitation.
Assume the family physician should not be involved in ongoing overall health or post-treatment supportive care.
Treat the letter to primary care as sufficient despite the lack of active PCP responsibility in the plan.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the recommended care coordination requires an experienced core team responsible for assessment, treatment, planning, management, survivorship, and rehabilitation, and the PCP should remain responsible for ongoing overall health and supportive care. Here, the team is missing key supportive members and the PCP is explicitly excluded from follow-up, so the safer interpretation is that coordination is incomplete and needs to be established before the plan is considered guideline-aligned.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 and R005 are not satisfied because the care is not being coordinated by a complete experienced core team and the PCP is not being assigned the ongoing supportive role described in the guideline. R002 is also invalidated because the PCP is not being made responsible for overall health and post-treatment supportive care.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man is diagnosed with stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx after biopsy of a persistent throat lesion and CT imaging showing a primary tumor with suspicious cervical nodes. He is referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck cancer clinic where the medical oncologist, radiation oncologist, head and neck surgeon, speech-language pathologist, dietitian, and social worker review his case together. The family physician is informed of the diagnosis and will continue routine medical care and post-treatment support. However, because the patient is leaning toward organ-preserving chemoradiation and has no current plan for surgery, the surgeon notes that reconstruction expertise is not being arranged at this time and would only be considered if salvage surgery becomes necessary later.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the original workflow is preserved, including the multidisciplinary clinic and PCP follow-up, but the subtle difference is that there is no current surgical management plan, so the reconstruction requirement is not triggered.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Automatically request a microvascular reconstruction surgeon as part of the initial management plan.
Document that reconstruction expertise is already required despite the absence of planned surgery.
Treat the case as if surgical reconstruction planning should proceed immediately.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette is not concordant with the reconstruction rule because reconstruction expertise is required for the surgical management of head and neck tumours, and this patient is currently being managed non-surgically with definitive chemoradiation. The safer interpretation is that reconstruction planning is not yet indicated unless surgery becomes part of the treatment strategy.
challenging_examples.rationale: R006 is invalidated because there is no current surgical management. The remaining general care-coordination elements are present, but the reconstruction-specific recommendation does not apply to this treatment path.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering radiotherapy alone given preoperatively or postoperatively
selected_rules.execute: Do not use radiotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for radiotherapy given alone, either preoperatively or postoperatively, compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering postoperative chemotherapy alone
selected_rules.execute: Do not use postoperative chemotherapy alone as it showed no survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Randomized trials demonstrated no survival benefit for postoperative chemotherapy given alone compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy is recommended as the preferred modality for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient has resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer and surgery is appropriate
selected_rules.execute: Consider preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy alone as an alternative treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Preoperative cisplatin-based chemotherapy alone is an alternative choice for the management of surgically resectable patients with esophageal cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Prefer preoperative chemoradiotherapy over surgery alone when choosing preoperative treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the majority of the evidence available at this time, the GI DSG believes that preoperative chemoradiotherapy for resectable carcinoma of the esophagus is the preferred approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering preoperative therapy for resectable esophageal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that either preoperative chemotherapy or preoperative chemoradiotherapy may provide survival benefit compared with surgery alone
selected_rules.source_statement: The GI DSG acknowledges there is evidence indicating survival benefits with either preoperative chemotherapy or chemoradiotherapy compared with surgery alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before administering preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinicians should recognize that the survival advantage of preoperative therapy may be minimal and a discussion with patients regarding potential adverse effects is required.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Deciding whether to give preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Decisions to administer preoperative therapy should be based on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and the fact that any survival benefit may be modest. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
recommended_actions: Use preoperative platinum-based chemotherapy plus radiotherapy as the preferred treatment modality.
Discuss potential adverse effects with the patient because the survival advantage may be minimal.
Base the decision on patient preferences, comorbidities, and suitability for trimodality therapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes an adult with resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer for whom surgery is appropriate, triggering the recommendation for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy. Because preoperative therapy is being considered, the team should discuss adverse effects and recognize that the survival advantage may be minimal. The choice should also be individualized based on preferences, comorbidities, and fitness for trimodality therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but potentially curable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery initially felt he might be an esophagectomy candidate after neoadjuvant treatment, but pulmonary function testing now shows severely reduced reserve from advanced COPD with exertional hypoxemia, and the surgeon says he is not suitable for trimodality therapy. He has well-controlled hypertension and no other major comorbidities. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options and discussing expected toxicities and whether the benefit would justify the risks. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The cancer remains thoracic, nonmetastatic, and seemingly resectable, but the key change is that he is no longer an appropriate surgical candidate for trimodality treatment because of severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy as the preferred treatment.
Frame the case as a standard resectable esophageal cancer pathway with neoadjuvant therapy before esophagectomy.
Base the decision mainly on the patient’s preference for cure despite the new physiologic inoperability.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for preoperative therapy because the patient is not suitable for trimodality treatment. The safer interpretation is that nonoperative or palliative-intent management should be reconsidered, with treatment decisions driven by his physiologic operability and multidisciplinary reassessment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation requires an adult with resectable, operable, potentially curable thoracic esophageal cancer for whom surgery is appropriate. Here, the tumor remains anatomically localized, but severe COPD makes surgery inappropriate, so the preoperative chemoradiotherapy recommendation no longer applies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and a 7-kg weight loss is diagnosed with a mid-thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma on endoscopy and biopsy. Staging CT and PET show no distant metastases, and endoscopic ultrasound suggests a locally advanced but resectable tumor with preserved performance status. Thoracic surgery feels he is an appropriate candidate for esophagectomy after neoadjuvant treatment. He has well-controlled hypertension and no major cardiopulmonary disease. In clinic, he asks whether he can proceed directly to surgery or if treatment before surgery would improve his chances. The oncology team is considering preoperative therapy options, but a recent diagnostic bronchoscopy shows definite invasion of the carina with a small malignant tracheobronchial fistula, and the surgeon now worries that the disease is no longer safely operable. The patient says he values the best chance of cure but wants to understand how treatment might affect swallowing, fatigue, and recovery before making a decision.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette is unchanged, including thoracic location, squamous histology, and no distant metastases, but airway invasion with fistula quietly changes the case from resectable to not safely operable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed as if he is a standard candidate for preoperative platinum-based chemoradiotherapy.
