On exam, the groin nodes are enlarged and firm bilaterally, but a recent ultrasound-guided core biopsy of the most abnormal right inguinal node showed only reactive lymphoid tissue with no metastasis. She has no prior groin surgery and is otherwise fit for treatment. The gynecologic oncology team is discussing the next step in nodal staging, and the patient asks whether a sentinel lymph node biopsy could be done to avoid a larger dissection. The team is weighing whether the biopsy result changes the plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This still sounds like a patient with concerning groin findings and early vulvar cancer, but the suspicious nodes have been sampled and shown benign on biopsy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Skip SLNB because the groin was initially suspicious on exam.
Move directly to a full groin dissection on the basis of the physical exam alone.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the exam is concerning, the vignette no longer cleanly satisfies the rule because the suspicious nodes were biopsied and found negative, making the clinical picture different from untreated clinically suspicious nodal disease. The safer interpretation is that management should be based on the full staging workup rather than assuming the original SLNB prohibition still applies.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is not directly triggered in the same way once the suspicious node has undergone biopsy and shown no metastasis. This creates a near-miss that should not be treated as the original clinically suspicious-node scenario.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Before preoperative therapy is planned
selected_rules.execute: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with surface or endorectal coil to assess the T category, MRI with surface or endorectal coil to assess the N category, a good digital rectal exam, computerized axial tomography (CAT) scan or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CAT scan or MRI of the abdomen to assess for potential metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When preoperative staging may be inaccurate
selected_rules.execute: Discuss potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
selected_rules.source_statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning rectal surgery after preoperative therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use total mesorectal excision (TME) principles for the eventual rectal surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering preoperative treatment benefits
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that the quality of surgery strongly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.
selected_rules.source_statement: The quality of surgery greatly influences the potential benefits of preoperative treatments.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating preoperative treatment evidence
selected_rules.execute: Account for the fact that many trials did not use currently recommended surgical standards, including TME.
selected_rules.source_statement: A substantial number of trials included in the evidentiary base did not use currently recommended standards of surgery, including TME.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When preoperative therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
selected_rules.source_statement: Recommendations for preoperative therapy presuppose adequate preoperative staging investigations...

selected_rules.activation_condition: When discussing preoperative therapy with the patient
selected_rules.execute: Discuss the potential inaccuracies of staging tests so the patient can make an informed choice.
selected_rules.source_statement: Potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumour staging should be discussed with patients to allow them to make informed decisions (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning surgery after preoperative RT
selected_rules.execute: Plan surgery with TME principles in mind.
selected_rules.source_statement: The eventual rectal surgery is expected to include total mesorectal excision (TME) principles.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced, but the staging workup is not yet complete. He has had no dedicated pelvic MRI, no endorectal ultrasound, and no CT of the abdomen or chest imaging. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
recommended_actions: Perform adequate preoperative staging investigations, including transrectal ultrasound and/or MRI for T category, MRI for N category, digital rectal exam, CT or MRI to assess the mesorectal margin, CT or MRI of the abdomen for metastatic or stage IV disease, and chest x-ray for pulmonary imaging.
Ensure preoperative therapy recommendations are applied only after adequate staging investigations have been completed.
Discuss the potential inaccuracies of preoperative testing on tumor staging with the patient to support informed decision-making.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes consideration of preoperative therapy before staging is complete, which triggers the requirement for adequate staging first. It also includes the need to counsel the patient that staging tests can be inaccurate. No other rules are needed to support the scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma returns to discuss possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. A pelvic MRI has now been completed and suggests a T3 lesion with suspicious mesorectal nodes, but the scan also notes that the distal edge of the tumor is 12 cm from the anal verge, placing it in the upper rectum. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can still be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a straightforward rectal cancer neoadjuvant-staging discussion, but the tumor is actually described as upper rectal rather than mid-rectal, making the standard preoperative chemoradiation pathway less directly applicable.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with preoperative chemoradiation as if this were a typical mid-rectal stage III tumor.
Assume staging is adequate and move directly to neoadjuvant therapy planning without reconsidering tumor location.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original preoperative rectal-therapy recommendation because the lesion is now described as upper rectal, which changes the treatment context. The safer interpretation is to reassess whether this should be managed along an upper-rectal or colon-like pathway rather than automatically applying mid-rectal neoadjuvant recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation presupposes a mid-rectal scenario where preoperative therapy is being considered after adequate staging. Changing the tumor to the upper rectum subtly shifts the clinical setting and invalidates direct application of the same preoperative rectal chemoradiation recommendation.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 59-year-old man with a recently diagnosed mid-rectal adenocarcinoma is being evaluated for possible neoadjuvant treatment. He has mild intermittent rectal bleeding but no bowel obstruction. On digital rectal exam, the tumor is palpable on the posterior rectal wall, about 7 cm from the anal verge, with reduced mobility. The oncology team is considering preoperative chemoradiation because the lesion may be locally advanced. A pelvic MRI and chest imaging have already been obtained, but the abdomen has only been assessed by noncontrast CT that was done for renal colic, and it did not adequately evaluate the liver or peritoneum. The patient asks whether the tumor is definitely stage III, and the team explains that preoperative staging can be imperfect and the final extent may differ from initial tests.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a rectal cancer preoperative-workup vignette with apparently most staging completed, but the metastatic evaluation is incomplete because the abdomen was not properly staged.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start neoadjuvant chemoradiation based on the pelvic MRI alone.
Treat the patient as nonmetastatic without obtaining dedicated abdominal staging.
Tell the patient the staging workup is sufficiently complete to proceed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for immediate preoperative therapy because adequate staging has not been completed: the abdomen has not been properly evaluated for metastatic disease or stage IV involvement. The safer interpretation is to complete appropriate abdominal staging before applying preoperative treatment recommendations.
challenging_examples.rationale: The guideline requires adequate preoperative staging, including abdominal imaging to assess for metastatic disease. A noncontrast CT done for another indication does not satisfy that requirement, even though the rest of the vignette closely resembles the source case.

selected_rules.activation_condition: newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage III or IV)
selected_rules.execute: Use chemoradiotherapy-based management with consideration of adding neoadjuvant, concurrent, or adjuvant chemotherapy as addressed in the guideline
selected_rules.source_statement: The objectives of the guideline are to make recommendations regarding the addition of neoadjuvant/concurrent/adjuvant chemotherapy to chemoradiotherapy in the management of locally advanced squamous cell or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (NPC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Administer induction chemotherapy every 3 weeks for 3 cycles; at minimum give 2 cycles
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Offer PF every 4 weeks for a total of 3 cycles
selected_rules.source_statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with locally advanced NPC treated in Ontario context
selected_rules.execute: Apply the endorsed CSCO/ASCO recommendations as modified by Ontario Health endorsement
selected_rules.source_statement: The Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy for Nasopharyngeal Cancer Development Group (GDG) of Ontario Health (Cancer Care Ontario) endorses the Chinese Society of Clinical Oncology (CSCO)/American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) recommendations ... modified by the endorsement process described in this document.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a 3-week cycle interval for induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, the regimens should be administered every three weeks for a total of three cycles, or at the minimum two cycles.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a 4-week cycle interval for PF adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For all patients with NPC receiving adjuvant chemotherapy, PF ... administered every four weeks for a total of three cycles should be offered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not delay chemoradiotherapy beyond 28 days after the first day of the last induction cycle
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with NPC receiving induction chemotherapy, chemoradiotherapy should be commenced within 21-28 days from the first day of the last cycle of induction chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage IVB locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement but no distant metastases. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is considered chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team plans induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy. He understands the treatment will be coordinated closely because the timing of the transition to radiation is important after induction therapy.
recommended_actions: Use a chemoradiotherapy-based management approach for this newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible stage IV locally advanced nasopharyngeal cancer.
If induction chemotherapy is given, administer it every 3 weeks for a total of 3 cycles; at minimum, 2 cycles are acceptable.
Start chemoradiotherapy within 21 to 28 days from the first day of the last induction chemotherapy cycle.
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced undifferentiated nasopharyngeal cancer (stage IV), triggering the recommendation for chemoradiotherapy-based management. It also specifies induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy, which triggers the induction schedule and timing rules: q3-week induction for 3 cycles minimum 2, and initiation of chemoradiotherapy within 21–28 days from the first day of the last induction cycle.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show a stage II locally advanced primary tumor with bilateral cervical nodal involvement but no distant metastases. He has good performance status and normal renal and hepatic function, and is otherwise chemotherapy-eligible. The oncology team discusses definitive treatment options and considers adding systemic therapy, with coordination between medical oncology and radiation oncology because radiation remains central to management. The patient asks about a treatment plan similar to what was discussed for more advanced disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar presentation, pathology, and Ontario workflow, but the stage is II rather than stage III/IV.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with a chemoradiotherapy-based management plan as if this were newly diagnosed stage III/IV disease.
Recommend induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy without rechecking whether the stage meets the guideline threshold.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the cited recommendation because the disease is stage II, not locally advanced stage III or IV. The safer interpretation is to manage according to stage II nasopharyngeal cancer guidance rather than automatically applying the locally advanced chemoradiotherapy-based recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule is activated by newly diagnosed, chemotherapy-eligible, locally advanced stage III or IV nasopharyngeal cancer. Changing the stage to II removes the indication despite the otherwise similar clinical picture.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old man in Ontario presents with a newly diagnosed, biopsy-proven undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Staging MRI and PET-CT show stage IVB locally advanced disease with bilateral cervical nodal involvement and a small biopsy-confirmed lung metastasis. He has good performance status, normal renal and hepatic function, and is being evaluated for systemic treatment. The team discusses whether combined-modality curative-intent therapy is appropriate, but the presence of metastatic spread changes the discussion from the original localized plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The histology, age, Ontario setting, and head-and-neck oncology workflow mirror the source, but distant metastasis is now present.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat this as locally advanced nonmetastatic disease and start induction chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoradiotherapy.
Assume the standard nasopharyngeal chemoradiotherapy pathway applies unchanged because the primary tumor still looks extensive.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the locally advanced nonmetastatic recommendation because the patient has distant metastatic disease (lung involvement). The safer interpretation is that management should follow metastatic NPC pathways rather than the curative locally advanced chemoradiotherapy recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to newly diagnosed stage III/IV locally advanced nasopharyngeal cancer without distant metastases. A confirmed lung metastasis makes this discordant even though the rest of the vignette is highly similar.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the document is used as a guideline reference
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
selected_rules.source_statement: The provided text contains only document front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, with no clinical recommendations.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic for a new guideline review after his primary care physician asked for oncology guidance on a recently diagnosed condition. The document available for reference contains only the front matter, section headings, and copyright/contact information, without any disease-specific management content or treatment recommendations. The patient’s history, exam, and test results are not being used to derive any intervention from this source because no clinical guidance is present in the document.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from the provided text.
source_case.rationale: Rule R001 is triggered because the document is being used as a guideline reference, but the source contains only non-clinical front matter and no actionable recommendations. Therefore, no management action can be derived from this text.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is seen in clinic after his primary care physician requested oncology guidance for a newly diagnosed condition. The only document available for review is a PDF that includes the guideline title page, section headings, author list, and copyright/contact information, but the body of the document is inaccessible because the file was truncated during upload. The patient’s symptoms, exam, and labs are otherwise stable, and the team is asking whether the source document supports any immediate management step before the next visit.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setup still looks like a guideline review for a new oncology diagnosis, but the actual source is an incomplete/truncated file rather than a text containing only front matter.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the document as if it still contains usable recommendations and proceed with a guideline-driven intervention.
Assume the presence of section headings implies the recommendation can be extracted from the source.
Order management based on the presumed guideline content instead of confirming the document is incomplete.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the source text available to the clinician does not contain actionable recommendations; it is incomplete and stops at front matter/headers. The safer interpretation is that no recommendation can be extracted from the provided document, so management should not be attributed to it.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires a usable guideline reference. Here, the apparent guideline is truncated and lacks clinical content, so no recommendation can be derived from the source.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man presents for oncology guidance after a recent diagnosis, and the chart note specifically asks whether the reference document supports immediate treatment. On review, the available file is a scanned PDF that contains only the title page, table of contents, author affiliations, and publication/copyright information. There are no disease-specific sections, algorithms, or treatment statements in the accessible pages, although the clinician initially assumes the guideline may simply continue on later pages.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely mirrors a guideline-consult workflow, but the accessible document never reaches any management content and remains limited to preliminary material only.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use the document as if it were a complete guideline and select a treatment pathway.
Infer recommendations from the title and table of contents.
Document that the guideline endorses a specific action despite the absence of clinical text.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not concordant with any recommendation-based action from the source because the accessible document contains only preliminary material and no clinical guidance. The safer interpretation is that the document provides no extractable management recommendation and should not be used to justify treatment decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is triggered only when a guideline can be used as a clinical reference. Since this source has no disease-specific management content, it yields no action.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The 2007 joint CCO/ASCO guideline recommended chemotherapy for stage II and IIIA disease but not stage IA.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC with sensitizing EGFR mutation
selected_rules.execute: do not use EGFR-TKI routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: These data were considered insufficient to justify the routine use of EGFR-TKIs in patients with tumors with sensitizing EGFR mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
selected_rules.execute: do not use erlotinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: No significant differences in DFS or OS were detected in the overall unselected study population.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage IB to IIIA NSCLC without EGFR mutation selection
selected_rules.execute: do not use gefitinib routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There was no benefit for either of the subgroups with EGFR wild-type tumors or the 15 tumors (4% of the total sample) with EGFR sensitizing mutations.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use MAGE-A3 immunotherapy routinely as adjuvant treatment
selected_rules.source_statement: MAGRIT... found no significant difference in the primary outcome DFS... Because there was no difference between groups, the study was unable to identify a biomarker that would enable selection of patients for this treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I to IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not use bevacizumab added to chemotherapy routinely as adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: a phase III RCT... found that the addition of angiogenesis inhibitor bevacizumab to chemotherapy failed to improve DFS or OS for individuals with surgically resected earlystage NSCLCs compared with chemotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage I or II NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: do not give postoperative radiotherapy (PORT) routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: there has been no new evidence that would alter the recommendation against PORT in patients with stage I or II disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has completely resected stage II or IIIA NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: offer adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Cisplatin-based adjuvant chemotherapy was recommended for routine use in patients with stage II or IIIA disease extent

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IA (pT1bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and she has no evidence of residual disease on imaging.
recommended_actions: Do not routinely give adjuvant chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches completely resected stage IA NSCLC, which triggers the recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy. No other provided rule is needed because the tumor is stage IA and there is no EGFR-directed or radiotherapy indication in the rule set for this case.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IB (pT2aN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. Postoperative recovery was uncomplicated, and she is now seen in oncology clinic 4 weeks later asking whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good, and she has no evidence of residual disease on imaging.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation closely mirrors a resected early-stage NSCLC follow-up visit, but the pathologic stage has been shifted from IA to IB.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend routine adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy based on the prior early-stage lung cancer template.
Document the case as stage IA and advise no further treatment without rechecking the pathology report.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not stage IA; it is stage IB (pT2aN0) after complete resection. The specific recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy for stage IA does not apply. The safer interpretation is that adjuvant therapy should be considered in the context of stage IB risk features rather than assumed unnecessary from the source case.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 only covers completely resected stage IA NSCLC. A stage IB tumor falls outside that activation condition, so the source recommendation cannot be directly applied.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with a 35-pack-year smoking history underwent right upper lobectomy for a 2.1 cm lung adenocarcinoma. Final pathology showed a completely resected stage IA (pT1bN0) non-small cell lung cancer with clear margins and no nodal involvement on systematic sampling. During postoperative review, the surgeon notes a single indeterminate 8 mm right paratracheal node on the latest CT scan that was not present preoperatively, although the resection margins are negative and the patient feels well. She asks whether she should start chemotherapy to reduce recurrence risk. Her performance status is good.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Almost everything matches the source case, but there is now a suspicious postoperative mediastinal node, so the case is no longer a straightforward completely resected stage IA scenario.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Say this is still stage IA and advise against any additional workup or treatment.
Apply the no-adjuvant-chemotherapy recommendation immediately without clarifying the new imaging finding.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The new mediastinal node makes this no longer a clean example of completely resected stage IA NSCLC with no evidence of residual disease. Because there is possible residual or recurrent disease, the stage-based recommendation against routine adjuvant chemotherapy cannot be assumed to apply. The safer interpretation is to evaluate the node first rather than treat it as a standard postoperative surveillance visit.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires completely resected stage IA NSCLC. A suspicious new lymph node on imaging introduces possible residual/recurrent disease and breaks the guideline activation condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect a survival benefit from CLND based on MSLT-II.
selected_rules.source_statement: The non-significant difference in MSS and increase in adverse events of the CLND group indicates that CLND may not be optimal for patients and does not offer a survival benefit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect benefit from CLND for any subgroup based on MSLT-II.
selected_rules.source_statement: Although the majority of patients had low volume tumor metastases, sub set analysis did not demonstrate a benefit for any groups of patient receiving CLND.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and comparing CLND with observation
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume CLND improves distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival based on DeCOG-SLT.
selected_rules.source_statement: The DeCOG-SLT trial found no difference in distant metastasis-free survival, overall survival, or recurrence-free survival when SLN positive patients who received CLND were compared to patients who were observed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive sentinel node and considering CLND versus observation
selected_rules.execute: Avoid routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible because CLND increases adverse events.
selected_rules.source_statement: Adverse events occurred with more frequency among the CLND patients than the observation group with lymphedema being the most common (24.1% of patients vs. 6.3% at last follow-up, p<0.001).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is managed with nodal observation after positive sentinel node
selected_rules.execute: Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical exam every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
selected_rules.source_statement: Monitoring with ultrasonography of the affected nodal basin and clinical exam will be required, at minimum, every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years and every 6 months from 3-5 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: ultrasound shows any two suspicious nodal features
selected_rules.execute: Confirm suspected nodal recurrence with biopsy of the basin.
selected_rules.source_statement: Suspicions of nodal recurrence via ultrasound should be confirmed with a biopsy of the basin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has sentinel node metastasis
selected_rules.execute: Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection.
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients with sentinel node metastasis should be considered for nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than CLND.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive sentinel node metastasis and CLND is the only option for local control
selected_rules.execute: Consider CLND as the better option than nodal observation with ultrasonography.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients with extensive sentinel node metastasis in which CLND would be the only option for local control

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He is otherwise well and asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The surgical team discusses that he is eligible for nodal observation with ultrasound rather than routine CLND, since the sentinel node metastasis is limited and ultrasound follow-up is feasible. He is comfortable with close surveillance and prefers to avoid the added morbidity of more extensive surgery.
recommended_actions: Do not pursue routine CLND when observation with ultrasonography is feasible, because CLND increases adverse events.
Consider nodal observation with ultrasonography rather than completion lymph node dissection for sentinel node metastasis.
Monitor the affected nodal basin with ultrasonography and clinical examination every 4 to 6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months from years 3 to 5.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches a patient with positive sentinel node metastasis in whom CLND is being compared with observation. The selected rules support observation over routine CLND because survival benefit has not been shown and adverse events are higher with CLND. Since the patient is managed with nodal observation, scheduled ultrasound and clinical follow-up are recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a microscopic metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He asks whether he can avoid completion lymph node dissection (CLND) in favor of observation. The surgical team notes that the nodal basin could be followed with ultrasound, but the patient has moved to a rural area and states he cannot reliably return for the frequent surveillance visits over the next several years. He is otherwise well and wants the least invasive option, but follow-up logistics are uncertain.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a typical low-volume sentinel-node-positive melanoma after staging, but the key difference is that ultrasound-based observation is not actually feasible because reliable serial follow-up cannot be arranged.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend nodal observation with ultrasonography instead of CLND
Schedule routine ultrasound surveillance every 4 to 6 months despite poor access
Frame CLND as unnecessary because the sentinel node deposit is small
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for observation-based management because the recommended nodal ultrasound follow-up is not feasible. The safer interpretation is that the patient needs an individualized discussion of local control options, since the guideline-supported alternative to CLND depends on reliable surveillance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies when observation with ultrasonography is feasible. Here, the patient’s inability to return for serial surveillance invalidates that condition, so the simple observation pathway no longer fits.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man with a history of cutaneous melanoma on the left calf underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathology shows a single positive sentinel node with a small metastatic deposit, and staging scans show no distant disease. He asks whether he should proceed with completion lymph node dissection (CLND) or be observed. The team explains that nodal observation with ultrasound is generally considered, but his ultrasound at baseline already shows two suspicious features in the involved groin basin, including a rounded node with cortical thickening and loss of the fatty hilum. He remains clinically well and prefers to avoid surgery if possible.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the vignette matches the usual post-sentinel-node melanoma surveillance scenario, but the nodal basin is no longer merely being observed after a positive sentinel node; ultrasound has already raised concern for recurrence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose observation with serial ultrasound as if this were uncomplicated sentinel-node-positive disease
Defer tissue confirmation and continue routine follow-up only
Apply the standard 4 to 6 month surveillance schedule without biopsy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not the straightforward observation scenario described by the guideline. Because ultrasound shows suspicious nodal findings, the safer interpretation is to confirm suspected nodal recurrence with biopsy rather than simply continue routine observation as if the basin were stable.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation for nodal observation assumes no suspicious recurrence on ultrasound. Once two suspicious features are present, the case shifts to a biopsy-confirmation pathway rather than routine surveillance alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not use sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy as standard practice
selected_rules.source_statement: Sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy should not be recommended as standard practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Avoid sequential radiotherapy and chemotherapy because no survival or local control benefit was shown and toxicity was increased
selected_rules.source_statement: There was no evidence to suggest any survival or local control benefit using sequential RTCT compared with RT alone. Given these results and the increased adverse effects associated with sequential RTCT, this approach should not be recommended in standard practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, considering non-surgical curative treatment, and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
selected_rules.execute: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.source_statement: Concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is recommended over radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized esophageal carcinoma considering a non-surgical approach and combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated
selected_rules.execute: Consider concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with favourable performance status and who have a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT, this approach is a reasonable option compared with radiotherapy alone.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has favourable performance status and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant RTCT
selected_rules.execute: Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach
selected_rules.source_statement: The current review illustrates a benefit in local control and survival with the use of concomitant RTCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) esophageal carcinoma and good performance status considering non-surgical treatment
selected_rules.execute: Apply the guideline recommendations to this patient group
selected_rules.source_statement: These recommendations apply to adult patients with localized (T1-3, small volume N1, M0) carcinoma of the esophagus and good performance status who are considering a non-surgical approach and for whom combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy can be tolerated in the judgment of the treating oncologist.

selected_rules.activation_condition: when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is being used
selected_rules.execute: Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen is a reasonable chemotherapy regimen to use when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is being considered for concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity with this approach
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients should be made aware of the increased acute toxicity associated with this approach.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia and a newly diagnosed localized mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is evaluated by oncology. Staging shows T2N1 with a small-volume nodal burden and no distant metastases. He has an ECOG performance status of 1, maintains oral intake with soft foods, and has no major cardiac or renal comorbidities. After discussion of treatment options, he declines surgery and asks about curative non-surgical therapy. The team considers combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy, which he is felt likely to tolerate. Because he is otherwise fit, the oncologist discusses a concomitant approach rather than radiotherapy alone. The patient also asks about side effects and which chemotherapy backbone is usually used with radiation.
recommended_actions: Use concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy rather than radiotherapy alone.
Offer concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as a curative-intent non-surgical approach.
Prefer a cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimen when concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is used.
Inform the patient about increased acute toxicity associated with concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with localized esophageal carcinoma, good performance status, and a reasonable chance of completing concomitant chemoradiotherapy in a non-surgical curative setting. The rules support concomitant RTCT over radiotherapy alone, indicate it is a reasonable curative-intent option, suggest cisplatin-based chemotherapy as a reasonable regimen, and advise counseling about increased acute toxicity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with progressive dysphagia is newly diagnosed with mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. Staging is otherwise limited, but PET/CT and endoscopic ultrasound now show T4aN1 disease with invasion into the adjacent pleura and a small-volume nodal burden, without distant metastases. He remains ECOG 1 and is still able to take soft foods. He has no major cardiac or renal comorbidities and declines surgery after discussion. The oncology team considers definitive non-surgical therapy and wonders whether concurrent chemoradiation should still be offered, given that he is otherwise fit and likely to tolerate treatment. The patient specifically asks whether a cisplatin-based regimen would be used if radiation is combined with chemotherapy and what toxicities to expect.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case mirrors the source vignette in age, histology, location, performance status, and refusal of surgery, but the tumor is T4a rather than localized T1-3 disease, which moves it outside the stated guideline population.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with curative-intent concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as for localized disease.
Select a cisplatin-based concurrent regimen by default.
Reassure the patient that the same guideline recommendation applies because he is ECOG 1 and non-surgical.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the cited recommendation because the disease is T4a, not localized T1-3 with small-volume N1/M0 as specified. A reader should avoid automatically applying the localized-disease chemoradiation recommendation and instead reassess staging and the broader treatment strategy for locally advanced disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key mismatch is invasion beyond the guideline’s intended localized stage range. Although the patient otherwise resembles the source case and may still be evaluated for multimodality therapy, the specific recommendation set does not directly apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with dysphagia is diagnosed with localized mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. Imaging shows T2N1M0 disease with small-volume nodal involvement and no distant spread. He has an ECOG performance status of 1 and is otherwise medically fit. After surgical consultation, he declines esophagectomy and asks about a curative non-surgical approach. The oncology team feels he could likely tolerate combined treatment, but notes that he also has a chronic inflammatory bowel condition with recent severe diarrhea and weight loss from a flare, raising concern that he may not complete a full course of chemotherapy with radiation. The patient asks whether radiation alone would be less risky and whether a cisplatin-based regimen is standard if concurrent treatment is still chosen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely resembles the source case in stage, histology, performance status, and refusal of surgery, but the recent severe GI flare makes the chance of completing concomitant RTCT questionable, undermining the eligibility assumption in the recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy as the preferred curative non-surgical option.
Choose cisplatin-based chemoradiation without further concern.
Discourage discussion of treatment tolerance because the patient is otherwise fit.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for the concurrent RTCT recommendation because the treating team does not judge him to have a reasonable chance of completing combined therapy due to active severe diarrhea and weight loss. A safer interpretation is that the patient needs stabilization and careful reassessment of treatment tolerance before applying the curative concurrent chemoradiation recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The staging still fits, but the guideline requires that combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy be tolerable in the clinician’s judgment. The active inflammatory bowel flare creates a clinically plausible reason to doubt completion of concurrent RTCT, making the recommendation inapplicable as written.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: In the only RCT for which long-term follow-up data are available, adjuvant RT following radical prostatectomy in patients with pathologic T3 or margin-positive prostate cancer has been shown to improve OS and reduce distant metastases compared to observation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has positive surgical margins
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has extracapsular extension
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: The OS benefit extends individually to the subgroups with positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, and seminal vesicle invasion.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion
selected_rules.execute: Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: On the basis of the overall trial findings as well as these subgroup analyses, it can be concluded that men found at radical prostatectomy to have any of positive surgical margins, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion benefit from adjuvant RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy and the comparison is observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.execute: Prefer adjuvant radiotherapy over observation with salvage therapy reserved
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is being considered for postoperative radiotherapy after prostatectomy
selected_rules.execute: Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases
selected_rules.source_statement: At a median follow-up of 12.6 years, adjuvant RT has now been shown to confer a significant benefit in terms of both OS and metastasis-free survival.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with extracapsular extension and a focally positive posterior surgical margin. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and discusses postoperative management options, noting that his adverse pathologic features place him at higher risk of recurrence despite the undetectable PSA.
recommended_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Use adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it improves overall survival.
Consider adjuvant radiotherapy because it reduces distant metastases.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers postoperative adjuvant radiotherapy rules because the patient has undergone radical prostatectomy and has pathologic T3 disease with extracapsular extension plus a positive surgical margin. Those findings meet the activation conditions for recommending adjuvant radiotherapy and for favoring adjuvant treatment after prostatectomy. The cited rules also support considering adjuvant radiotherapy due to overall survival and distant metastasis benefits.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT2c disease with organ-confined tumor, negative surgical margins, and no extracapsular extension or seminal vesicle invasion. His postoperative PSA is currently undetectable, and he is recovering well with improving urinary continence. At follow-up, he asks whether he should wait for a PSA rise before doing anything further. The oncology team reviews the pathology and notes that despite the undetectable PSA, there are no adverse pathologic features that would place him in the higher-risk postoperative group discussed for immediate radiation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The workflow is almost identical to the source case: post-prostatectomy follow-up, undetectable PSA, and a question about whether to wait. The key difference is that pathology is organ-confined with negative margins, removing the adverse features that trigger adjuvant radiotherapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy
Recommend immediate postoperative radiation because the PSA is undetectable but the patient is post-prostatectomy
Frame adjuvant RT as improving overall survival and reducing distant metastases in this case
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for adjuvant radiotherapy because the patient lacks the qualifying adverse pathologic features: there is no pT3 disease, no positive margin, no extracapsular extension, and no seminal vesicle invasion. The safer interpretation is observation with PSA surveillance, reserving treatment for biochemical recurrence if it develops.
challenging_examples.rationale: The cited recommendations apply to men after radical prostatectomy with pathologic T3 disease and/or margin positivity, extracapsular extension, or seminal vesicle invasion. This patient has organ-confined pT2c disease with negative margins, so the activation criteria are not met.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 66-year-old man underwent radical prostatectomy for localized prostate adenocarcinoma. Final pathology shows pT3a disease with focal extracapsular extension, but the surgical margins are negative and there is no seminal vesicle invasion. His postoperative PSA is detectable at 0.4 ng/mL on the first check, and a repeat value two weeks later is 0.5 ng/mL. He is recovering well and asks whether he should proceed with postoperative radiation now. The oncology team reviews the pathology and notes that the concern is no longer purely adjuvant management because there is already measurable PSA persistence after surgery.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Most of the postoperative context matches the source case, including pT3a disease and extracapsular extension. The subtle mismatch is that the PSA is not undetectable; it is persistently detectable, shifting the situation away from true adjuvant treatment after surgery.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy after prostatectomy as if this were the same as the source case
Describe the case as a standard adjuvant RT candidate because of extracapsular extension
Apply the survival and metastasis-reduction rationale for adjuvant radiotherapy without addressing the detectable PSA
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a guideline-concordant adjuvant radiotherapy scenario because adjuvant recommendations presume postoperative management in the setting of undetectable PSA after prostatectomy. A detectable and rising PSA suggests early biochemical persistence/recurrence, so the safer interpretation is salvage-oriented evaluation rather than calling this adjuvant RT.
challenging_examples.rationale: Although the pathology still includes extracapsular extension, the postoperative PSA is detectable and rising, which means this is no longer the classic adjuvant setting described by the rules. The recommendation set targets adjuvant RT after prostatectomy in patients with adverse pathology, not patients with PSA persistence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy specimen is being handled in the operating room
selected_rules.execute: do not perform frozen section analysis for margin status
selected_rules.source_statement: Frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen (RPS) for margin status is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: processing the apex of radical prostatectomy specimen
selected_rules.execute: do not use an en face shave technique at the apex
selected_rules.source_statement: An en face (shave) technique is to be discouraged at the apex, as this approach can result in false-positive margin interpretation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is low-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is intermediate-risk and surgery is the preferred option
selected_rules.execute: offer radical prostatectomy after full discussion and consideration of patient preferences
selected_rules.source_statement: RP should be offered to low-risk and intermediate-risk patients for whom surgery is the preferred option after full discussion with patient and taking into account patient preferences.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is high-risk
selected_rules.execute: offer referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC)
selected_rules.source_statement: High-risk patients should be offered a referral for radiation consultation or review at a Multidisciplinary Cancer Conference (MCC).

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins
selected_rules.source_statement: The main goals of RP are (a) complete eradication of the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins, (b) preservation of urinary function, and (c) preservation of erectile function, where appropriate, but, in some cases, it is not possible to achieve all three.

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: balance margin rates with functional outcomes
selected_rules.source_statement: Radical Prostatectomy should aim at achieving a negative margin, while ensuring a balance between margin rates and functional outcomes

selected_rules.activation_condition: radical prostatectomy is being performed
selected_rules.execute: consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach except in high-risk patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Sparing of the neurovascular bundles should be considered the "standard approach" except for high-risk patients.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The team discusses the operative goals, including complete removal of the cancer-containing organ and achieving negative margins while balancing functional outcomes. Because the patient is not considered high-risk, nerve-sparing is being considered as part of the standard approach. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing.
recommended_actions: Aim to eradicate the cancer-containing organ with negative surgical margins.
Balance margin rates with functional outcomes.
Consider sparing the neurovascular bundles as the standard approach, since the patient is not high-risk.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a patient undergoing radical prostatectomy, which triggers operative goals related to negative margins and functional preservation. The patient is not high-risk, so neurovascular bundle sparing is appropriately considered. No frozen section or apex-specific processing details are included, and no radiation referral is indicated because the patient is intermediate-risk and already selected surgery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. Because the preoperative MRI showed a small but distinct lesion abutting the left neurovascular bundle, the team discusses whether to widen the dissection on that side. The specimen is then handed off for routine pathologic processing. The pathologist notes the apex will be submitted carefully in standard sections rather than shaved en face. A frozen section is briefly considered only if the final margin appears unexpectedly close on gross inspection.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on radical prostatectomy for intermediate-risk localized prostate cancer with attention to margins and function, but the mention of possible frozen section on the specimen makes it a near-miss rather than a guideline-concordant example.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen for margin status.
Use intraoperative frozen section to guide additional margin resection if the specimen looks close.
Document the case as needing margin-status frozen section because surgery is underway.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for frozen section use. The vignette only suggests frozen section as a contingency, but the recommendation is that frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen for margin status is not recommended. The safer interpretation is routine pathologic processing without frozen section for margin assessment.
challenging_examples.rationale: The operative context remains similar, but the subtle mention of considering frozen section makes the case tempting. Guideline-concordant care is to avoid frozen section analysis of the radical prostatectomy specimen for margin status.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with biopsy-proven localized prostate adenocarcinoma elected radical prostatectomy after counseling about management options and his preference for surgery. He has intermediate-risk disease, good baseline urinary control, and preserved erectile function. In the operating room, the surgeon is beginning the radical prostatectomy and plans to preserve continence and sexual function when oncologically appropriate. The specimen is sent to pathology, and the apical portion is grossly close to the inked edge. The uropathologist notes that the apex will be handled with serial perpendicular sections because the interface is narrow, and the team discusses avoiding an en face shave. The rest of the specimen is processed routinely.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette closely mirrors a standard radical prostatectomy workflow with pathology handling, but the apex is specifically managed with perpendicular sections instead of the discouraged en face shave technique.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Process the apex with an en face shave technique to maximize tissue evaluation.
Document apical shaving as routine margin assessment during prostatectomy processing.
Interpret the case as supporting shave sectioning because the apex is close to the inked edge.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the apex. The case explicitly describes serial perpendicular sections and avoidance of en face shaving, which is appropriate because en face shave technique at the apex is discouraged due to false-positive margin interpretation. The safer interpretation is standard apical sectioning rather than shave processing.
challenging_examples.rationale: The overall setting is unchanged, but the key detail flips the pathology handling away from the discouraged technique. The guideline specifically discourages en face shave at the apex.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has porphyria or known allergy to porphyrins
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include porphyria or known allergies to porphyrins.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Tumor erodes into a major blood vessel
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... tumours that erode into a major blood vessel.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Existing tracheoesophageal fistula
selected_rules.execute: Do not use PDT
selected_rules.source_statement: There are a number of contraindications for the use of PDT in patients with lung cancer. These include ... existing tracheoesophageal fistulas.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is pregnant, nursing, or a child
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Photofrin because its use has not been established
selected_rules.source_statement: Its use in pregnant women, nursing women, and children has not been established, and, therefore, Photofrin® is not recommended for use in those cases (1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives endobronchial PDT
selected_rules.execute: Perform toilet bronchoscopy after treatment and repeat as indicated
selected_rules.source_statement: Toilet bronchoscopies should always be completed following endobronchial PDT to minimize the risk of bronchial obstruction, and the risk of hemoptysis should be considered prior to therapy and monitored post therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives PDT
selected_rules.execute: Monitor closely after the procedure for airway obstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: Since tumour necrosis can result in post-treatment airway obstruction, patients should be closely monitored after undergoing the procedure and toilet bronchoscopies repeated as indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient receives Photofrin-based PDT
selected_rules.execute: Advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients must avoid exposure of eyes and skin to direct sunlight or brightly focused indoor light.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with primary NSCLC and medically inoperable early-stage disease accessible by bronchoscopy
selected_rules.execute: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option
selected_rules.source_statement: Photodynamic therapy could be considered as an option for the treatment of early stage lung cancer in patients with medically inoperable disease that is accessible by bronchoscopy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.8 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is accessible endobronchially without evidence of invasion into a major vessel or a tracheoesophageal fistula. He has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the tumor is early stage and bronchoscopically accessible, photodynamic therapy is being considered. The team plans treatment with Photofrin-based PDT and discusses the need for post-procedure airway surveillance. The patient also reports he works outdoors and will need clear instructions about light precautions after therapy.
recommended_actions: Consider photodynamic therapy as an option for this medically inoperable early-stage NSCLC that is accessible by bronchoscopy.
After PDT, closely monitor the patient for airway obstruction due to tumour necrosis.
Perform toilet bronchoscopy after endobronchial PDT and repeat as indicated to reduce the risk of bronchial obstruction.
If Photofrin-based PDT is used, advise avoidance of direct sunlight and brightly focused indoor light for at least the photosensitivity period.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches the rule for medically inoperable early-stage primary NSCLC accessible by bronchoscopy, supporting consideration of PDT. It also triggers the endobronchial PDT follow-up recommendations: toilet bronchoscopy and close monitoring for airway obstruction. Because Photofrin-based PDT is used, photosensitivity precautions are also indicated. No contraindication rules are triggered.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 74-year-old man with severe COPD and poor pulmonary reserve is found to have a newly diagnosed 2.2 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe bronchus. He is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy shows the lesion is visibly endobronchial but extends beyond the bronchial wall with CT evidence of direct erosion into the adjacent pulmonary artery. There is no tracheoesophageal fistula, and he has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the tumor is otherwise early stage and bronchoscopically reachable, the team briefly discusses photodynamic therapy with Photofrin and postoperative airway surveillance. The patient also asks about light precautions because he works outdoors.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same medically inoperable, early-appearing endobronchial squamous NSCLC and even keeps bronchoscopy accessibility and Photofrin planning, but adds subtle vascular invasion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with photodynamic therapy because the lesion is early stage and accessible by bronchoscopy.
Arrange routine post-PDT toilet bronchoscopy and airway monitoring as if PDT were appropriate.
Provide standard Photofrin photosensitivity counseling.
Treat this as a straightforward endobronchial PDT candidate despite the vascular involvement.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for PDT because the tumor erodes into a major blood vessel, which is a contraindication. The safer interpretation is that endobronchial PDT should be avoided and alternative local or palliative strategies should be considered after multidisciplinary review.
challenging_examples.rationale: The superficial presentation resembles the source case, but major-vessel erosion invalidates the PDT recommendation and overrides the otherwise similar inoperable endobronchial setting.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 69-year-old woman with severe COPD and limited pulmonary reserve has a newly diagnosed 1.6 cm endobronchial squamous cell carcinoma in the left mainstem bronchus. She is medically inoperable, and bronchoscopy confirms the lesion is accessible endobronchially without invasion of a major vessel or evidence of a tracheoesophageal fistula. She has no history of porphyria or porphyrin allergy. Because the lesion appears early stage and technically reachable, the team considers photodynamic therapy. However, she reports that she is currently 18 weeks pregnant, and the discussion is deferred while the team reviews safer options and the fetal risks of treatment. She also mentions that she works in an office and has no major sunlight exposure concerns.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This mirrors the source vignette closely: medically inoperable early-stage endobronchial NSCLC, bronchoscopy-accessible, no vascular or fistula contraindication, and PDT is initially being considered.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer Photofrin-based photodynamic therapy because the tumor is bronchoscopically accessible.
Plan standard post-procedure airway surveillance and toilet bronchoscopy.
Focus only on photosensitivity counseling and proceed with PDT.
Treat the case as a routine early-stage inoperable NSCLC candidate for PDT.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for Photofrin-based PDT because the patient is pregnant, and Photofrin use has not been established in pregnancy. The safer interpretation is to avoid Photofrin and reassess management with obstetric and oncology input.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key mismatch is pregnancy, which changes the treatment recommendation even though the tumor scenario otherwise resembles a typical PDT candidate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and proposed risk-adapted surveillance based on tumour size or rete testis invasion
selected_rules.execute: Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management
selected_rules.source_statement: Thus, a risk-adapted approach cannot be recommended at this time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy
selected_rules.execute: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
selected_rules.source_statement: The DSG recommends surveillance as the preferred option, because adjuvant therapy is associated with important short and long-term toxicities and second malignancy risks with no evidence of improved survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Follow a minimum surveillance program with physical examination every 3 to 4 months, chest X-ray every 6 to 12 months, and CT abdomen/pelvis every 3 to 4 months for the first 3 years, then less often thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: The minimum surveillance program should be a physical examination every three to four months, chest X-ray every six to twelve months, and computerised tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis every three to four months in the first three years and then less often thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma on primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up investigations of sites at risk of relapse
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, follow-up should include appropriate investigations of sites at risk of relapse.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma who prefers immediate treatment or is unsuitable for primary surveillance
selected_rules.execute: Offer adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients who prefer immediate treatment, or who are unsuitable for primary surveillance, adjuvant RT is the recommended option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma receiving adjuvant radiotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Use a radiotherapy dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy
selected_rules.source_statement: When adjuvant RT is the preferred option, a radiation dose of at least 20 Gy and no more than 30 Gy is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma treated with adjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Continue post-treatment monitoring for relapse; except after extended-field radiotherapy, follow-up should be as thorough as surveillance
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients treated with adjuvant therapy, post treatment monitoring for disease relapse is still necessary. Except in the specific case of extended-field radiotherapy, the follow-up after adjuvant therapy should be as thorough as the surveillance conducted in the absence of adjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with stage I testicular seminoma and planned adjuvant therapy with fertility concerns
selected_rules.execute: Offer sperm banking; if radiotherapy is used, offer scrotal shielding
selected_rules.source_statement: If adjuvant therapy is planned, sperm banking (and scrotal shielding with RT) should be offered if future fertility is of concern to the patient.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging scans. He asks whether the size of the tumour and the absence of rete testis invasion could justify a less intensive follow-up plan. He is otherwise healthy and is considering options for post-orchidectomy management. After discussion, he is told that surveillance is the preferred approach after orchidectomy, and that management should not be based on a risk-adapted strategy using tumour size or rete testis invasion. He agrees to proceed with surveillance and understands the need for regular follow-up visits and imaging.
recommended_actions: Use surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy.
Do not use a risk-adapted approach as the basis for management.
source_case.rationale: This case matches adult stage I testicular seminoma after orchidectomy, triggering the recommendation for surveillance. The vignette also explicitly raises tumour size and rete testis invasion as the basis for tailoring follow-up, which triggers the rule against using a risk-adapted approach.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma. Final pathology shows focal invasion of the rete testis, and staging CT demonstrates a single borderline retroperitoneal node measuring 1.3 cm in short axis, though it was initially described as negative in the clinic note. He is otherwise healthy and asks whether the small primary tumor size could justify a less intensive follow-up plan.
