The oncology team discusses post-orchidectomy management options and notes that he is eager to avoid chemotherapy. Because the disease is no longer clearly stage I, the team decides surveillance would still be appropriate and plans a risk-adapted schedule based on tumor size and rete testis invasion.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Still a young man after radical orchidectomy for seminoma, with the same discussion about tumor size, rete testis invasion, and surveillance, but staging is subtly no longer stage I because of a borderline enlarged retroperitoneal node.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Choose surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
Base follow-up intensity on tumor size and rete testis invasion
Reassure the patient that this remains stage I seminoma requiring routine surveillance only
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the stage I seminoma surveillance recommendation because the staging is indeterminate/possibly beyond stage I due to the 1.3 cm retroperitoneal node. The safer interpretation is that he needs formal re-evaluation of staging before applying stage I post-orchidectomy guidance; risk-adapted surveillance based on tumor size or rete testis invasion is not the relevant decision point here.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation applies to adult stage I seminoma after orchidectomy. The new case adds a suspicious retroperitoneal node, which breaks the stage I condition and makes the surveillance recommendation non-applicable as written.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man underwent right radical orchidectomy for a 2.1 cm pure seminoma confined to the testis, with negative margins and no metastatic disease on staging CT. He remains anxious about relapse and asks whether tumor size and rete testis invasion should guide the intensity of follow-up. He is otherwise healthy, but he lives 5 hours from the cancer center and says regular CT-based surveillance would be difficult. After counseling, he is told that surveillance is still the preferred option after orchidectomy and that management should not be risk-adapted by tumor size or rete testis invasion.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely mirrors the original stage I seminoma case, including the same pathology, staging, and discussion of tumor size/rete testis invasion, but the practical issue is that reliable primary surveillance is not feasible because of major distance and anticipated nonadherence.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend surveillance as the preferred post-orchidectomy management strategy
Ignore access barriers and schedule routine surveillance anyway
Use a risk-adapted follow-up plan based on tumor size or rete testis invasion
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for the preferred surveillance recommendation because the patient is likely unsuitable for primary surveillance due to major logistical barriers to completing the required follow-up. The safer interpretation is to consider immediate treatment options, rather than treating him as a straightforward surveillance candidate; a risk-adapted approach based on tumor size or rete testis invasion is still not recommended.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation favors surveillance only for suitable stage I patients. This vignette adds a practical contraindication to dependable surveillance follow-up, making the original preferred management inapplicable even though the tumor details look identical.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering routine allogeneic transplantation for multiple myeloma
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend allogeneic transplantation as routine therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend allogeneic transplantation as routine therapy for multiple myeloma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient may be eligible for transplantation and stem cells have not yet been collected
selected_rules.execute: Avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who are potentially eligible for transplantation should be referred for transplant assessment early after diagnosis and should not be given extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan prior to the collection of stem cells.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.execute: Do not use two transplants performed in succession outside a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to recommend a treatment plan that includes two transplants performed in succession (tandem transplantation) outside of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Newly diagnosed patient is being considered for thalidomide
selected_rules.execute: Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial
selected_rules.source_statement: Thalidomide should only be used in newly diagnosed patients in the context of a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering planned double (tandem) autologous stem cell transplantation
selected_rules.execute: Do not recommend planned tandem ASCT on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: Planned double (tandem) ASCT cannot be recommended on the current evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Considering purging or selection of harvested stem cells
selected_rules.execute: Do not use currently available purging methods routinely
selected_rules.source_statement: Currently available methods of purging have not demonstrated clinical benefit and are not, therefore, recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with advanced-stage multiple myeloma and good performance status
selected_rules.execute: Recommend autologous transplantation
selected_rules.source_statement: Autologous transplantation is recommended for patients with advanced-stage myeloma and good performance status.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is under 65 years old, has no significant renal dysfunction, and has had hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in this group
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is strongest for patients under 65 years of age without significant renal dysfunction following hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is newly diagnosed with IgG multiple myeloma after presenting with fatigue, anemia, lytic bone lesions, and mild hypercalcemia. He has ECOG performance status 1, normal creatinine, and no major comorbidities. After hydration and initial remission-induction chemotherapy, his disease burden has improved. He asks whether he should undergo stem cell transplant and whether he should receive a planned second transplant because a friend told him that “more is better.” His oncologist is also considering adding thalidomide to the upfront regimen and wonders if stem cells should be purged before collection. Stem cells have not yet been collected.
recommended_actions: Proceed with autologous transplantation; evidence is strongest in patients under 65 years old without significant renal dysfunction after hydration and remission-induction chemotherapy.
Refer for transplant assessment early and avoid extensive exposure to alkylating agents such as melphalan before stem cell collection.
Do not recommend planned tandem transplantation outside a clinical trial.
Use thalidomide only in the context of a clinical trial.
Do not use currently available stem cell purging methods routinely.
source_case.rationale: This patient matches the strongest evidence group for autologous transplantation: age under 65, preserved renal function, and completion of hydration plus induction therapy. Because stem cells have not yet been collected and he may be transplant-eligible, exposure to alkylating agents should be limited before collection. The vignette also raises two practices that are not routinely recommended on current evidence: planned tandem transplantation outside a trial and routine purging of harvested stem cells. Thalidomide should only be used in newly diagnosed patients within a clinical trial.

selected_rules.activation_condition: accessory labeled single use or disposable
selected_rules.execute: do not reprocess the accessory
selected_rules.source_statement: Accessories labeled as either single use or disposable should not be reprocessed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: withdrawal time as a quality metric
selected_rules.execute: do not require a specific withdrawal time target or audit withdrawal time for quality assurance
selected_rules.source_statement: It is not necessary to achieve a specific withdrawal time target or to audit this indicator for quality assurance purposes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: gastroenterologist seeking credentialing
selected_rules.execute: complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with an option for a third year, before entering full-time practice
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered for credentialing, gastroenterologists must complete a formal two-year subspecialty training program, with the option of a third year of subspecialty training, before entering full-time practice.

selected_rules.activation_condition: other physician including surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy
selected_rules.execute: acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over at least six months
selected_rules.source_statement: Prior to being qualified, other physicians, including surgical residents, must acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee being assessed for competence
selected_rules.execute: ensure average independent cecal intubation rate is at least 85% for all colonoscopies
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing colonoscopy training
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 300 colonoscopies are performed during training
selected_rules.source_statement: To be considered competent colonoscopists, trainees should achieve an average independent cecal intubation rate (CIR) of at least 85% for all colonoscopies and are expected to have performed at least 300 colonoscopies during training.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing 300 colonoscopies during training
selected_rules.execute: ensure at least 50 polypectomies are anticipated during training
selected_rules.source_statement: If 300 colonoscopies are performed during training, it is anticipated that at least 50 polypectomies would have been performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: trainee completing training
selected_rules.execute: assess cognitive competence including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition to proficiency in the technical aspects of colonoscopy, proficiency in cognitive aspects of the procedure is essential, including knowledge of appropriate contraindications and indications for colonoscopy, application of appropriate screening and surveillance intervals (4), histologic classification of polyps and their significance, and knowledge of how to deal with findings encountered at the time of colonoscopy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a short endoscopy elective and has assisted on several procedures, but his supervising physician notes that he has not yet completed a structured colonoscopy training period. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling but has not independently performed enough procedures to demonstrate consistent performance, and he is still learning indications, contraindications, and surveillance intervals.
recommended_actions: Acquire the necessary specific knowledge and technical training in colonoscopy over a period of at least six months before being qualified.

Assess cognitive competence, including contraindications, indications, screening and surveillance intervals, histologic classification of polyps, and management of findings encountered at colonoscopy.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes an other physician/surgical resident seeking qualification for colonoscopy, which triggers the recommendation for at least six months of specific knowledge and technical training. It also describes a trainee completing training with incomplete cognitive knowledge, triggering assessment of the cognitive competencies required for colonoscopy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old surgical resident is rotating through gastroenterology and asks to be signed off for independent colonoscopy practice before starting a community job next month. He has completed a six-month endoscopy elective and has assisted on numerous procedures, but his supervising physician notes that he has only recently begun performing colonoscopies independently. During review, the resident says he is comfortable with basic scope handling and has completed nearly 300 colonoscopies during training, with an average independent cecal intubation rate above 85%. He still feels less confident with pathology interpretation and says he would want to review a few surveillance intervals before practicing on his own.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This still looks like a trainee nearing colonoscopy credentialing, with a long procedural experience and a request for independent practice, but he has already completed the minimum training duration and the main remaining issue is limited confidence with a few details rather than an incomplete training period.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Sign off the resident for independent colonoscopy practice based on his apparent procedural volume
Focus only on technical proficiency and ignore whether his cognitive knowledge is fully assessed
Apply the six-month training recommendation as if it were still unmet
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a case of an other physician lacking the required six months of specific colonoscopy training; that threshold has already been met. The safer interpretation is that he may still need a formal cognitive competency review before independent practice, but the vignette does not support the original recommendation as stated.
challenging_examples.rationale: R004 is invalidated because the resident has already completed a six-month endoscopy elective. R008 is also not cleanly triggered because he expresses only limited uncertainty about a few surveillance intervals, not a clear failure of cognitive assessment across contraindications, indications, surveillance intervals, histology, and management of findings.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 37-year-old internal medicine physician is being evaluated for credentialing in colonoscopy at a new outpatient center. She completed a formal two-year gastroenterology subspecialty program abroad and has since worked in endoscopy for several months, but the credentialing committee notes that her procedure log is incomplete for the local hospital requirements. She has performed about 240 colonoscopies during training, with an independent cecal intubation rate documented at 86%, and has done fewer than 40 polypectomies. She is otherwise competent with indications, contraindications, and common surveillance intervals, but the program is asking whether she can be approved now because she has substantial prior experience.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This resembles a straightforward credentialing request from a trained gastroenterologist, but her documented training volume falls short of the expected procedural numbers and the log is missing enough cases to make the credentialing recommendation inapplicable as written.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Approve her for full-time practice immediately because she completed two years of subspecialty training
Treat her as already meeting colonoscopy competence standards based on an 86% cecal intubation rate
Ignore the low procedure and polypectomy counts and proceed with credentialing
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although she has substantial endoscopy exposure, this vignette does not meet the procedural training expectations described in the recommendation set. Her training log is below the expected 300 colonoscopies, and the anticipated polypectomy experience is also below the benchmark; the safer interpretation is that additional supervised training and documentation are needed before credentialing decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: R003 is invalidated because she already completed a formal two-year gastroenterology program. R006 is invalidated because she has only about 240 colonoscopies, not 300. R007 is invalidated because she has performed fewer than 50 polypectomies, so the anticipated benchmark is not met.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has not progressed on first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
selected_rules.source_statement: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions to people with limited-stage disease SCLC and World Health Organization (WHO) performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has not progressed on first-line treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites
selected_rules.execute: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation
selected_rules.source_statement: Consider thoracic radiotherapy with prophylactic cranial irradiation for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC who have had a partial or complete response to chemotherapy within the thorax and at distant sites.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and disease has responded to first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
selected_rules.source_statement: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at a dose of 25 Gy in 10 fractions for people with extensive-stage disease SCLC and WHO performance status 0 to 2, if their disease has responded to first-line treatment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI because the recommendation strength is not strong
selected_rules.source_statement: The committee agreed that "consider" is the appropriate strength for the recommendation on PCI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has extensive-stage SCLC and has responded to treatment
selected_rules.execute: Consider PCI when benefits are judged to probably outweigh harms, acknowledging uncertainty
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members agreed that the benefits are equal to or probably outweigh the harms, but they had some uncertainty about this. Therefore, they agreed with the NICE committee that the recommendation should not be strong and using the word "consider" was appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has SCLC without brain metastases and the question is whether to use prophylactic cranial irradiation instead of routine MRI follow-up
selected_rules.execute: Support further research comparing prophylactic cranial irradiation with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up
selected_rules.source_statement: What is the effectiveness and cost effectiveness of prophylactic cranial irradiation compared with routine magnetic resonance imaging follow-up in people with SCLC without brain metastases?

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Do not add extra recommendations beyond the endorsed NICE recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering prophylactic cranial irradiation for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Use the endorsed recommendations as written because the Working Group endorsed them without modifications
selected_rules.source_statement: The Working Group members decided to endorse all of the recommendations from NICE without any modifications.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1, no neurologic symptoms, and post-treatment CT shows no progression of disease. Brain MRI shows no metastases. He is recovering well, with mild fatigue but otherwise stable weight and appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses next steps to reduce the risk of future brain metastases. The patient asks whether any additional treatment is recommended now that his cancer has not progressed on first-line therapy.
recommended_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and no progression after first-line treatment, which directly triggers the recommendation for prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 63-year-old man with limited-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of platinum-etoposide chemotherapy followed by concurrent thoracic radiotherapy. He has WHO performance status 1 and no neurologic symptoms. Post-treatment CT shows no progression in the chest, but a follow-up brain MRI obtained before the visit now shows two tiny enhancing cerebellar lesions suspicious for early metastases. He is recovering well overall, with mild fatigue and stable appetite. At follow-up, the oncology team discusses whether additional treatment is recommended to reduce the risk of future brain involvement, and the patient asks if prophylactic cranial irradiation should be started now that his disease has otherwise responded.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like limited-stage SCLC with good performance status after first-line chemoradiation, but the new MRI finding means he no longer has no brain metastases.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Schedule PCI urgently because the thoracic disease has not progressed
Reassure him that the negative chest CT is enough to proceed with prophylactic brain treatment
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for prophylactic cranial irradiation because the patient already has suspected brain metastases on MRI. The safer interpretation is that this is no longer a prophylaxis scenario; he needs evaluation and management for established intracranial disease rather than PCI.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires limited-stage SCLC, WHO performance status 0 to 2, and no progression after first-line treatment, with no brain metastases. The new MRI lesions invalidate the prophylactic setting.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed four cycles of carboplatin-etoposide and had a good partial response in the thorax and liver. She remains WHO performance status 1 and has no headaches, focal deficits, or gait changes. Her oncologist reviews restaging scans, which show continued response systemically. However, her brain MRI, done as part of restaging, demonstrates several small asymptomatic metastatic lesions without mass effect. The patient asks whether she should now receive brain-directed preventive treatment, since the cancer has responded well elsewhere.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has extensive-stage SCLC, good performance status, and response to first-line therapy, but the presence of brain metastases means this is not a prophylactic setting.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Consider prophylactic cranial irradiation at 25 Gy in 10 fractions
Plan PCI because the systemic response is favorable
Proceed with prophylactic brain irradiation while monitoring the known lesions
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for PCI because PCI is intended for patients without known brain metastases after response to treatment. Since the MRI already shows metastatic brain disease, she needs treatment planning for established intracranial metastases rather than prophylactic irradiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: R003 applies to extensive-stage SCLC with WHO performance status 0 to 2 and response to first-line treatment, but only in the absence of brain metastases. Known intracranial metastases invalidate the recommendation.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with high-risk localized prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with high-risk localized prostate cancer, bisphosphonates are not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: do not use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC), denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage is not recommended to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.

selected_rules.activation_condition: creatinine clearance is below 30 mL/min or serum creatinine is above 265 µmol/L in a man considered for zoledronic acid
selected_rules.execute: do not give zoledronic acid
selected_rules.source_statement: ZA is not recommended below a CrCl of 30 mL/min (or SCr >265 µmol/L).

selected_rules.activation_condition: a patient is receiving denosumab and zoledronic acid together
selected_rules.execute: do not give denosumab and zoledronic acid in combination
selected_rules.source_statement: Denosumab and ZA should not be given in combination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use zoledronic acid at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use denosumab at the bone metastasis-indicated dosage for preventing or delaying skeletal-related events
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with metastatic CRPC (mCRPC), either ZA (minimally symptomatic or asymptomatic disease) or denosumab (disease independent of symptoms) (both at bone metastasis-indicated dosages) is recommended for preventing or delaying SREs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: In men with symptomatic mCRPC, Ra-223 is recommended to extend overall survival.

selected_rules.activation_condition: men with symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases
selected_rules.execute: use radium-223 to extend overall survival
selected_rules.source_statement: This recommendation applies to men with predominantly bony metastases and no evidence of visceral metastases or large nodal metastases.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma is seen after staging workup for a rising PSA and abnormal MRI. Biopsy shows Gleason 9 disease with extracapsular extension, but CT and bone scan show no distant metastases. He has no bone pain, maintains a good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy yet. His creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bone-strengthening medication now to prevent cancer spread to the bones. He is otherwise healthy and takes only tamsulosin for urinary symptoms.
recommended_actions: Do not use bisphosphonates to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis in this patient with high-risk localized prostate cancer.
source_case.rationale: This vignette describes a man with high-risk localized prostate cancer without metastatic disease, which matches the activation condition for R001. The rule specifically states bisphosphonates are not recommended for reducing the risk of first bone metastasis in this setting.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with prostate adenocarcinoma returns after staging for a rising PSA and MRI showing extracapsular extension. Biopsy demonstrates Gleason 9 disease. CT and bone scan show no definite distant metastases, but the bone scan notes a single indeterminate sclerotic focus in the left iliac wing that the radiologist recommends correlating with follow-up imaging. He has no bone pain, good performance status, and has not started androgen deprivation therapy. Creatinine clearance is 78 mL/min. He asks whether he should start a bisphosphonate now to prevent future spread to the bones. He takes only tamsulosin and otherwise feels well.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely mirrors a newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer patient asking about bone protection, but the staging is no longer cleanly nonmetastatic because of an indeterminate bone lesion.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start a bisphosphonate now to reduce the risk of first bone metastasis.
Treat him as straightforward high-risk localized disease and apply the no-bisphosphonate rule without further workup.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean high-risk localized prostate cancer case because the imaging shows an indeterminate bone lesion that may represent occult metastatic disease. The safer interpretation is that metastatic status is unresolved, so the original recommendation about preventing first bone metastasis in clearly localized disease should not be applied until clarification.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies to men with high-risk localized prostate cancer. The indeterminate osseous finding makes the case potentially metastatic rather than definitively localized, so the guideline statement cannot be cleanly invoked.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 72-year-old man with prostate cancer is followed for rising PSA despite castrate testosterone on ongoing androgen deprivation therapy. Restaging CT shows new sclerotic bone lesions and a mildly enlarged retroperitoneal node, but no visceral organ metastases. He reports intermittent back pain controlled with acetaminophen and remains ambulatory with an ECOG performance status of 1. His oncologist asks whether radium-223 should be started to help with survival and bone symptoms. He has not previously received chemotherapy and is interested in bone-targeted treatment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This resembles symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with bone-predominant disease, but there is a subtle nodal burden that changes eligibility for the specific radium-223 recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start radium-223 because he has symptomatic mCRPC with bone metastases.
Assume the absence of visceral metastases is enough and ignore the enlarged retroperitoneal node.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although he has symptomatic mCRPC with osseous metastases, the presence of a retroperitoneal node means he does not meet the stricter 'predominantly bony metastases with no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases' condition. The safer interpretation is that this specific radium-223 recommendation is not clearly guideline-concordant here, and the nodal disease should be considered before choosing therapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: R008 requires symptomatic mCRPC with predominantly bony metastases and no visceral metastases or large nodal metastases. The enlarged retroperitoneal node makes the case fail that condition, even though the overall clinical picture seems similar.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and is age 25
selected_rules.execute: do not start screening from age 25
selected_rules.source_statement: This (Ontario) Working Group, however, did not find justification for screening from age 25 in this group (or any of the other high risk groups).

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in target population risk category 1, 2, 3, or 5
selected_rules.execute: perform MRI in addition to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: MRI in addition to mammography is recommended for women in target population risk categories 1, 2, 3, and 5 above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen annually
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the appropriate screening frequency, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually, as this was the frequency typical of the identified studies on which the recommendation for screening is based.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women are being screened for high breast cancer risk
selected_rules.execute: screen from age 30 to 69 years
selected_rules.source_statement: While there is insufficient evidence at this time to make a definitive recommendation regarding the ages of patients who should be screened, it is the opinion of the Working Group that women should be screened annually from 30 to 69 years of age.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30
selected_rules.execute: use MRI in addition to mammography because benefits outweigh harms
selected_rules.source_statement: It is the opinion of the Working Group that the benefits of MRI in terms of increased sensitivity outweigh the potential harms of higher recall rates and biopsy rates for all women in risk category 5 who received ≥20 Gy radiation before the age of 30.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is in risk category 4 with high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer plus breast density at least 50% and family history of breast cancer but not enough to be category 3
selected_rules.execute: consider adding MRI to mammography
selected_rules.source_statement: With respect to risk category 4, there are preliminary data that a subgroup of patients in risk category 4 might benefit from the addition of MRI to mammography (e.g., women who in addition to a high risk benign biopsy or previous breast cancer, also have breast density ≥50% and a family history of breast cancer though insufficient to put them in category 3).

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is in risk category 4
selected_rules.execute: do not make a recommendation for MRI screening based on current evidence
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence is insufficient to recommend MRI screening for patients in risk category 4.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. She asks whether she should begin screening now, since she has heard that high-risk women need early imaging. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she falls into a high-risk group, but also notes that she is only 25 years old. The discussion focuses on what screening, if any, should be started at this age and whether MRI should be added to mammography later on.
recommended_actions: Do not start screening from age 25.
If/when screening is undertaken in risk category 5 patients who received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30, use MRI in addition to mammography because the benefits outweigh the potential harms.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient is a woman in risk category 5 and is age 25, and the rule explicitly says not to start screening from age 25. R005 is also triggered because she is in risk category 5 and received at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30; for this group, the Working Group considers MRI in addition to mammography to have benefits that outweigh harms.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman presents to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 18 Gy, and her current risk assessment places her in risk category 5 based on her history. She has no breast symptoms and is anxious because a relative was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. Her clinician reviews her history and confirms that she is still considered high risk, but notes that her prior chest radiation dose was just below the threshold often used for enhanced imaging recommendations. The discussion centers on whether she should begin any screening now and whether MRI should be added to mammography in the near future.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case keeps the same age, Hodgkin lymphoma history, and high-risk framing, but the prior radiation dose is slightly below 20 Gy, which removes the MRI-plus-mammography recommendation tied to that threshold.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start high-risk breast screening immediately because she is 25 and high risk.
Order annual breast MRI plus mammography now based on her Hodgkin lymphoma history.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the same recommendations because the radiation exposure is 18 Gy, not at least 20 Gy before age 30. The patient may still warrant individualized high-risk counseling, but the specific recommendation to add MRI because benefits outweigh harms no longer applies on the stated rule. Likewise, the source rule discourages starting screening from age 25 in this group.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 requires risk category 5 plus at least 20 Gy radiation before age 30; this patient falls short at 18 Gy. R001 also describes not starting screening from age 25 in this high-risk group, so immediate screening is not supported by that rule.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 25-year-old woman comes to discuss breast cancer screening. She was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 18 with mantle radiation totaling 30 Gy and is otherwise well. Her chart initially lists her as risk category 5, but on review by the clinician she is classified as risk category 4 because her personal and family history do not meet the criteria for the highest-risk category. She has no breast symptoms and wants to know if she should start screening now because she expects to turn 26 soon. The conversation focuses on whether she needs MRI in addition to mammography at this age and whether the prior chest radiation changes that plan.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still has prior mantle radiation at 30 Gy and is the same age as the source vignette, but the risk category has been revised from 5 to 4, which changes the screening recommendation set.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Begin screening now because she had 30 Gy chest radiation before age 30.
Order MRI plus mammography as if she were still in risk category 5.
Assume annual high-risk screening from age 25 is indicated.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the source recommendation because the patient is no longer in risk category 5. The MRI-plus-mammography recommendation tied to category 5 and ≥20 Gy radiation before age 30 does not apply, and the source rule also does not support screening from age 25. For risk category 4, the evidence is insufficient for a general MRI screening recommendation, so the safer interpretation is to defer routine MRI-based screening unless other qualifying features are present.
challenging_examples.rationale: R005 is invalidated because it specifically requires risk category 5, which she does not have. R001 and R002 also do not directly apply to this revised risk category, and R007 indicates insufficient evidence to recommend MRI screening in category 4 generally.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence
selected_rules.execute: Evaluate according to the symptoms
selected_rules.source_statement: Survivors who develop symptoms suggestive of recurrence, should be evaluated according to those symptoms.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for NSCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: After curative-intent treatment for SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Schedule follow-up visits with medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging every 3 months in years 1 and 2, every 6 months in year 3, then annually thereafter
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for SCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging. Clinical evaluations should be conducted every three months in years 1 and 2, every six months in year 3 and annually thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has late side effects of curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide support to help manage symptoms and improve quality of life
selected_rules.source_statement: Health-related quality of life (QoL) is very important for long-term survivors suffering from late side effects of their curative-intent therapy (including surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy). The following is a summary of issues reported by survivors. Health care professionals need to aid lung cancer survivors in handling these symptoms to improve QoL.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy
selected_rules.execute: Provide surveillance follow-up care
selected_rules.source_statement: For lung cancer survivors who have completed curative-intent therapy, surveillance is required and may be provided by specialists, family physicians or hospital-based nurses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy for NSCLC or SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Provide smoking cessation counselling
selected_rules.source_statement: Smoking cessation counselling is recommended for patients who have completed curativeintent therapy for NSCLC and SCLC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Providing smoking cessation advice to a lung cancer survivor
selected_rules.execute: Use behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice when possible
selected_rules.source_statement: Although verbal cessation advice from a health care professional is of benefit, interventions that involve behavioural and pharmacotherapy support in addition to verbal advice is recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: A lung cancer survivor has completed curative-intent therapy and ongoing surveillance is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging
selected_rules.source_statement: Following curative-intent treatment for NSCLC, survivors should receive scheduled follow-up visits that include a medical history, physical examination and chest imaging.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough, new left-sided chest discomfort, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg. He also reports increasing fatigue but no fever or infectious symptoms. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. Given the new symptoms in a lung cancer survivor, recurrence must be considered, while he also remains due for ongoing surveillance after curative-intent treatment.
recommended_actions: Evaluate the new cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and fatigue according to symptoms suggestive of recurrence.
Provide surveillance follow-up care after completion of curative-intent therapy.
Ensure surveillance includes medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging.
Provide smoking cessation counselling.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because this lung cancer survivor has new symptoms suggestive of recurrence. R005 applies because he has completed curative-intent therapy, so surveillance follow-up care is required. R008 applies because ongoing surveillance should include medical history, physical examination, and chest imaging. R006 applies because smoking cessation counselling is recommended after curative-intent therapy for NSCLC.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has had intermittent dry cough and mild fatigue, but no chest pain, hemoptysis, weight loss, or fevers. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. A surveillance chest CT obtained last week showed only stable postoperative scarring with no new nodules, adenopathy, or pleural effusion. He presents mainly to review the scan and to discuss whether he needs any further cancer testing at this time.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains a post-lobectomy lung cancer survivor with cough and ongoing smoking, so it looks like a recurrence workup is indicated at first glance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Escalate immediately to a full recurrence evaluation based on the cough alone
Order urgent additional cancer-directed imaging or biopsy despite the negative surveillance CT
Treat the visit as symptomatic recurrence until proven otherwise
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not clearly meet the recurrence-symptom trigger because the new complaints are nonspecific and the recent chest CT is unrevealing. The safer interpretation is routine surveillance follow-up with symptom review and reassessment if symptoms evolve or objective findings appear.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires symptoms suggestive of recurrence. Here, the symptoms are mild and nonspecific, and recent imaging does not support recurrence. The case is better handled as routine surveillance rather than assuming symptomatic relapse.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man is 18 months after lobectomy and adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II non-small cell lung cancer. He completed curative-intent therapy and has been attending routine surveillance visits. Over the past 3 weeks, he has developed a persistent dry cough and new left-sided chest discomfort, with unintentional weight loss of 4 kg and increasing fatigue. He also reports waking at night with reflux and occasional choking after meals. He continues to smoke about 5 cigarettes per day despite prior quit attempts. On exam, he is thin but in no acute distress; oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, and lung exam is unchanged from baseline. A recent contrast CT chest and upper abdomen shows a new distal esophageal mass with associated mediastinal widening, while the remaining lung parenchyma is stable and there is no discrete pulmonary nodule or pleural recurrence. The question is whether his symptoms should be managed as lung cancer relapse or evaluated through another diagnostic pathway.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the classic post-treatment lung cancer survivor with cough, chest discomfort, weight loss, and smoking, making recurrence seem likely on a superficial read.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Assume this is lung cancer recurrence and proceed with lung cancer relapse management
Order lung-directed staging as if the chest symptoms were from recurrent NSCLC
Document the symptoms as suggestive of recurrent lung cancer without further differential workup
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although he is a lung cancer survivor with concerning symptoms, the imaging localizes the abnormality to a distal esophageal mass rather than recurrent lung cancer. This is not guideline-concordant for the lung-cancer recurrence pathway; the safer interpretation is that he needs prompt evaluation of a new non-pulmonary malignancy or alternate cause of symptoms.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies when a lung cancer survivor develops symptoms suggestive of recurrence. In this vignette, the objective findings point away from recurrent NSCLC and toward a separate esophageal process, so the original recurrence recommendation is not the best fit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance reaches age 80.
selected_rules.execute: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
selected_rules.source_statement: Serial biopsy should not continue past the age of 80.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has low-risk localized prostate cancer (Gleason score ≤6).
selected_rules.execute: Use active surveillance as the preferred disease management strategy.
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with low-risk (Gleason score ≤6) localized prostate cancer, AS is the preferred disease management strategy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include PSA testing every 3 to 6 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -PSA test every 3 to 6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Include digital rectal examination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -DRE every year.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance for localized prostate cancer.
selected_rules.execute: Perform a 12- to 14-core confirmatory TRUS biopsy including anterior directed cores within 6 to 12 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient is on active surveillance after confirmatory biopsy.
selected_rules.execute: Repeat serial biopsy at least every 3 to 5 years.
selected_rules.source_statement: The AS protocol should include the following tests: -12-to 14-core confirmatory transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) biopsy (including anterior directed cores) within 6 to 12 months, then serial biopsy a minimum of every 3 to 5 years thereafter.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, or very low PSA free/total ratio.
selected_rules.execute: Investigate further with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Discordant findings between a patient's clinical course and pathologic findings can include rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, higher PSA than expected for prostate size, DRE abnormality, and very low PSA free/total ratio. Presence of these findings requires further investigation with mpMRI or earlier repeat biopsy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient on active surveillance has discordant clinical findings and pathologic findings.
selected_rules.execute: Use mpMRI at entry or during follow-up when indicated.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, being cognizant of both the high cost of mpMRI and its promise, it is recommended that when a patient's clinical findings are discordant with the pathologic findings, a mpMRI is indicated. When indicated, it may be considered at entry or during follow-up.

source_case.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been managed with active surveillance. His PSA has been stable around 5.1 ng/mL, his digital rectal exams have remained normal, and his confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading. He feels well and asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
recommended_actions: Do not continue serial biopsy past age 80.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that serial biopsy should not continue once a patient on active surveillance reaches age 80. No additional discordant findings are present to trigger imaging or earlier repeat biopsy recommendations.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: An 80-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) has been followed on active surveillance. His PSA has recently risen from 5.0 to 7.4 ng/mL over 8 months, and his PSA density is now 0.23 based on a relatively small prostate volume. He still feels well, but his recent digital rectal exam was slightly more firm than prior exams, though not frankly nodular. A confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago showed no upgrading, and he asks whether he can simply continue with routine scheduled biopsies now that he is older. He has no urinary obstruction or bone pain.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on an 80-year-old man on active surveillance asking about ongoing biopsy, but subtle discordant PSA and exam changes make the situation unlike a stable surveillance follow-up.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Stop all serial biopsies solely because he is 80
Assure him no further workup is needed since the prior biopsy was stable
Continue the same surveillance schedule without reassessment
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the age-based stop-biopsy rule because the patient has discordant clinical findings: a rapidly rising PSA, PSA density over 0.2, and a change on DRE. The safer interpretation is that he needs further evaluation with mpMRI and/or earlier repeat biopsy rather than simply discontinuing surveillance because of age.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 applies only to stopping serial biopsy at age 80 in an otherwise stable active-surveillance patient. The added PSA kinetics, PSA density, and DRE change invalidate that simple age-based application and instead trigger discordant-finding workup.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 79-year-old man with localized prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago (Gleason 3+3) remains on active surveillance. His PSA has stayed around 5.2 ng/mL, his PSA density is 0.12, and his digital rectal exams have remained normal. He had a confirmatory TRUS biopsy 3 years ago that showed no upgrading. He asks whether he still needs scheduled biopsies, since he is approaching 80 and feels well. He has no new urinary symptoms and no concerning changes in PSA kinetics or exam findings.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This is almost identical to the source vignette, including low-risk disease, active surveillance, stable PSA, normal DRE, and a prior negative confirmatory biopsy, but the patient is not yet 80.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend stopping serial biopsy now because he is nearly 80
Discontinue surveillance biopsies based on age alone
Tell him no further tissue sampling is needed
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case does not meet the rule for discontinuing serial biopsy because the patient is 79, not 80. The safer interpretation is that age-based cessation has not yet been reached, so ongoing active-surveillance follow-up should continue according to protocol unless other clinical factors change.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 specifically requires that the patient on active surveillance reach age 80 before serial biopsy should not continue. Being one year younger makes the recommendation inapplicable, even though the rest of the vignette is similar.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document contains only publication/contact and copyright information, with no clinical recommendations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: For information about this document, please contact Dr. Eric Winquist, the lead author, through the PEBC via: Phone: 905-527-4322 ext. 42822 Fax: 905 526-6775 E-mail: ccopgi@mcmaster.ca

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document states that the evidence review has been published as a supplement.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: The evidence review has been published as a Supplement

selected_rules.activation_condition: Document states copyright restrictions on reproduction of the report and illustrations.
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action can be extracted.
selected_rules.source_statement: This report is copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario; the report and the illustrations herein may not be reproduced without the express written permission of Cancer Care Ontario.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. At the end of the note, the document includes only publication details and contact information for the lead author, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, along with a statement that the evidence review has been published as a supplement. It also notes that the report and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission. No diagnostic algorithm, treatment recommendation, or follow-up plan is provided in the document excerpt.
recommended_actions: No clinical action can be extracted from this document excerpt.
source_case.rationale: The excerpt contains only publication/contact information, a statement that the evidence review was published as a supplement, and copyright restrictions, with no clinical recommendations. Therefore the triggered rules all map to no actionable clinical guidance.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The printed consult packet he brings to clinic appears to be from Cancer Care Ontario and includes the lead author’s contact information, Dr. Eric Winquist, through the PEBC, plus a note that the evidence review was published as a supplement. However, unlike a usual guideline handout, the final page also contains a brief management algorithm recommending repeat imaging in 3 months for small nodules and biopsy for enlarging lesions. The report still states that the text and illustrations are copyrighted by Cancer Care Ontario and may not be reproduced without written permission.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Looks like the same administrative guideline excerpt, but it now includes an actual management algorithm rather than only publication/contact details.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the document as a full guideline and follow the included nodule surveillance algorithm without further verification
Assume the publication/contact page itself constitutes a valid recommendation source for this patient
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the original no-recommendation rule because the excerpt is no longer limited to publication/contact and copyright information; it now contains a specific management algorithm. The safer interpretation is to verify the full source guideline and apply standard clinical nodule workup only if the algorithm is confirmed in the official document.
challenging_examples.rationale: The activation condition requires only publication/contact and copyright information with no clinical recommendations. The added surveillance algorithm breaks that condition, so the original no-action recommendation no longer applies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 58-year-old man is referred to oncology after an incidental lung nodule is found on CT performed for persistent cough. The note he brings includes the same Cancer Care Ontario footer, the lead author contact details for Dr. Eric Winquist through the PEBC, and a statement that the evidence review was published as a supplement. It also includes the copyright notice stating the report and illustrations may not be reproduced without written permission. The misleading difference is that the patient’s packet is not just a single-page excerpt: the front page is followed by unrelated pulmonary function test results and a local clinic template asking the oncologist to decide next steps. No actual diagnostic algorithm or treatment recommendation is present anywhere in the document itself.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The document still looks like the same guideline packet with supplement and copyright language, but it is only an administrative cover sheet attached to unrelated clinic material, not the guideline content itself.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the packet as if it contains a clinical recommendation because it mentions a supplement and copyright notice
Search for a treatment plan in the attached clinic template and assume the guideline directs management
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains non-actionable, but for a different reason: the material containing the supplement and copyright language is only a cover sheet attached to unrelated local documents. There is still no guideline recommendation to apply. The safer interpretation is to ignore the administrative footer and obtain the actual clinical guideline if decision support is needed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source rules only permit a no-action conclusion when the excerpt itself contains publication/contact information, supplement language, and copyright restrictions without clinical guidance. Here, those phrases appear only on a cover sheet and are not accompanied by any recommendation, so there is no guideline-concordant action to extract.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as routine primary therapy with chemotherapy in this population
selected_rules.execute: Do not use G-CSF routinely as primary therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor as primary therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering a fractionated weekly CHOP schedule instead of standard CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Use standard CHOP rather than fractionated weekly CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No benefits from fractionating the treatment schedule have been detected; the potential for outcomes to be inferior is suggested.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide in a compressed 14-day schedule
selected_rules.execute: Avoid CHOEP-14 because of excessive toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: patients receiving CHOEP every 14 days experienced excessive toxicity

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering CHOP plus etoposide versus CHOP alone in older patients
selected_rules.execute: Do not add etoposide routinely to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: No differences were detected in response rate, TTF, or OS, and the authors commented that the addition of etoposide was associated with significant toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering more intensive regimens than CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Avoid more intensive regimens when they add toxicity without survival benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: Two studies testing chemotherapy regimens that are more intensive than CHOP both detected an increase in toxicity with no survival benefit detected.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering second- or third-generation regimens instead of CHOP
selected_rules.execute: Prefer CHOP because it is at least as effective or less toxic
selected_rules.source_statement: Subset analyses comparing CHOP with "second and third generation" regimens show that CHOP is at least as effective or less toxic than these other regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is older than 60 with newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbid illness
selected_rules.execute: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisone (CHOP) is recommended for patients with no apparent cardiac disease or significant comorbidity. Dose and schedule should be the same as that used in younger patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and meets the guideline target population
selected_rules.execute: Add rituximab to CHOP
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to CHOP is recommended for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure.
recommended_actions: Use CHOP combination chemotherapy with the same dose and schedule as in younger patients.
Add rituximab to CHOP.
source_case.rationale: This patient fits the guideline target population: older than 60, newly diagnosed advanced-stage aggressive histology non-Hodgkin lymphoma, ECOG less than 4, and no significant comorbidity. For this group, CHOP is recommended at the same dose and schedule as in younger patients. Because he has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, rituximab should be added to CHOP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension and no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure. During treatment planning, the family mentions a recent inpatient admission for atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, but he converted quickly and has had no structural heart disease on prior echo.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like an older, fit patient with advanced diffuse large B-cell lymphoma being evaluated for standard first-line CHOP-based therapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start standard-dose CHOP and add rituximab without further discussion
Assume he meets the older-adult CHOP recommendation because he has no major comorbidity
Proceed as if cardiac status is clearly adequate despite the recent arrhythmia admission
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean match for the older-adult CHOP recommendation because the newly disclosed atrial fibrillation admission raises a cardiac concern that makes the ‘no apparent cardiac disease’ condition uncertain. The safer interpretation is to verify cardiac suitability before applying the standard older-patient CHOP pathway, rather than automatically treating him as guideline-concordant for that recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette is intentionally close to the source but adds a subtle cardiac history that weakens the required ‘no apparent cardiac disease’ criterion. That makes the rule-based recommendation for standard CHOP in this population no longer straightforwardly applicable.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed stage IV diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with bulky abdominal nodes, night sweats, and weight loss. His ECOG performance status is 1. He has well-controlled hypertension but no known cardiac disease, heart failure, or other major comorbid illness. Baseline labs show mild anemia and elevated LDH; renal and liver function are acceptable. The oncology team is planning first-line treatment and discusses CHOP-based chemotherapy. Because of his age, they consider whether to intensify treatment, add etoposide, or use a compressed 14-day schedule with growth factor support. The patient asks whether a more aggressive regimen would improve his chances of cure. PET/CT staging is completed before therapy and shows disease confined to cervical, mediastinal, and abdominal nodal stations below the diaphragm, with no extranodal involvement or marrow disease.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This retains the same age, histology, symptoms, performance status, and CHOP planning, but the staging detail quietly changes the disease extent.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat as advanced-stage disease and apply the older-adult CHOP recommendation automatically
Add rituximab to CHOP on the assumption that the guideline target population is met
Ignore the staging result because the rest of the case appears typical for first-line DLBCL
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is no longer guideline-concordant for the advanced-stage recommendation because the PET/CT description suggests limited-stage nodal disease rather than advanced stage IV lymphoma. A safer interpretation is to confirm stage-specific management before applying the older-adult advanced-stage CHOP guidance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation depends on advanced-stage disease. Changing the staging to a pattern more consistent with limited-stage nodal involvement removes the key trigger even though the rest of the presentation is nearly unchanged.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV-related lymphoma and CD4 count below 50/mm3
selected_rules.execute: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: The addition of rituximab to chemotherapy in patients with CD4 <50/mm 3 is not recommended.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with aggressive histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated HIV-related lymphoma, CD4 count at least 50/mm3, and candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give combination chemotherapy with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with HIV-related lymphoma with a CD4 count ≥50/mm 3 who are candidates for treatment with curative intent and will receive combination chemotherapy with curative intent ... should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously untreated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: Previously untreated patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy, should receive their chemotherapy in combination with rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, no prior rituximab, and candidate for chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Give chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have not previously received rituximab and who are appropriate candidates for chemotherapy should receive this chemotherapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Previously treated indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL, prior rituximab exposure, response lasted at least one year, and candidate for therapy
selected_rules.execute: Give therapy in combination with rituximab or rituximab monotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who have previously received rituximab ... and who have achieved a response of at least one year's duration from the last rituximab administration and who are appropriate candidates for therapy should receive this therapy in combination with rituximab or as rituximab monotherapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphoma excluding SLL and response to combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab
selected_rules.execute: Follow treatment with maintenance rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: For patients with indolent histology CD20-positive B-cell lymphomas, excluding SLL, who respond to treatment with combination chemotherapy and/or rituximab, this treatment should be followed by the use of maintenance rituximab.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Any patient planned for rituximab treatment
selected_rules.execute: Screen for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody before rituximab
selected_rules.source_statement: The Hematology Disease Site Group recommends that all patients be screened for surface antigen for hepatitis B (HBsAg) and for hepatitis B core antibody (HBcAb) prior to treatment with rituximab.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease but no CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
recommended_actions: Do not add rituximab to chemotherapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers R001 because the patient has HIV-related lymphoma and a CD4 count below 50/mm3. Under this rule, rituximab should not be added to chemotherapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 58/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease without CNS involvement. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team considers him a candidate for curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function. He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case remains HIV-associated, newly diagnosed, and headed toward curative-intent combination chemotherapy, but the CD4 count is just above the cutoff that would prohibit rituximab.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Automatically omit rituximab because the patient has HIV-associated lymphoma
Treat this as the low-CD4 exception and choose chemotherapy alone
Focus on the detectable viral load and assume rituximab is contraindicated
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the 'do not add rituximab' rule because the CD4 count is 58/mm3, not below 50/mm3. The safer interpretation is that the low-CD4 exclusion does not apply here; rituximab may be guideline-concordant if no other contraindications exist.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 only applies when HIV-related lymphoma is paired with CD4 <50/mm3. This vignette is close but misses that threshold.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents for treatment planning. He has no prior lymphoma therapy. His CD4 count is 34/mm3, HIV viral load is detectable, and staging shows advanced nodal disease. Brain MRI is negative, but lumbar puncture shows low-level CSF involvement without neurologic symptoms. He is otherwise fit, with ECOG performance status 1, and the oncology team is considering curative-intent combination chemotherapy. Baseline labs show normal renal and hepatic function.
