He asks whether rituximab should be included with his chemotherapy regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The vignette preserves the HIV-associated lymphoma, very low CD4 count, and planned curative-intent therapy, but adds occult CNS involvement that changes the clinical context.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Continue with the original plan of adding rituximab to chemotherapy
Apply the HIV lymphoma rule without reconsidering the new CNS disease
Ignore the CSF finding because the patient has no neurologic symptoms
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original recommendation because the case now includes CNS involvement, which makes it a different scenario than straightforward systemic HIV-related lymphoma. The safer interpretation is that this patient needs CNS-directed management and specialist review rather than simply applying the low-CD4 rituximab rule.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 was triggered in the source by HIV-related lymphoma with CD4 <50/mm3 and no CNS involvement. The added CSF disease creates a clinically different situation, so the source recommendation cannot be applied directly.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with relapsed SCLC
selected_rules.execute: Consider second-line chemotherapy only after weighing treatment-free interval, response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity, and performance status.
selected_rules.source_statement: The selection of patients for treatment with second-line therapy should be dependent on the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and the performance status of the patient.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
selected_rules.source_statement: patients who relapse three or more months following the completion of first-line chemotherapy may benefit from retreatment with the same regimen that induced their initial response. This would generally mean retreatment with etoposide-cisplatin.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient relapses three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider alternative regimens such as CAV or carboplatin plus etoposide.
selected_rules.source_statement: Alternative regimens may include cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and vincristine (CAV) or carboplatin and etoposide.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient initially responded to chemotherapy and response duration was 45 days or longer
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral topotecan is a possible alternative for patients who initially responded to chemotherapy and had a response duration of 45 days or longer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is not suitable for intravenous therapy and topotecan is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Consider oral topotecan because it is more convenient and may be an option when IV therapy is unsuitable.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is more convenient and may be a treatment option for patients not suitable for intravenous therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given orally
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 diarrhea.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: topotecan is given intravenously
selected_rules.execute: Monitor for grade 3/4 neutropenia.
selected_rules.source_statement: Oral administration is associated with a higher incidence of grade 3/4 diarrhea, whereas intravenous administration may result in a higher frequency of grade 3/4 neutropenia.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient fails to respond to first-line therapy or relapses shortly after first-line therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider enrollment in a clinical trial to help determine the optimal treatment regimen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Clinical trials are needed to determine the optimal treatment regimen.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 5 months ago and had a partial response with marked symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is just over 5 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks about options. Because he previously responded and the relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of chemotherapy, retreatment with the same regimen is being considered, along with other second-line options. Oral topotecan is also discussed as an alternative, since he responded initially and his response lasted longer than 45 days.
recommended_actions: Consider second-line chemotherapy after weighing the treatment-free interval, the extent of response to first-line therapy, residual toxicity from first-line therapy, and performance status.
Because relapse occurred more than 3 months after completion of first-line chemotherapy, consider retreatment with the same regimen that produced the initial response, generally etoposide-cisplatin.
Because he initially responded and the response duration was longer than 45 days, consider oral topotecan as an alternative second-line option.
source_case.rationale: This vignette meets the activation conditions for relapsed SCLC, relapse more than 3 months after first-line chemotherapy, and prior response lasting at least 45 days. The recommendations are limited to those specified by the triggered rules and reflect the need to balance treatment-free interval, prior response, residual toxicity, and performance status.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old man with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line etoposide-cisplatin 2 months ago and initially had a partial response with symptom improvement. He now returns with worsening cough, weight loss, and new right hilar enlargement on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. His treatment-free interval is a little under 3 months. He has mild residual peripheral neuropathy from prior cisplatin but remains ambulatory, independent in activities of daily living, and has an ECOG performance status of 1. He is interested in further treatment and asks whether the same chemotherapy can be repeated, since it worked before. The team is also discussing topotecan as a second-line option because of the earlier response. The relapse timing is the main question, but otherwise his presentation resembles a typical platinum-sensitive recurrence.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical to a platinum-sensitive relapsed SCLC case, but the chemotherapy-free interval is just under 3 months rather than 3 or more months, and the prior response lasted only about 40 days rather than 45 days.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Retreat with etoposide-cisplatin as if this were a platinum-sensitive relapse
Offer oral topotecan based on the prior response alone
Proceed as though the timing automatically supports standard second-line rechallenge
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is relapsed SCLC, but the interval since first-line chemotherapy is too short to meet the rule supporting retreatment with the same regimen, and the prior response duration does not reach the threshold for oral topotecan. A safer interpretation is that this is a more treatment-resistant recurrence, so standard rechallenge is not guideline-concordant and other non-specified second-line approaches or trial consideration would be more appropriate to discuss.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 requires relapse three or more months after completion of first-line chemotherapy; this case is just under that threshold. R004 requires an initial response lasting 45 days or longer; this case falls short. The superficial similarity is high, but the timing invalidates both recommendations.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer completed first-line carboplatin-etoposide 6 months ago and had a partial response with marked improvement in dyspnea. She now presents with new liver lesions and recurrent mediastinal adenopathy on CT, consistent with relapsed disease. Her performance status is ECOG 2 because of increasing fatigue, and she has persistent grade 2 hearing loss from prior platinum therapy, though she remains self-caring at home. She asks about additional treatment. Because the relapse occurred well beyond 3 months after chemotherapy, the oncologist wonders whether the original platinum-based regimen could be repeated. Oral topotecan is also mentioned because she did respond initially and the response lasted more than 45 days. The question is complicated by her residual toxicities and borderline functional status.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Looks like a standard relapsed SCLC scenario with a long treatment-free interval and prior response, but the patient has meaningful residual platinum toxicity and is not fully fit, making the choice of second-line therapy less straightforward than the source case.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Automatically repeat carboplatin-etoposide because the relapse is after 3 months
Select oral topotecan without considering her residual toxicities and performance status
Treat her as an uncomplicated candidate for routine second-line chemotherapy
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although this is relapsed SCLC after a longer interval, the patient has ECOG 2 and persistent platinum-related hearing loss, so the guideline’s emphasis on weighing residual toxicity and performance status becomes unfavorable for routine reuse of the same regimen. Her prior response also does not by itself make oral topotecan automatically appropriate; the case no longer cleanly matches the ideal selection criteria for either recommendation. A safer interpretation is to reassess fitness carefully and consider alternative non-rechallenge strategies or trial options rather than applying the source recommendation reflexively.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated because the case is no longer a straightforward candidate for second-line treatment selection based on favorable functional status and limited residual toxicity. R002 is weakened by the presence of clinically relevant residual toxicity and poorer performance status despite the long interval. R004 is not a clean fit because the prior response criterion alone is insufficient when the overall clinical picture is less suitable. The case is intentionally close to the source but not guideline-concordant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with inoperable, advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent systemic treatment strategies should be part of an informed discussion of treatment options for this group of patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer wants a break from treatment
selected_rules.execute: Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Patients who want a break from treatment can be reassured that intermittent strategies of administering first-line therapy are a reasonable alternative to continuous administration.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Meta-analysis demonstrates no clinically significant survival difference between the continuous and intermittent chemotherapy strategies (HR, 1.02; 95%CI, 0.95-1.10, p=0.62).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is considering intermittent versus continuous first-line systemic therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Intermittent strategies of administering first-line systemic therapies to patients with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC) do not result in a statistically significant reduction in overall survival and either improve or maintain quality of life compared to continuous administration of therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult patient with unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer is receiving intermittent systemic therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval
selected_rules.execute: Continue best supportive care
selected_rules.source_statement: During maintenance therapy or a chemotherapy-free interval, best supportive care should be continued for patients.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment as a reasonable guideline to consider
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Intermittent strategy is being used for unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer
selected_rules.execute: Monitor with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression. These represent reasonable guidelines to consider when using an intermittent strategy, but adaptation of a strategy to individual circumstances should always be considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Disease progresses during the intermittent phase
selected_rules.execute: Reintroduce the induction chemotherapy at disease progression
selected_rules.source_statement: All but one of the intermittent strategies offered 12 to 18 weeks of induction treatment and were monitored with imaging at least every 8 to 12 weeks during the intermittent phase of treatment, with reintroduction of the induction chemotherapy at disease progression.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases, good performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. After reading about treatment side effects, he says he would prefer a plan that includes treatment breaks if possible, because he wants to spend more time at home and maintain his energy for family activities. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and explains that intermittent first-line systemic treatment is also a reasonable option to consider. The discussion includes the possibility that intermittent therapy may preserve quality of life without a statistically significant survival disadvantage compared with continuous therapy. The patient agrees that an intermittent approach fits his goals if disease monitoring is built in.
recommended_actions: Discuss intermittent systemic treatment strategies as a reasonable alternative to continuous administration as part of informed treatment decision-making.
Reassure the patient that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable alternative to continuous therapy.
Use intermittent therapy as an option because it does not show a statistically significant reduction in overall survival compared with continuous therapy.
Consider intermittent therapy because it may improve or maintain quality of life compared with continuous therapy.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches an adult with inoperable advanced stage IV colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy, and he also explicitly wants a break from treatment. The selected rules support discussing and reassuring about intermittent therapy, noting no statistically significant overall survival reduction and potential quality-of-life benefit versus continuous therapy.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma returns to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has biopsy-proven liver and lung metastases, excellent performance status, and no prior chemotherapy. He says he would prefer a plan with treatment breaks so he can stay active with his family and avoid feeling worn down continuously. His oncologist reviews standard continuous therapy and asks about his goals, but also notes that the disease is now considered potentially resectable after a favorable response to staging, with surgery being discussed at a multidisciplinary conference. The patient still asks whether intermittent therapy could be an option if he needs systemic treatment first. The team explains that treatment scheduling will depend on the surgical plan and that they need to clarify intent before choosing a regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely resembles the source case: an older man with stage IV colorectal cancer, good functional status, no prior chemotherapy, and a desire for treatment breaks. The subtle difference is that the metastases are now considered potentially resectable, shifting the situation away from unresectable/inoperable first-line metastatic management.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Discuss intermittent first-line systemic therapy as a standard option right away
Reassure him that intermittent therapy is a guideline-supported alternative to continuous therapy
Present the survival and quality-of-life data for intermittent versus continuous therapy as directly applicable
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the intermittent-therapy recommendation because the disease is no longer clearly in the unresectable/inoperable first-line metastatic setting. Before applying intermittent-vs-continuous metastatic chemotherapy guidance, the treatment intent and surgical plan must be clarified.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule set applies to adult patients with unresectable or inoperable metastatic colorectal cancer being considered for first-line systemic therapy. A potentially resectable metastatic presentation changes the clinical context and makes the intermittent-therapy recommendation non-applicable as stated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old man with inoperable stage IV colorectal adenocarcinoma presents to discuss first-line systemic therapy. He has liver and lung metastases and has not received chemotherapy before. He remains active, but over the last several weeks he has developed worsening abdominal pain, reduced oral intake, and mild jaundice from progressive liver involvement. He says he would like treatment breaks if possible, because he wants more time at home with his family. His oncologist discusses continuous chemotherapy and mentions that intermittent therapy is sometimes considered in metastatic disease, but also notes that because his bilirubin is rising and symptoms are progressing quickly, they are not yet in a position to plan a treatment holiday strategy safely. The team emphasizes the need to stabilize his current disease burden and organ function first.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This remains an older man with unresectable stage IV colorectal cancer, no prior chemotherapy, and a preference for breaks from treatment. The subtle mismatch is that he is now clinically deteriorating with worsening symptoms and hepatic dysfunction, making a treatment-break approach inappropriate as a straightforward first-line strategy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Reassure him that intermittent first-line therapy is a reasonable option
Recommend an intermittent schedule because he prefers time at home
Apply the survival and quality-of-life data for intermittent therapy without considering current instability
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although the overall diagnosis is similar, this vignette is not guideline-concordant for recommending intermittent first-line therapy as a reasonable alternative in the usual sense because the patient has active clinical deterioration and rising bilirubin. A safer interpretation is that he needs urgent reassessment and stabilization before any elective intermittent strategy is considered.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation is intended for patients being considered for first-line systemic therapy in unresectable metastatic colorectal cancer who are suitable for that strategy. Rapid symptom progression and hepatic dysfunction make the situation more urgent and less suitable for a planned chemotherapy-free interval approach.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman has never been sexually active
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is younger than 21 years old
selected_rules.execute: Do not screen routinely.
selected_rules.source_statement: the authors of this guideline have concluded that the harms of screening women under 21 years of age significantly outweigh the benefits.

selected_rules.activation_condition: organized screening program with adequate database infrastructure
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV DNA testing of cervical cells for primary cervical screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV DNA testing of cells collected from the cervix is recommended for primary cervical screening.

selected_rules.activation_condition: positive HPV DNA test result
selected_rules.execute: Perform cytology screening as triage rather than using cytology as the primary screen.
selected_rules.source_statement: Cytology screening, which was recommended for primary screening in the previous version of this guideline, is now recommended only in the event of a positive HPV DNA test result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 years of age or older
selected_rules.execute: Use HPV-based primary screening.
selected_rules.source_statement: the primary recommendations presented in Part 1 of this guideline are for HPVbased testing for women 30 years of age and over.

selected_rules.activation_condition: woman is 30 to 65 years old and initial HPV test is negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV DNA testing at five-year intervals.
selected_rules.source_statement: For women aged 30-65, HPV DNA testing is to occur at five-year intervals after an initial negative result.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV test is positive
selected_rules.execute: Assess the result with cytology testing and do not refer directly to colposcopy.
selected_rules.source_statement: HPV-positive tests should be assessed with cytology testing and not referred directly to colposcopy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HPV positive and cytology negative
selected_rules.execute: Repeat HPV testing after one year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Repeat HPV testing for results of HPV positive/cytology negative should be conducted after one year.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. After discussing her preventive care questions, she specifically asks whether cervical cancer screening is needed now.
recommended_actions: Do not perform cervical screening.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the rule that women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening. The recommended action is therefore to avoid cervical screening in this patient.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks about cervical cancer screening. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior abnormal Pap results. She is otherwise healthy and not immunocompromised. Her only change from a typical preventive visit is that she mentions she is planning to become sexually active later this year and wonders if she should have screening now “just to be safe.”
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still centers on a healthy 27-year-old woman asking about cervical screening, but the key difference is that she is about to initiate sexual activity rather than having a history of never being sexually active as a stable status relevant to the recommendation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise no cervical screening solely because she has not yet been sexually active today.
Document that cervical cancer screening is unnecessary regardless of upcoming sexual activity.
Reassure her without discussing that screening recommendations will change once she becomes sexually active or based on age-based screening guidance.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a clean match to the rule that women who have never been sexually active do not require cervical screening, because the vignette introduces imminent change in sexual exposure and shifts the counseling context. The safest interpretation is to avoid treating this as a definitive exemption based only on current history and instead apply the broader age- and risk-based screening framework at the appropriate time.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation applies to women who have never been sexually active; adding imminent planned sexual activity makes the situation less clearly covered by that exemption and prevents a simplistic 'no screening ever' conclusion.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 27-year-old woman presents for a routine primary care visit and asks whether she should have a Pap test. She reports that she has never been sexually active and has no history of genital symptoms, abnormal bleeding, or prior cervical screening. She is otherwise healthy and has no immunocompromising conditions. On review of records, however, she had a brief sexual relationship two years ago that included vaginal intercourse, though she does not recall discussing it previously and says she has not been sexually active since. She asks whether screening is needed now.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The encounter is almost identical to the source case, but the subtle record review reveals prior sexual activity, which breaks the 'never been sexually active' condition.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend no cervical screening because she is currently not sexually active.
Assume her self-report alone satisfies the exemption and defer screening indefinitely.
Dismiss the old history as irrelevant and tell her she needs no cervical cancer screening.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for the 'never sexually active' no-screening recommendation because the chart documents prior vaginal intercourse. The safer interpretation is that she does not meet the exemption and should be managed using standard age-appropriate cervical screening guidance.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires that the woman has never been sexually active. A prior episode of vaginal intercourse invalidates that criterion, even if she is not currently sexually active.

selected_rules.activation_condition: inflammatory breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
selected_rules.source_statement: BCS is not advised in inflammatory breast cancer because the extent of tumour involvement cannot be reliably ascertained.

selected_rules.activation_condition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is being considered both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform SLNB both before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: This suggests that SLNB should not be performed both before and after NACT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: cases being discussed for treatment planning
selected_rules.execute: Discuss cases in a multidisciplinary case conference.
selected_rules.source_statement: Communication between oncologists, surgeons, radiologists, and pathologists is essential. A multidisciplinary case conference is the recommended forum for discussion of cases.

selected_rules.activation_condition: specimen submitted after neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Indicate any prior neoadjuvant therapy on the pathology specimen submission.
selected_rules.source_statement: Any prior use of neoadjuvant therapy should be indicated when specimens are submitted for pathologic examination.

selected_rules.activation_condition: before starting neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Insert surgical clips marking the original pretreatment tumour location.
selected_rules.source_statement: It is recommended that surgical clips marking the original (pretreatment) tumour location be inserted before administration of neoadjuvant therapy.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer and surgery is being planned after response to neoadjuvant therapy
selected_rules.execute: Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: For most patients with LABC, mastectomy should be considered to be the standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient strongly desires breast-conserving surgery
selected_rules.execute: Perform wide excision of the remaining tumour in the original tumour bed plus radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Wide excision of the remaining tumour in the region of the original pre-neoadjuvant treatment tumour bed plus RT is recommended for patients with LABC who strongly desire BCS.

selected_rules.activation_condition: female patient with locally advanced breast cancer after mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Give postoperative radiotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Radiotherapy following mastectomy is recommended for patients with LABC.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving more than one-third of the breast, consistent with inflammatory breast cancer. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet.
recommended_actions: Do not advise breast-conserving surgery.
Consider mastectomy as the standard of care for most patients with locally advanced breast cancer after response to neoadjuvant therapy.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes inflammatory breast cancer, which triggers the recommendation against breast-conserving surgery because tumour extent cannot be reliably ascertained. It also includes locally advanced breast cancer being managed with neoadjuvant therapy and later surgery planning, which supports considering mastectomy as standard of care for most such patients.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 7 cm left breast mass, peau d’orange, and erythema involving most of the upper outer quadrant, but not the full one-third of the breast. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. She is being evaluated at diagnosis for local treatment options, and she asks whether she can have lumpectomy because she hopes to avoid mastectomy. The team is also planning neoadjuvant therapy, with surgery to be reconsidered afterward depending on response. No surgery has been performed yet. Imaging shows a large tumor, but the skin findings are limited and the breast surgeon notes that this does not meet criteria for inflammatory breast cancer.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case preserves the same age, tumor size, neoadjuvant workflow, and desire to avoid mastectomy, but the skin involvement is limited and no longer fits inflammatory breast cancer.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise against breast-conserving surgery as if this were inflammatory breast cancer.
Proceed assuming mastectomy is automatically required for all locally advanced presentations.
Document the case as inflammatory breast cancer without further clarification.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the inflammatory breast cancer rule because the erythema and peau d’orange do not involve more than one-third of the breast. The safer interpretation is locally advanced breast cancer without inflammatory features; local therapy should be individualized after neoadjuvant treatment, rather than automatically excluding breast-conserving options on the basis of inflammatory disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation against breast-conserving surgery is triggered by inflammatory breast cancer. Here, the skin involvement is deliberately borderline but falls short of that definition, so the specific no-BCS inflammatory rule does not apply. Because the case still resembles locally advanced disease, a reader might overgeneralize to mandatory mastectomy, but the inflammatory-specific prohibition is invalidated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 57-year-old woman is diagnosed with a 6.5 cm left breast invasive ductal carcinoma with biopsy-proven axillary node involvement. She is discussed at a multidisciplinary conference and starts neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Before treatment, the interventional radiologist places a clip in the tumor bed. At surgery planning, the patient says she strongly wants to preserve her breast if possible, and the surgeon asks whether a wide excision of the residual tumor bed plus radiotherapy would be appropriate. Post-treatment imaging shows a near-complete response, but the residual abnormality is still multifocal within the original breast quadrant. The team is deciding between breast-conserving surgery and mastectomy.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This case keeps the same locally advanced setting, neoadjuvant approach, clip placement, and desire to avoid mastectomy, but the residual disease is multifocal rather than a single resectable focus in the original tumor bed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend wide excision of the original tumor bed plus radiotherapy as if breast-conserving surgery were straightforward.
Treat her preference alone as sufficient to proceed with BCS.
Ignore the multifocal residual disease and offer standard lumpectomy planning.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the breast-conserving surgery recommendation because the residual disease is multifocal, making a single wide excision of the original tumor bed unlikely to be appropriate. The safer interpretation is that breast-conserving surgery is not clearly supported here, and mastectomy or further surgical reassessment should be considered based on extent of residual disease and feasibility of complete excision.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule for LABC patients who strongly desire BCS assumes a remaining tumor that can be widely excised in the original bed. Multifocal residual disease breaks that condition, so the recommendation does not cleanly apply. The case remains similar enough that a superficial reader might still choose lumpectomy, but the anatomic requirement for BCS is no longer met.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR2 or greater, including refractory or relapsed disease
selected_rules.execute: Perform allo-SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Allo-SCT is recommended in CR2 or greater (refractory or relapsed).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in first complete remission (CR1) and eligible for allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) as a treatment option
selected_rules.source_statement: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT) is an option for adult patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in first complete remission (CR1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is deemed unsuitable for standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC)
selected_rules.execute: Use reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) for SCT
selected_rules.source_statement: Reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) is an option for patients with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in remission when they are deemed unsuitable for the standard myeloablative conditioning (MAC) regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in remission who is ineligible for MAC allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC as an effective therapeutic option
selected_rules.source_statement: Based on the evidence, the members of the Working Group have determined that RIC could be an effective therapeutic option for patients with ALL who are ineligible for MAC allo-SCT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient with high risk of treatment-related mortality from MAC
selected_rules.execute: Consider RIC instead of MAC
selected_rules.source_statement: The systematic review (4) stated that RIC may be a potential therapeutic option in patients with high risk of treatment-related mortality (TRM) associated with MAC regimens, as there was a lack of overall survival benefit of MAC over RIC regimens.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) after allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Use a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: Post-transplant use of a BCR-ABL tyrosine-kinase inhibitor (TKI) in patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL (Ph+ ALL) is a reasonable option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with Ph+ ALL undergoing allo-SCT
selected_rules.execute: Consider TKI therapy pre- and/or post-transplant
selected_rules.source_statement: The consensus is that TKI therapy is useful pre and/or posttransplant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult ALL patient in CR1 or later who lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor
selected_rules.execute: Consider haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (haplo-SCT)
selected_rules.source_statement: Haploidentical hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation (haplo-SCT) for patients with ALL in CR1 or later who lack a suitable related or unrelated donor is a reasonable option.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case meets criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
recommended_actions: Perform allogeneic stem cell transplantation (allo-SCT).
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches adult ALL in CR2 after relapse, which directly triggers the recommendation for allo-SCT. No additional rules are activated because the patient is not described as Ph+ ALL, unsuitable for myeloablative conditioning, or lacking a suitable donor.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a documented second complete remission (CR2) with no detectable blasts on marrow exam. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, but the donor search found no HLA-matched sibling and no suitably matched unrelated donor. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. Because he is an adult patient with ALL in CR2 after relapse, the case appears to meet criteria for transplant in the recurrence setting.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The relapse timing, CR2 status, and transplant evaluation are unchanged, but the key donor availability detail has shifted from a matched sibling donor to no suitable related or unrelated donor.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed directly with standard allogeneic stem cell transplantation using the sibling donor
Document the case as guideline-concordant for allo-SCT in CR2 without further donor consideration
Overlook the missing donor and continue transplant planning as if a matched donor were available
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not concordant with the CR2 allo-SCT recommendation as written because the vignette no longer includes a suitable donor. The safer interpretation is that he still has transplant-intent disease, but alternate donor strategies must be considered rather than assuming the standard matched-donor allo-SCT pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires adult ALL in CR2 or greater, which is still present, but the original recommendation was applied in the context of an available matched sibling donor. R008 becomes relevant because the patient lacks a suitable related or unrelated donor; thus the case shifts away from straightforward matched-donor allo-SCT and toward consideration of haploidentical transplantation or other donor-access strategies.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia initially achieved remission after induction and consolidation but relapsed 14 months later. He received salvage chemotherapy and now has a marrow showing persistent low-level lymphoblasts, with the most recent assessment documenting approximately 3% blasts rather than a full second remission. He is otherwise fit, without major cardiac, renal, or pulmonary comorbidities, and he has an HLA-matched sibling donor available. The transplant team is evaluating next steps for durable disease control. The overall situation still resembles post-relapse transplant planning, but the marrow response has not fully cleared.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still looks like a relapsed adult ALL case with a donor and transplant consultation, but the subtle change is that he is not actually in CR2 because measurable blasts remain.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend allogeneic stem cell transplantation immediately as if he were in CR2
Label the case as relapse in second remission and proceed to transplant planning without reassessing disease status
Treat the residual blasts as clinically insignificant and ignore remission criteria
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the CR2 transplant recommendation because the patient does not meet CR2 criteria; persistent marrow blasts indicate incomplete response. A safer interpretation is that additional disease control or restaging is needed before applying the CR2 allo-SCT recommendation.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is activated only for adult ALL patients in CR2 or greater after relapse. Here, the marrow still shows residual disease, so the key condition is not satisfied. The presence of a donor and prior relapse makes the case deceptively similar, but the remission status is the decisive mismatch.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient intends postoperative radiation and has lymph node positive early breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, or locally advanced breast cancer
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: early breast cancer who are lymph node positive, inflammatory breast cancer or locally advanced breast cancer who will require postoperative RT.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Invasive tumor involves the skin or nipple-areolar complex clinically
selected_rules.execute: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: SSM, NSM, and ASM are not recommended for women intending to receive postoperative radiation with: any clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex (NAC) involvement by invasive tumour

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient has Paget disease of the breast or a retroareolar tumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not perform nipple-sparing or areola-sparing mastectomy
selected_rules.source_statement: NSM and ASM are not recommended for women with Paget disease of the breast or women with a retroareolar tumour.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Postmastectomy breast reconstruction has been performed
selected_rules.execute: Do not use surveillance mammography routinely in the reconstructed breast
selected_rules.source_statement: There is insufficient evidence to support the use of postmastectomy surveillance mammography in the reconstructed breast.

selected_rules.activation_condition: At the time mastectomy is offered for therapeutic mastectomy
selected_rules.execute: Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction
selected_rules.source_statement: The discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction should be initiated at the time that mastectomy is offered by the general surgeon.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for DCIS
selected_rules.execute: Perform preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), preoperative evaluation with a general surgeon and a plastic surgeon should be performed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate breast reconstruction is being sought for early stage breast cancer and adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be needed
selected_rules.execute: Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in preoperative evaluation, either by formal consultation or multidisciplinary cancer conference
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate breast reconstruction for early stage breast cancer who will potentially require adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy (RT), a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist should be included in preoperative evaluation, either through a formal consultation or by a multidisciplinary cancer conference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Immediate reconstruction is being planned
selected_rules.execute: Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations
selected_rules.source_statement: For women seeking immediate reconstruction, there should be adequate preoperative imaging of the breasts, aligning with existing guideline recommendations.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
recommended_actions: Do not use skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, or areola-sparing mastectomy because she intends to receive postoperative radiation and has lymph node-positive early breast cancer.
Initiate discussion of immediate or delayed breast reconstruction at the time mastectomy is offered.
Include a medical oncologist and/or radiation oncologist in the preoperative evaluation through formal consultation or a multidisciplinary cancer conference.
Obtain adequate preoperative breast imaging according to existing guideline recommendations.
source_case.rationale: R001 is triggered because the patient has lymph node-positive early breast cancer and will receive postoperative radiation, making skin-sparing, nipple-sparing, and areola-sparing mastectomy not recommended. R005 applies because mastectomy is being offered, so reconstruction should be discussed. R007 applies because immediate reconstruction is being considered and adjuvant radiotherapy is likely, so oncology input should be included preoperatively. R008 applies because immediate reconstruction is being planned and adequate preoperative breast imaging is required.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma returns to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement, but her staging workup also reveals a small contralateral lung nodule that is being followed and has not been characterized. The breast surgeon recommends mastectomy, and the patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and may need postoperative radiation, the team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has already had diagnostic mammography and breast MRI for local staging. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical and reconstructive planning, but the final treatment sequence is still being determined pending workup of the lung lesion.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case closely mirrors a node-positive breast cancer patient considering immediate reconstruction with preoperative imaging and multidisciplinary planning, but the presence of an indeterminate distant lung nodule makes the cancer no longer clearly an early-stage, nonmetastatic setting for the original postoperative-radiation reconstruction guidance.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with skin-sparing or nipple-sparing mastectomy because the breast findings are otherwise favorable.
Treat the patient as a routine immediate reconstruction candidate and finalize plastics consultation without clarifying the lung nodule.
Assume postoperative radiation planning is straightforward despite incomplete metastatic evaluation.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the original rule because the disease is not clearly confined to early-stage breast cancer; the indeterminate lung lesion means metastatic status is unresolved. Safer interpretation is to complete staging and clarify intent before applying reconstruction timing or mastectomy-sparing decisions.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires node-positive early breast cancer with intended postoperative radiation; the unresolved lung nodule introduces possible metastatic disease, so the condition is not clearly met. R007 is also not cleanly met because immediate reconstruction planning should be coordinated in a clearly early-stage setting where adjuvant therapy needs are being assessed; staging uncertainty should be resolved first.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 52-year-old woman with a newly diagnosed left breast invasive ductal carcinoma presents to discuss surgery. Imaging shows a 4.2 cm tumor with biopsy-proven axillary lymph node involvement, and the surgeon recommends mastectomy. The patient is interested in immediate reconstruction if feasible. Because she is node-positive and is expected to receive postoperative radiation, the surgical team reviews reconstruction options carefully. On exam, there is no clinical skin or nipple-areolar complex involvement, and she has no history of Paget disease. She has had diagnostic mammography, but breast MRI has not yet been completed because she has a documented iodinated contrast allergy and is waiting on a noncontrast protocol discussion. A multidisciplinary visit is arranged to coordinate surgical, reconstructive, and radiation planning before finalizing the operative approach.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This is nearly identical to the source vignette, including node-positive disease, anticipated radiation, and reconstruction planning, but the preoperative breast imaging is incomplete because the MRI component has not yet been obtained.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Approve immediate reconstruction as fully worked up.
Move straight to mastectomy without obtaining the missing preoperative breast imaging.
Document that imaging requirements are already satisfied because mammography was completed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for the imaging recommendation because adequate preoperative breast imaging has not yet been completed. The safer interpretation is to finish the recommended breast imaging workup before proceeding with immediate reconstruction planning.
challenging_examples.rationale: R008 requires adequate preoperative breast imaging before immediate reconstruction is planned. Here, only diagnostic mammography is complete and the MRI has not been performed, so the imaging workup is incomplete. The reconstruction discussion may still occur, but the case does not satisfy the imaging prerequisite.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering limited ACP implementation
selected_rules.execute: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
selected_rules.source_statement: Addressing only one, or a few, of these elements creates the risks of leaving critical gaps that could prevent positive impacts on quality patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When using education alone to improve ACP care
selected_rules.execute: Do not expect education alone to improve care or patient experience without supporting system changes.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, high-quality education, although essential, on its own is unlikely to improve care or the patient experience in the absence of supporting systemic changes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering whether pediatric ACP causes distress
selected_rules.execute: Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: Concerns that ACP discussions will cause distress in children and parents are not supported by the evidence.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When considering ACP or GoCD tools for practice
selected_rules.execute: Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
selected_rules.source_statement: Outcome evidence for ACP suggests the greatest impact is likely to be made by a system-wide approach to integrating ACP into practice, one that maximizes clinical skills of all interprofessional team members.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When developing ACP tools or processes
selected_rules.execute: Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
selected_rules.source_statement: Considering these emerging themes from the evidence suggests that tool development should focus on enabling healthcare providers to facilitate values-based discussions.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When evaluating ACP tools
selected_rules.execute: Use values-based outcome measures to assess impact on patient care.
selected_rules.source_statement: Future studies with corresponding valuesbased outcome measures are needed to evaluate the impact on patient care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When implementing ACP programs
selected_rules.execute: Include community engagement, professional education, standard practices, quality improvement monitoring, supporting policies, and technology infrastructure.
selected_rules.source_statement: A comprehensive approach to ACP includes community engagement, professional education, development of standard practices, monitoring of these practices through Quality Improvement initiatives, supporting policies and practices, and a technology infrastructures.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When planning ACP implementation across care settings
selected_rules.execute: Apply ACP across public health and healthcare sectors with system-level coordination and planning.
selected_rules.source_statement: This approach must be applied across public health and healthcare sectors to support the exchange of clinical information and will involve system-level coordination and planning.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been discussed inconsistently across visits: one clinician focused mainly on code status, while another gave a pamphlet about end-of-life decisions. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured conversation about these values. His daughter asks whether these discussions might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient ACP workflow and realizes that a few isolated steps have not changed care quality in the past.
recommended_actions: Do not rely on only one or a few ACP elements to achieve meaningful patient care impact.
Do not assume ACP discussions increase distress in children or parents.
Use a system-wide approach to integrate ACP into routine practice and maximize interprofessional team skills.
Focus tool development on enabling values-based discussions rather than treatment-focused discussions.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes limited, fragmented ACP implementation, which triggers the warning against relying on only a few ACP elements. It also includes concern that ACP discussions may distress the patient and family, matching the rule that evidence does not support assuming increased distress. The team is planning broader ACP workflow changes, which supports a system-wide approach, and the patient’s stated priorities point to values-based rather than treatment-only discussions.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team notes that advance care planning has been addressed in a piecemeal way: one visit focused on completing a code-status form, and another included a brief handout about hospice services, but no broader conversation about the patient’s goals has occurred. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but he has not had a structured discussion about those values. His daughter asks whether these conversations might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team is considering a new outpatient workflow, but the current proposal would only add a single scripted ACP checklist at discharge rather than changing clinic processes or team roles.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves metastatic lung cancer, fragmented ACP, and a proposed workflow change, but the proposed intervention is narrowed to one discharge checklist rather than a meaningful ACP implementation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend the single ACP checklist as sufficient to improve care quality.
Treat the brief code-status discussion and handout as adequate advance care planning.
Assume the existing limited ACP elements are enough to achieve meaningful patient impact.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for relying on only one or a few ACP elements. The safer interpretation is that the current plan remains too narrow and risks leaving major gaps in values-based planning; a broader, system-level ACP approach is needed.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the same clinical context but changes the workflow detail so the proposed intervention is only a single discharge checklist. That invalidates the recommendation against depending on a few isolated ACP elements.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 68-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer is admitted for progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His oncology team has tried to improve advance care planning, but the only intervention in place so far is a short educational session for clinicians on how to complete resuscitation forms; there are no standardized clinic prompts, role changes, or follow-up processes. The patient says he wants care that fits his priorities—staying alert enough to talk with family and avoiding prolonged ICU treatment if recovery is unlikely—but those values have not yet been explored in a structured way. His daughter asks whether discussing prognosis and future care might upset him, since he becomes anxious when illness is mentioned. The team wonders whether the brief teaching session alone will be enough to improve patient experience and documentation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This keeps the same diagnosis, family concern, and ACP setting, but the key change is that the implementation is limited to education alone without system supports.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Assume the clinician education session will be enough to improve ACP quality and patient experience.
Rely on teaching staff how to complete forms without adding workflow changes.
Proceed as if no additional system redesign is needed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because education alone is unlikely to improve ACP care or the patient experience without supporting system changes. The safer interpretation is that the team should not expect meaningful impact from training in isolation and should add process redesign or other structural supports.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette matches the original context but specifies that the only intervention is education, which directly conflicts with the recommendation that education alone is insufficient without systemic changes.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria
selected_rules.execute: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
selected_rules.source_statement: Ineligible patients include those who have received >2 prior anticancer regimens or who had refractory disease, patients with a history of bowel obstruction (including subocclusive disease) related to underlying disease, a history of abdominal fistula, gastrointestinal perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess, or evidence of rectosigmoid involvement by pelvic examination, bowel involvement on computed tomography, or clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Assess each patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference; recognize systemic therapy is not curative.
selected_rules.source_statement: Systemic therapy for recurrent ovarian cancer is not curative. As such, it is recognized that, to determine the optimal therapy, each patient needs to be assessed individually in terms of recurrence, sensitivity to platinum, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all patients with recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer participation in clinical trials if appropriate.
selected_rules.source_statement: All patients should be offered the opportunity to participate in clinical trials, if appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and a single platinum agent is not being considered because of toxicity or allergy
selected_rules.execute: Use monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin.
selected_rules.source_statement: If a single platinum agent is not being considered (e.g., because of toxicity or allergy), then monotherapy with paclitaxel, topotecan, or pegylated liposomal doxorubicin is a reasonable treatment option.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer with known BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, somatic or germline, after platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease
selected_rules.execute: Use PARP inhibitor maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy.
selected_rules.source_statement: PARP inhibitors are recommended for patients with known BRCA 1 or 2 mutation (somatic and germline) as maintenance treatment post platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrent disease.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Set treatment goals to improving quality of life by extending symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or prolonging life if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Lower levels of response to treatment are expected for this group; therefore, the goals of treatment should be to improve patient's quality of life by extending the symptom-free interval, reducing symptom intensity, increasing progression-free interval, or if possible, prolonging life.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-refractory or platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Use single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, and gemcitabine have demonstrated activity in this patient population and are reasonable treatment options.

selected_rules.activation_condition: platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer
selected_rules.execute: Offer chemotherapy with biologics after discussing the safety profile.
selected_rules.source_statement: Women with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer should be offered chemotherapy with biologics after a discussion concerning the safety profile

source_case.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with symptomatic recurrence 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens and now has increasing abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and a CT scan showing peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. Her disease is therefore platinum-resistant and she is not eligible for the AURELIA trial criteria because she has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. She asks about the next treatment steps and whether the goal is cure.
recommended_actions: Do not apply the bevacizumab plus chemotherapy option described for AURELIA-eligible patients.
Set treatment goals around improving quality of life, symptom control, and prolonging progression-free interval or life if possible.
Consider single-agent paclitaxel, topotecan, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or gemcitabine as reasonable treatment options.
Assess the patient individually for recurrence status, platinum sensitivity, toxicity, ease of administration, and patient preference, recognizing that systemic therapy is not curative.
Offer participation in a clinical trial if appropriate.
source_case.rationale: This vignette matches platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer and specifically excludes AURELIA eligibility because the patient has had more than two prior anticancer regimens. That triggers the recommendation not to use the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option for AURELIA-eligible patients. Platinum-resistant disease also supports setting palliative goals and using one of the listed single-agent chemotherapy options. Because she has recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer, individualized treatment assessment and trial consideration are also recommended.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer returns with increasing abdominal discomfort and early satiety 5 months after finishing her last platinum-based regimen. She has had only two prior anticancer regimens in total, and CT imaging shows diffuse peritoneal progression without bowel obstruction. On pelvic exam there is no rectosigmoid involvement, no fistula history, and no intra-abdominal abscess. Her disease remains platinum-resistant, and she asks what treatment comes next. Because she seems to fit the general profile of recurrent ovarian cancer, the team is considering the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy approach used in AURELIA-eligible patients.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The recurrence timing, platinum resistance, symptoms, and peritoneal progression closely mirror the source case, but the prior treatment burden is reduced to exactly two prior regimens, making the AURELIA exclusion no longer apply on that basis alone.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start bevacizumab plus chemotherapy as if she were AURELIA-eligible.
Treat the case as a straightforward candidate for the bevacizumab-based recurrent ovarian cancer pathway.
Overlook the need to verify all AURELIA exclusion criteria before choosing the regimen.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer, but it is not clearly discordant with the AURELIA criteria on the basis of prior therapy because she has had only two prior regimens. The safe interpretation is that eligibility must be reassessed against the full trial-style exclusions and disease features before using bevacizumab plus chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated because the source rule excludes patients with more than two prior anticancer regimens; this patient has exactly two, so that specific exclusion is not met.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 64-year-old woman with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer presents with worsening abdominal discomfort and early satiety 5 months after completing her last platinum-based regimen. She has received three prior anticancer regimens, and CT shows progressive peritoneal disease. However, she also reports intermittent crampy abdominal pain and constipation, and the radiology report notes a mildly distended small-bowel loop with a transition point suggesting a low-grade partial bowel obstruction. She asks whether the next step should include bevacizumab with chemotherapy because she is still able to eat and has not required surgery.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting remains platinum-resistant recurrent ovarian cancer with peritoneal progression, but a subtle bowel obstruction signal is added, which makes the bevacizumab-containing option inappropriate even though the overall scenario looks nearly identical.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Use bevacizumab plus chemotherapy for recurrent disease.
Proceed as though she were an AURELIA-eligible patient.
Ignore the obstruction symptoms because she is not yet completely obstructed.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the bevacizumab-plus-chemotherapy option because a history or evidence of bowel obstruction is an exclusion. The safer interpretation is to avoid bevacizumab and consider non-bevacizumab palliative systemic options and symptom-directed management.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated because the rule excludes patients with clinical symptoms of bowel obstruction or bowel involvement/obstructive disease; this vignette adds a partial obstruction feature.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer excluding sarcomas and squamous cell carcinomas
selected_rules.execute: use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Combination chemotherapy is favoured over single agent chemotherapy because of higher response rates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: Paclitaxel in combination with cisplatin/doxorubicin chemotherapy improves both response rate and median survival; however, the use of this three-drug combination is associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer and minimal symptoms or non-life threatening disease
selected_rules.execute: consider hormonal therapy
selected_rules.source_statement: Hormonal therapy may be a therapeutic option for those patients with minimal symptoms or non-life threatening advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer when choosing between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and doxorubicin/cisplatin
selected_rules.execute: weigh greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The decision to use the three-drug combination, consisting of cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel, should be made with consideration of both the greater toxicity and the three-month increase in median survival time in comparison with the two-drug doxorubicin/cisplatin regimen.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity
selected_rules.source_statement: However, recent data suggest no benefit to the three-drug combination in terms of recurrence-free survival and was associated with increased toxicity.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%.

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%
selected_rules.source_statement: For double-agent chemotherapy, randomized trials of doxorubicin/cisplatin reported response rates ranging from 28-45%, other agents in combination with doxorubicin reported response rates of 30% (cyclophosphamide) and 43% (paclitaxel).

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult patient with advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer
selected_rules.execute: consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates
selected_rules.source_statement: Single-agent chemotherapy has reported response rates as follows: doxorubicin 17-27% and platinum agents 21%.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with progressive vaginal bleeding, pelvic discomfort, and new pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT, consistent with recurrent advanced disease. She remains ambulatory and has good renal function and performance status, but she is seeking treatment that offers the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncology team discusses systemic therapy options, including single-agent and combination chemotherapy. Because her disease is advanced/recurrent and she is fit for treatment, the team considers multi-agent regimens rather than single-agent therapy. They also review the potential benefits and increased toxicity of a paclitaxel-containing three-drug regimen versus a two-drug comparator.
recommended_actions: Use combination chemotherapy rather than single-agent chemotherapy.
Consider paclitaxel plus cisplatin/doxorubicin, recognizing improved response rate and median survival but increased toxicity.
Weigh the greater toxicity against about a three-month median survival increase before using the three-drug cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel regimen.
Note that recent data suggest no recurrence-free survival benefit for the three-drug combination and increased toxicity.
Consider doxorubicin/cisplatin as a comparator regimen with reported response rates of 28-45%.
Consider doxorubicin/paclitaxel as a double-agent option with reported response rate of 43%.
Consider single-agent doxorubicin or platinum agents only with awareness of lower reported response rates.
source_case.rationale: This vignette fits advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer in an adult and triggers the chemotherapy selection rules. The patient is symptomatic and fit for systemic treatment, so combination chemotherapy is favored over single-agent therapy. The vignette also supports discussion of paclitaxel-containing combinations, the toxicity-survival tradeoff of the three-drug regimen, and comparator response-rate data for alternative regimens.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma returns with intermittent vaginal spotting, mild pelvic pressure, and a few tiny pulmonary nodules on surveillance CT that have remained unchanged for 12 months. She is ambulatory, has preserved renal function, and asks about treatment options, but she reports that her symptoms are still minimal and not interfering with daily activities.
