Her oncologist reviews systemic therapy options and notes that the disease is recurrent but not rapidly progressive. Because she is seeking a treatment plan, the team discusses whether to start chemotherapy now versus other options, including less intensive approaches. The visit focuses on balancing expected benefit against toxicity, especially since she has not yet had any life-threatening complications from her cancer.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like advanced recurrent endometrial cancer with preserved performance status and a chemotherapy discussion, but the symptoms are minimal and the pulmonary findings are stable rather than clearly progressive.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start combination chemotherapy immediately because advanced/recurrent disease is present.
Choose a paclitaxel-containing multi-agent regimen as the default best-shrinkage option.
Frame the visit as a direct comparison between cisplatin/doxorubicin/paclitaxel and other cytotoxic regimens without considering lower-intensity strategies.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the chemotherapy-forward recommendation because the patient’s disease burden is currently low-symptom and not clearly threatening; the vignette no longer supports the same urgency for multi-agent cytotoxic therapy. A safer interpretation is to consider less intensive management, including hormonal therapy or observation depending on tumor biology and clinical trajectory, rather than assuming combination chemotherapy is the preferred next step.
challenging_examples.rationale: The subtle change is the shift from progressive symptomatic recurrence to minimal, non-life-threatening disease with stable lung nodules. That weakens the activation for the combination-chemotherapy recommendation and makes a less aggressive approach more appropriate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 67-year-old woman with a history of stage IV endometrioid endometrial adenocarcinoma presents with worsening vaginal bleeding and pelvic discomfort, and surveillance CT now shows a few pulmonary nodules with slight interval growth. She remains ambulatory, has good renal function, and wants treatment that offers the best chance of tumor shrinkage. Her oncology team reviews systemic therapy options and considers multi-agent chemotherapy because the cancer is recurrent and advanced. They also discuss paclitaxel-containing regimens and the tradeoff between response and toxicity. However, the notes mention that her recurrence is limited to the lung and pelvis and that the disease is not considered rapidly progressive or immediately threatening to organ function.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This closely resembles the source case with advanced recurrent endometrial cancer, symptoms, and a chemotherapy discussion, but the clinical burden is still relatively limited and not clearly life-threatening.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend hormonal therapy as the preferred option solely because the disease is recurrent.
De-emphasize chemotherapy on the assumption that any advanced recurrence with limited symptoms should default to endocrine treatment.
Apply the original chemotherapy selection framework without reassessing symptom severity and pace of progression.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not a clean match for the hormonal-therapy recommendation because the patient is symptomatic with worsening bleeding and pain, so the disease is not minimal-symptom or clearly non-life-threatening. The safer interpretation is that systemic therapy is reasonable, but the vignette does not specifically justify choosing hormonal therapy over chemotherapy on the basis of low symptom burden.
challenging_examples.rationale: The key near-miss is that the patient is no longer minimally symptomatic; she has active bleeding and discomfort. That undermines the condition required for the hormonal-therapy option, even though the overall diagnosis remains advanced recurrent endometrial cancer.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma that does not overexpress HER2 and first-line chemotherapy is being given
selected_rules.execute: Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and HER2 is not overexpressed
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe a biological agent with first-line chemotherapy
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with metastatic gastric cancer or GEJ carcinoma not overexpressing human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), medical oncologists should not prescribe a biological agent in addition to a first-line chemotherapy regimen

selected_rules.activation_condition: Advanced gastric or GEJ carcinoma and later-line therapy is being considered
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor in addition to standard of care
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with gastric or GEJ carcinoma undergoing later lines of therapy, medical oncologists should not prescribe ICI in addition to standard of care.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Adult with advanced, non-resectable gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Recurrent or metastatic gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add an immune checkpoint inhibitor to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma starting first-line treatment
selected_rules.execute: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with HER2 overexpressing gastric or GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe the addition of trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen with an immune checkpoint inhibitor
selected_rules.source_statement: In patients with recurrent or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma, medical oncologists should prescribe an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) in addition to a fluoropyrimidine doublet chemotherapy regimen in the first-line setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Gastric or GEJ carcinoma is recurrent or metastatic and first-line treatment is being selected
selected_rules.execute: Use a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical oncologists should prescribe either a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen in the first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic gastric and GEJ carcinoma.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is not overexpressed. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team considers adding a targeted biologic agent to chemotherapy because of the metastatic disease, but the tumor is HER2-negative and this is his initial systemic regimen.
recommended_actions: Prescribe a fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin doublet or a fluoropyrimidine-irinotecan doublet regimen for first-line treatment.
Do not add a biological agent to the first-line chemotherapy regimen because the tumor does not overexpress HER2.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes metastatic gastric/GEJ carcinoma starting first-line therapy with HER2 not overexpressed, which triggers the recommendation against adding a biological agent to first-line chemotherapy. It also meets the general first-line advanced gastric/GEJ treatment rule recommending a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet regimen.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing reports HER2 as 1+ without overexpression. He has not received prior systemic therapy and is being evaluated for first-line treatment. His performance status is fair, and he is interested in starting treatment promptly. The oncology team discusses whether to add trastuzumab to chemotherapy because of the metastatic presentation, but the tumor is not HER2 overexpressing and this is his initial systemic regimen.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Very similar metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma starting first-line therapy, but HER2 is low-level/negative rather than overexpressed.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add trastuzumab to a fluoropyrimidine-based doublet because the disease is metastatic.
Select a HER2-targeted biologic alongside chemotherapy on the assumption that any HER2 staining qualifies.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for trastuzumab because the tumor is not HER2 overexpressing. The safer interpretation is to treat as HER2-negative metastatic GEJ adenocarcinoma and choose a standard fluoropyrimidine-doublet regimen without trastuzumab.
challenging_examples.rationale: The recommendation for trastuzumab requires HER2 overexpression. A 1+ result does not satisfy that condition, so HER2-targeted therapy is not indicated.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 62-year-old man with progressive dysphagia, early satiety, and a 9-kg weight loss is diagnosed with metastatic gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma after CT shows liver and peritoneal metastases. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, and testing shows HER2 is not overexpressed. He previously completed adjuvant fluoropyrimidine-oxaliplatin chemotherapy 7 months ago after resection of a localized gastric primary and now has biopsy-proven recurrence with distant metastases. He is being evaluated for first-line treatment for recurrent disease, and the oncology team is considering adding a biologic agent to chemotherapy because the disease is metastatic and treatment-naive in the metastatic setting.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like metastatic, HER2-negative gastroesophageal cancer starting systemic therapy, but this is recurrent disease after prior perioperative chemotherapy rather than truly untreated first-line metastatic therapy.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Add a biologic agent to first-line chemotherapy because the cancer is metastatic and HER2-negative.
Apply the initial-diagnosis metastatic recommendation without considering the prior adjuvant regimen.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for a first-line add-on biologic decision under the initial metastatic setting because the case is recurrent after prior systemic therapy. The safer interpretation is that treatment should be selected in the recurrent/metastatic context with attention to prior exposure rather than reflexively adding a biologic to first-line chemotherapy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source rule against adding a biologic agent is tied to metastatic gastric/GEJ cancer not overexpressing HER2 in first-line treatment. Here, the key mismatch is prior adjuvant chemotherapy with recurrence, which changes the treatment context and makes the simple initial-first-line rule inapplicable.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling
selected_rules.execute: Do not use plastic cassettes because recoveries are poor
selected_rules.source_statement: Plastic cassettes (37-mm) yielded poor recoveries of acrylamide and are therefore unsuitable

selected_rules.activation_condition: Monitoring airborne acrylamide exposure
selected_rules.execute: Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube
selected_rules.source_statement: A worker's exposure to airborne acrylamide should be determined by using a personal sampling train consisting of a glass-fiber filter in a Swinnex cassette (13-mm) followed by a silica gel tube

selected_rules.activation_condition: Collecting airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air
selected_rules.source_statement: Samples should be collected at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for a minimum of 2 hr; the maximum air volume should be 120 liters

selected_rules.activation_condition: After collecting airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol
selected_rules.source_statement: The silica gel tube should then be treated with methanol to extract the acrylamide

selected_rules.activation_condition: After sampling airborne acrylamide on glass-fiber filters
selected_rules.execute: Transfer filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses
selected_rules.source_statement: An important step in this method is the transfer of the glass-fiber filters to glass vials containing 1 ml of methanol immediately after sampling to avoid losses of acrylamide from the filter by evaporation

selected_rules.activation_condition: Analyzing airborne acrylamide samples
selected_rules.execute: Use gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector
selected_rules.source_statement: Analysis should be conducted by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector

selected_rules.activation_condition: Using animal evidence to guide acrylamide exposure limits
selected_rules.execute: Base occupational exposure limits on animal studies showing exposure-response relationships
selected_rules.source_statement: However, many studies demonstrate a relationship between exposure and adverse health effects in animals. These studies are the bases for occupational exposure limits recommended by many organizations and government agencies

selected_rules.activation_condition: Evaluating acrylamide carcinogenicity in animals
selected_rules.execute: Treat acrylamide as an animal carcinogen based on rat and mouse studies
selected_rules.source_statement: Studies in rats and mice indicate that acrylamide is an animal carcinogen

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They initially considered a 37-mm plastic cassette because it is commonly used for other dusts, but the sampling plan is being revised after a review of the acrylamide method. The hygienist also wants to ensure the sample can be analyzed reliably after collection and that losses during transport are minimized.
recommended_actions: Do not use a 37-mm plastic cassette for acrylamide sampling because recoveries are poor.
Use a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube.
Collect samples at a maximum flowrate of 1 liter/min for at least 2 hours and no more than 120 liters of air.
After sampling, transfer the glass-fiber filters immediately to glass vials containing 1 ml methanol to avoid evaporation losses.
Extract acrylamide from the silica gel tube with methanol.
Analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes workplace monitoring for airborne acrylamide and explicitly includes use of a 37-mm plastic cassette, which triggers the recommendation against that device due to poor recoveries. The scenario also supports the standard acrylamide monitoring method: a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, sampled at no more than 1 L/min for at least 2 hours and with a maximum air volume of 120 L. Post-collection handling and analysis steps are also triggered: immediate transfer of filters to methanol-containing glass vials, methanol extraction of the silica gel tube, and gas chromatography with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. They initially considered a 37-mm plastic cassette because it is commonly used for other dusts, but the revised plan uses a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube. Sampling is set for 1.5 hours at 1 L/min to fit the technician’s short task assignment, and the filter will be placed into a vial with methanol only after the shift ends, several hours later, before GC analysis with a nitrogen/phosphorus detector.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still involves occupational acrylamide monitoring with the same sampler and analysis method, but the sampling duration is too short and the filter is not preserved immediately after collection.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Proceed with the planned acrylamide personal sampling because the flowrate and device look correct.
Accept delayed transfer of the glass-fiber filter to methanol after the shift.
Use the collected sample as a valid comparison for workplace exposure despite the abbreviated run.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because acrylamide samples must be collected at no more than 1 L/min for at least 2 hours, and the filter should be transferred immediately to a glass vial with 1 mL methanol after sampling. A 1.5-hour run and delayed preservation risk undercollection and loss of analyte, so the safer interpretation is that the sample plan should be revised before monitoring proceeds.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the same workplace exposure scenario and nearly the same method, but two subtle details invalidate the recommendation: the sampling time is under the minimum 2 hours, and the filter preservation step is delayed instead of immediate.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old quality-control technician in a cable-coating plant comes to occupational health after a routine air-monitoring review. The workplace uses acrylamide-based materials, and the hygienist is planning personal exposure sampling for the technician during a 3-hour shift near the mixing area. The technician has no symptoms, but the team wants a method that will accurately capture airborne acrylamide. The draft plan uses a personal sampling train with a glass-fiber filter in a 13-mm Swinnex cassette followed by a silica gel tube, with collection at 1 L/min. After sampling, the silica gel tube is intended to be extracted with isopropanol, and the laboratory will analyze the samples by gas chromatography using a flame ionization detector. The filters themselves will be transferred immediately into glass vials containing methanol.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setup closely resembles the standard acrylamide monitoring workflow, but the extraction solvent and detector choice are subtly wrong.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Approve the plan because the sampler and immediate filter handling appear appropriate.
Proceed with isopropanol extraction of the silica gel tube.
Send the samples for GC with a flame ionization detector.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the silica gel tube should be extracted with methanol, and analysis should be performed by gas chromatography using a nitrogen/phosphorus detector. The safer interpretation is that the sampling approach is close, but the post-collection extraction and analytical method need correction before the plan can be accepted.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette keeps the same occupational acrylamide monitoring context and most of the recommended steps, but it swaps in an incorrect solvent and detector, making the recommendation inapplicable as written.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering any antiretroviral medication other than TDF/FTC or TDF for PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP
selected_rules.source_statement: The use of other antiretroviral medications for PrEP, either in place of or in addition to TDF/FTC (or TDF) is not recommended. (IIIA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering noncontinuous or coitally timed oral PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use
selected_rules.source_statement: The prescription of oral PrEP for coitally-timed or other noncontinuous daily use is not recommended. (IIIA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for sexually-active adult MSM (men who have sex with men) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: heterosexually active adult men or women at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult heterosexually active men and women who are at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: adult injection drug users at substantial risk of HIV acquisition
selected_rules.execute: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option
selected_rules.source_statement: PrEP is recommended as one prevention option for adult injection drug users (IDU) at substantial risk of HIV acquisition. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: before prescribing PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing
selected_rules.source_statement: Acute and chronic HIV infection must be excluded by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before PrEP is prescribed. (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: prescribing PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: The only medication regimen approved by the Food and Drug Administration and recommended for PrEP with all the populations specified in this guideline is daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) (IA)

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient is taking PrEP
selected_rules.execute: Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months
selected_rules.source_statement: HIV infection should be assessed at least every 3 months while patients are taking PrEP so that those with incident infection do not continue taking it. The 2-drug regimen of TDF/FTC is inadequate therapy for established HIV infection, and its use may engender resistance to either or both drugs. (IA)

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function. After discussing options, he is interested in daily PrEP and agrees to follow-up testing.
recommended_actions: Offer daily oral PrEP with TDF/FTC as one prevention option.
Exclude acute and chronic HIV infection by symptom history and HIV testing immediately before prescribing PrEP.
Use daily TDF 300 mg co-formulated with FTC 200 mg (Truvada) as the approved regimen.
Assess HIV infection at least every 3 months while the patient is taking PrEP.
Do not use other antiretroviral medications for PrEP.
Do not prescribe oral PrEP for intermittent, episodic, or other noncontinuous daily use.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers the recommendation for daily oral PrEP in an adult MSM at substantial risk of HIV acquisition, along with the requirement to exclude HIV infection before prescribing and to use the approved TDF/FTC regimen. It also triggers follow-up HIV assessment every 3 months while on PrEP. The patient’s questions about adding another antiretroviral or using PrEP only on sex days activate the not-recommended rules against other antiretrovirals and noncontinuous oral PrEP.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy, but today’s baseline labs show an eGFR of 38 mL/min/1.73 m2. He is eager to start immediately and plans to follow up in 3 months.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The sexual risk profile, recent negative HIV test, and PrEP request are nearly unchanged, but reduced renal function makes the standard oral TDF/FTC PrEP regimen inappropriate.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start daily oral TDF/FTC PrEP despite the low eGFR
Recommend coitally timed PrEP because he only has sex intermittently
Add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection
Proceed without rechecking HIV status or renal eligibility
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although he remains at substantial sexual risk, this is not guideline-concordant for TDF/FTC PrEP because his renal function is below the usual threshold for safe use. The safer interpretation is to defer that regimen and consider an alternative prevention strategy or further renal evaluation rather than reflexively prescribing standard oral PrEP.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case preserves the PrEP workflow but invalidates the recommendation by changing kidney function to a level that makes TDF/FTC unsafe or unsuitable. The tempting action is to apply the original MSM PrEP recommendation without noticing the renal contraindication.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men presents for HIV prevention counseling. He reports multiple male partners over the past 6 months, inconsistent condom use, and one recent bacterial STI. He has no fever, rash, sore throat, or other symptoms suggestive of acute HIV infection, and he had a negative HIV test 2 weeks ago. He asks about starting PrEP and wonders whether he can take it only on days he expects sex, or whether he should add another antiretroviral medication for extra protection. He is otherwise healthy and has normal renal function. On review of outside records, however, he was diagnosed with HIV 8 months ago at another clinic, took ART briefly, and then stopped after losing insurance; he has not had confirmatory testing since. He is still interested in prevention counseling and follow-up testing.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still looks like a standard PrEP consult in a high-risk MSM with a recent negative test, but prior documented HIV infection changes the entire management pathway.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer daily oral TDF/FTC PrEP as routine prevention
Reassure him that the recent negative HIV test clears him for PrEP
Suggest intermittent PrEP or adding another antiretroviral for extra protection
Schedule routine PrEP follow-up without addressing possible established HIV infection
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not a PrEP-eligible scenario because prior documented HIV infection makes exclusion of chronic infection impossible without full HIV re-evaluation. The safer interpretation is that he needs diagnostic assessment and re-engagement in HIV care, not PrEP initiation.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette is intentionally similar to the source case, but the hidden prior HIV diagnosis invalidates the prerequisite to exclude chronic HIV infection immediately before prescribing PrEP. A superficial read may miss that this is no longer a prevention-only visit.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Bird has signs of avian chlamydiosis
selected_rules.execute: Do not buy or sell the bird.
selected_rules.source_statement: Avoid purchasing or selling birds that have signs of AC.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Birds are being treated for avian chlamydiosis
selected_rules.execute: Do not expose treated birds to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.
selected_rules.source_statement: Because treated birds can be reinfected, they should not be exposed to untreated birds or other potential sources of infection.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Humans with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Use tetracyclines as the drugs of choice.
selected_rules.source_statement: Tetracyclines are the drugs of choice (3 ).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Most patients with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Treat with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
selected_rules.source_statement: Most patients respond to oral therapy (100 mg of doxycycline administered twice a day or 500 mg of tetracycline hydrochloride administered four times a day).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Severely ill patient with psittacosis needing initial treatment
selected_rules.execute: Give intravenous doxycycline hyclate 4.4 mg/kg/day divided into two infusions, up to 100 mg per dose.
selected_rules.source_statement: For initial treatment of severely ill patients, doxycycline hyclate can be administered intravenously at a dosage of 4.4 mg/kg (2 mg/lb) body weight per day divided into two infusions per day (up to 100 mg per dose).

selected_rules.activation_condition: Psittacosis treatment
selected_rules.execute: Continue treatment for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.
selected_rules.source_statement: However, relapse can occur, and treatment must continue for at least 10-14 days after fever abates.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Patient with psittacosis
selected_rules.execute: Use standard infection-control precautions; do not use special isolation procedures such as private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks.
selected_rules.source_statement: Standard infection-control precautions are sufficient for patients with psittacosis, and specific isolation procedures (e.g., private room, negative pressure air flow, and masks) are not indicated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: Person in contact with infected birds
selected_rules.execute: Inform them about the disease and have them wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and an N95 or higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.
selected_rules.source_statement: Inform all persons in contact with infected birds about the nature of the disease. Instruct them to wear protective clothing, gloves, a disposable surgical cap, and a respirator with an N95 rating or a higher-efficiency respirator when cleaning cages or handling infected birds.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers.
recommended_actions: Do not buy or sell the birds that have signs of avian chlamydiosis.
Treat the patient with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily or tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg four times daily.
Use standard infection-control precautions; special isolation procedures such as a private room, negative pressure airflow, or masks are not indicated.
source_case.rationale: The patient has psittacosis consistent with exposure to ill birds, triggering the recommendation for tetracycline-class treatment and standard infection-control precautions. The vignette also includes birds with signs of avian chlamydiosis, which triggers the recommendation not to buy or sell those birds.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. He asks whether he should keep working around the birds while he recovers. The shop owner mentions the birds were not being sold; they were already set aside for veterinary treatment after a recent diagnosis of avian chlamydiosis.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The presentation still strongly suggests psittacosis from ill parakeets, but the birds are no longer being bought or sold—they are already designated for treatment, so the specific avoidance recommendation does not apply.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Tell the shop not to buy or sell the birds because they appear sick
Focus on the avian chlamydiosis exposure and ignore that the birds are not part of a sale
Assume the bird-handling restriction is automatically triggered by any sick bird contact
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still compatible with human psittacosis, but it does not meet the condition for the bird-purchase/sale prohibition because the birds are not being bought or sold. The safer interpretation is to manage the patient’s illness appropriately and address infection-control and occupational exposure, while recognizing that the cited bird-sale rule is not activated.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rule requires birds with signs of avian chlamydiosis that are being bought or sold. Here, the birds look ill, but they have been removed from sale and are under veterinary treatment, so that specific prohibition is not guideline-concordant.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 54-year-old pet shop employee presents with 6 days of fever, headache, dry cough, and worsening fatigue. He reports cleaning several parakeet cages at work last week and says two birds had been “sneezing” and looked ill, but he still helped move them for sale. Today he is febrile to 39.1°C and mildly short of breath, but he is alert and hemodynamically stable. Chest exam shows scattered crackles. A chest radiograph obtained in clinic shows only mild peribronchial thickening without focal infiltrate or respiratory distress, and he is able to take oral medications.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The exposure history and symptom complex still resemble psittacosis, but the patient is not severely ill, so the intravenous initial-treatment recommendation does not fit.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start intravenous doxycycline because psittacosis is suspected
Treat as if he needs inpatient-level initial therapy
Use the severity of the bird exposure alone to justify IV doxycycline
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains a plausible outpatient psittacosis case, but he is hemodynamically stable, not hypoxic, and not severely ill. The safer interpretation is that oral therapy is the guideline-concordant starting approach rather than IV doxycycline for severe disease.
challenging_examples.rationale: IV doxycycline is recommended for severely ill patients needing initial treatment. This patient has fever and mild dyspnea but no evidence of severe illness, instability, or inability to take oral therapy, so the severe-disease criterion is not met.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is used primarily as a softener in plastics, especially PVC; occupational exposure can occur during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics
selected_rules.execute: No clinical action specified
selected_rules.source_statement: Occupational exposure occurs during the production of DEHP, during the addition of DEHP to plastics, and in the processing of softened plastics.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP exposure is being assessed in an occupational setting
selected_rules.execute: Consider that reported air concentrations in workplaces have ranged from very low to high levels depending on industry
selected_rules.source_statement: Air concentrations between 0.01 and 2.0 mg/m3 were recorded... Concentrations between 1.7 and 66 mg/m3 were recorded... DEHP air concentrations between 0.09 and 0.16 mg/m3 were reported.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered orally in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect substantial gut hydrolysis to MEHP before absorption
selected_rules.source_statement: DEHP is hydrolyzed in the gut.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is given at high oral doses in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect unmetabolized DEHP to appear in liver only after high doses
selected_rules.source_statement: In the liver unhydrolyzed DEHP can only be found after administration of high doses.

selected_rules.activation_condition: MEHP is administered orally
selected_rules.execute: Expect MEHP to be well absorbed
selected_rules.source_statement: Experiments with p.o. administration of MEHP indicated that this metabolite was well absorbed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered intravenously in rats
selected_rules.execute: Expect rapid elimination from blood with short half-lives
selected_rules.source_statement: Intravenously administered DEHP is rapidly eliminated from blood.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP exposure is being evaluated for distribution in the body
selected_rules.execute: Expect rapid distribution to liver, carcass, lung, and spleen after intravenous dosing
selected_rules.source_statement: Radioactivity from DEHP was rapidly distributed to liver, carcass, lung and spleen when administered i.v.

selected_rules.activation_condition: DEHP is administered orally
selected_rules.execute: Expect distribution mainly as MEHP
selected_rules.source_statement: Perorally administered DEHP is mainly distributed as MEHP.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 41-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. No acute symptoms are reported, but he is concerned after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure is related to production and processing tasks rather than a one-time spill. The workplace also has variable ventilation, and measured air levels in similar facilities are known to range widely depending on industry and task.
recommended_actions: Recognize that the patient’s occupational exposure is plausibly related to DEHP use in PVC/plastic production and processing.
Consider that workplace air concentrations can vary widely, from very low to high levels depending on the industry and task.
source_case.rationale: The vignette matches DEHP exposure in an occupational setting during production and processing of softened plastics, triggering R001 and R002. No clinical intervention is specified by these rules beyond recognizing the exposure context and noting that workplace air concentrations may vary widely.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 39-year-old man works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. He reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing and processing, and he asks about a chemical used in the materials. He has no acute symptoms, but he is worried after learning the compound is common in flexible plastics. The occupational health nurse documents that his exposure occurs mainly in a nearby storage area where sealed drums are received and inventoried, rather than during addition to plastics or processing of softened products. The workplace ventilation is variable, but there is no evidence that he personally performs the production tasks associated with the material.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Same PVC flooring workplace and concern about flexible-plastic plasticizers, but his actual contact is limited to storage/handling of sealed drums rather than production or processing exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Assume this is an occupational DEHP exposure during PVC production and document it as such.
Treat the workplace as a typical processing exposure with variable airborne concentrations based on the production line description.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for DEHP production/addition/processing exposure because his described role is inventory and receipt of sealed containers, not direct production or processing of softened plastics. A safer interpretation is that he may have indirect occupational contact, but the specific exposure scenario needed for the rule is not established.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 requires exposure during production, addition to plastics, or processing of softened plastics. R002 applies when DEHP exposure is being assessed in an occupational setting; here the question is about indirect storage handling, which makes the intended exposure context uncertain and not clearly the same workplace exposure scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 46-year-old woman works in a PVC flooring plant where softened plastics are cut and heat-sealed on the production line. She asks the occupational health nurse about a chemical commonly used in flexible plastics because several coworkers have discussed it. She reports that the job involves handling plasticizers during manufacturing, but her own assignment is quality control in an isolated office adjacent to the plant, with no routine access to the production floor. Air monitoring in the facility has been variable, but she has not had direct task-based exposure to the softened-plastic processing area.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The same industrial setting and plasticizer concern are present, but her actual duties are office-based quality control without direct production-floor exposure.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Record her as having occupational DEHP exposure from PVC production and processing.
Apply the workplace air concentration note as if it directly reflects her personal exposure.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette does not cleanly satisfy the exposure condition because she is not performing production, addition, or processing tasks involving softened plastics. The safer interpretation is that she is in the same facility but lacks the direct occupational exposure described by the rule, so the DEHP exposure context is not established for her individual job role.
challenging_examples.rationale: R001 is invalidated because her role is not in production or processing of softened plastics. R002 is also not clearly met because the rule is about assessing DEHP exposure in an occupational setting, and her own exposure is not demonstrated despite being in the same plant.

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged 2 through 4 years with asthma, recurrent wheezing, or wheezing/asthma in the past 12 months
selected_rules.execute: Do not give FluMist.
selected_rules.source_statement: Children whose parents or caregivers answer 'yes' to this question and children who have asthma or who had a wheezing episode noted in the medical record within the past 12 months should not receive FluMist.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person caring for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment
selected_rules.execute: Do not give FluMist / LAIV.
selected_rules.source_statement: Persons who care for severely immunosuppressed persons who require a protective environment should not receive FluMist given the theoretical risk for transmission of the live-attenuated vaccine virus.

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged under 9 years
selected_rules.execute: Do not use Afluria.
selected_rules.source_statement: ACIP does not recommend the U.S.-licensed CSL Biotherapies' TIV, Afluria, for children aged <9 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person with a previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine
selected_rules.execute: Do not give future influenza vaccine.
selected_rules.source_statement: A previous severe allergic reaction to influenza vaccine, regardless of the component suspected to be responsible for the reaction, is a contraindication to future receipt of the vaccine.

selected_rules.activation_condition: all persons aged 6 months or older
selected_rules.execute: Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
selected_rules.source_statement: Routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged ≥6 months.

selected_rules.activation_condition: vaccination is being planned and influenza activity has not yet started in the community
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination optimally should occur before onset of influenza activity in the community. Therefore, vaccination providers should offer vaccination as soon as vaccine is available.

selected_rules.activation_condition: influenza viruses are circulating in the community
selected_rules.execute: Continue offering vaccination throughout the influenza season.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination should be offered throughout the influenza season (i.e., as long as influenza viruses are circulating in the community).

selected_rules.activation_condition: child aged 6 months through 8 years receiving influenza vaccine for the first season
selected_rules.execute: Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart.
selected_rules.source_statement: Children aged 6 months through 8 years require 2 doses of influenza vaccine (administered a minimum of 4 weeks apart) during their first season of vaccination to optimize immune response.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season.
recommended_actions: Do not give FluMist.
Offer annual influenza vaccination every year.
Vaccinate as soon as vaccine is available, before influenza activity begins if possible.
Give 2 doses of influenza vaccine at least 4 weeks apart for his first season of vaccination.
source_case.rationale: R001 applies because he is age 2 through 4 years with asthma and wheezing in the past 12 months, so FluMist should not be used. R005 applies because routine annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all persons aged 6 months or older. R006 applies because vaccination is being planned before influenza activity has started in the community. R008 applies because he is 6 months through 8 years old and is receiving influenza vaccine for the first season, so he needs 2 doses at least 4 weeks apart.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He received one influenza vaccine dose last winter but missed the second dose because of a family move. The community has not yet reported influenza activity this season. The parent specifically asks whether he needs two doses again this year or just one.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The child remains age 3, seen before local influenza circulation, and the family is still asking about routine seasonal vaccination. The only change is that he already received one influenza vaccine dose previously, which alters the first-season dosing rule.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Automatically schedule 2 influenza vaccine doses 4 weeks apart as if this were his first season of vaccination.
Document that he is unvaccinated and needs the full two-dose primary series.
Apply the original first-season recommendation without checking prior vaccine history.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the first-season two-dose rule because he is not influenza-vaccine naive; he already received one dose last winter. The safer interpretation is to verify whether he completed the age-appropriate series previously and then vaccinate according to current season history rather than reflexively treating him as a first-time recipient.
challenging_examples.rationale: R008 applies only to children 6 months through 8 years receiving influenza vaccine for the first season. Prior receipt of one influenza dose means the vignette no longer clearly meets the first-season criterion, so the two-dose recommendation cannot be applied without confirming prior immunization status.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic in early October for a routine visit. He has a history of asthma with intermittent wheezing over the past year, including an urgent care visit for wheeze 4 months ago, but he is currently well and afebrile. His mother asks about the flu vaccine because the family wants him protected before flu season starts. He has never received an influenza vaccine before. The community reported influenza activity began last week, and several families in his daycare have had influenza-like illness. The parent wonders whether they should wait until later in the month to vaccinate.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The child is still an eligible age, has asthma, is well at the visit, and the family is still seeking seasonal influenza vaccination. The subtle difference is that influenza activity has already started in the community, so the ‘vaccinate before influenza begins’ condition no longer holds.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: State that vaccination should be deferred because the ideal pre-season window has passed.
Tell the family to wait until influenza activity declines before immunizing.
Treat the timing recommendation as inapplicable and skip offering the vaccine now.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not concordant with the pre-season timing recommendation because influenza is already circulating locally. The safer interpretation is to continue offering vaccination now, since vaccination should still be provided throughout the season while influenza viruses are circulating.
challenging_examples.rationale: R006 requires that vaccination be planned before influenza activity has begun in the community. Here, local influenza activity has already started, so the specific pre-season recommendation is invalidated even though routine vaccination remains appropriate.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Control employee exposure by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
selected_rules.source_statement: recommends that employee exposure to inorganic lead in the workplace be controlled by adherence to the following sections.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Apply the standard as a lifetime 8-hour day, 40-hour week protection target.
selected_rules.source_statement: The standard is designed to protect the health and safety of workers for an 8-hour day, 40-hour week over a working lifetime; compliance with the standard should therefore prevent adverse effects of lead on the health and safety of workers.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
selected_rules.source_statement: The standard is measurable by techniques that are valid, reproducible, and available to industry and government agencies.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace exposure is to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Recognize that sufficient technology exists to comply with the recommended standard.
selected_rules.source_statement: Sufficient technology exists to permit compliance with the recommended standard.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workplace air exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Keep the 8-hour time-weighted average airborne lead concentration at or below 0.15 mg Pb/m^3.
selected_rules.source_statement: workers shall not be exposed to inorganic lead at a concentration greater that 0.15 mg Pb/m^ determined as a time-weighted average (TWA) exposure for an 8-hour workday.

selected_rules.activation_condition: environmental samples are collected for lead monitoring
selected_rules.execute: Collect environmental samples using Appendix I or an equivalent method, and analyze them using Appendix II or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy.
selected_rules.source_statement: Procedures for collection of environmental samples shall be as provided in Appendix I, or by an equivalent method. Analysis of samples shall be as provided in Appendix II, or by any method shown to be equivalent in precision and accuracy to the method specified in Appendix II.

selected_rules.activation_condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
selected_rules.source_statement: Biologic monitoring shall be made available to all workers subject to "exposure to inorganic lead."

selected_rules.activation_condition: worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead
selected_rules.execute: Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
selected_rules.source_statement: It consists of sampling and analysis of whole blood, or alternatively, of urine for lead content.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible.
recommended_actions: Control employee exposure to inorganic lead by adhering to the standard sections in this document.
Use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques for compliance determinations.
Make biologic monitoring available to the worker.
Use whole blood lead testing, or urine lead testing as an alternative, to assess lead absorption.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a worker with occupational exposure to inorganic lead, which triggers the general control and monitoring recommendations. Because he is subject to exposure, biologic monitoring should be made available, and lead absorption should be assessed with whole blood lead testing or urine lead testing as an alternative. The workplace is also performing air sampling, so compliance determinations should use valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years, works full time, and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations. However, he was transferred last month to a clerical role in the administrative office and no longer enters the production floor or participates in battery dismantling, cleanup, or smelting operations.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting remains a battery recycling plant with the same occupational health workflow and historical lead exposure, but his current duties are now office-based and no longer involve exposure to inorganic lead.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend the full lead-exposure control package as if he is still subject to inorganic lead exposure
Arrange biologic monitoring with whole blood lead testing immediately
Document ongoing lead surveillance solely based on his prior job title
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the exposed-worker recommendations because he is no longer currently subject to inorganic lead exposure. A careful reading should distinguish past exposure from present work assignment; routine lead biologic monitoring and the exposure-triggered controls are not automatically indicated for his new clerical role.
challenging_examples.rationale: The rules activate when the worker is subject to exposure to inorganic lead. Although the plant context is unchanged, his current position removes the exposure trigger.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man works in a battery recycling plant and presents for a routine occupational health visit. He reports no acute symptoms, but says his job involves handling lead-containing dust during dismantling and cleanup of lead-acid batteries. The company recently began air sampling in the work area, and the occupational nurse is reviewing exposure controls. His supervisor asks whether the current monitoring program is appropriate and whether workers like him should have medical surveillance. The patient has been on the job for 6 years and spends most shifts near the smelting and sorting stations where inorganic lead exposure is possible. On review of the industrial hygiene log, the sampler used a field screen for lead particulates, but the actual compliance determination is being made from an unvalidated in-house colorimetric method that has not been shown to match a standard analytical procedure.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The overall lead-exposure scenario is unchanged, and environmental sampling is still being performed, but the compliance analysis method is not valid, reproducible, or shown equivalent to the standard approach.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Accept the in-house colorimetric result for compliance decisions
Proceed as though the environmental monitoring program satisfies lead standard requirements
Document that the air sampling methods are appropriate because samples were collected
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for compliance determination because the measurement technique lacks validation, reproducibility, and demonstrated equivalence. The safer interpretation is that the workplace may still need proper environmental sampling and analysis using an approved or equivalent method before any compliance conclusion is made.
challenging_examples.rationale: The standard requires valid, reproducible, and available measurement techniques, and environmental samples must be collected/analyzed by Appendix methods or an equivalent method with comparable precision and accuracy. An unvalidated in-house assay breaks that condition.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public may be worried or outraged
selected_rules.execute: Do not dismiss outrage or tell people they should not be afraid.
selected_rules.source_statement: Don't Dismiss Outrage. ... When people are afraid and worried, the worst thing to do is pretend that they're not. The second worst thing is to tell them they shouldn't be.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When giving reassurance during an early crisis
selected_rules.execute: Do not over-reassure or minimize the risk.
selected_rules.source_statement: Don't over-reassure. ... Reassurance can backfire.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public or media ask about cause, blame, or liability early in the event
selected_rules.execute: Do not discuss liability, blame, or cause before the situation is contained.
selected_rules.source_statement: Avoid discussing liability: Questions of cause, blame, responsibility, and liability often follow a crisis. It is not appropriate to discuss them at the early stages.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the event involves a possible bioterrorism claim or rumor
selected_rules.execute: Do not speculate about bioterrorism; refer questions to law enforcement and coordinate statements.
selected_rules.source_statement: CDC will not comment to the media about the possibility of an outbreak being a bioterrorist event; nor will it comment on the FBI's participation in an investigation unless first confirmed by the FBI to the media.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
selected_rules.execute: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to help people make the best possible decisions for their well-being.
selected_rules.source_statement: CERC is the attempt by public health professionals to provide information that allows individuals, stakeholders, and entire communities to make the best possible decisions for their well-being during a crisis or emergency.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When communicating during a crisis or emergency
selected_rules.execute: Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
selected_rules.source_statement: Throughout this book, six principles of effective crisis and risk communication are emphasized: 1. Be First... 2. Be Right... 3. Be Credible... 4. Express Empathy... 5. Promote Action... 6. Show Respect.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When the public is affected by a crisis
selected_rules.execute: Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
selected_rules.source_statement: When communicating in the initial phase of an emergency, it is important to present information that is simple, credible, accurate, consistent, and delivered on time.

selected_rules.activation_condition: When people need to know what to do during a crisis
selected_rules.execute: Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
selected_rules.source_statement: Giving people meaningful things to do calms anxiety, helps restore order, and promotes a restored sense of control.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has not yet received confirmed public health information, and local officials are still investigating the source and scope of the illness.
recommended_actions: Use crisis and emergency risk communication principles to guide the response.
Be first, be right, be credible, express empathy, promote action, and show respect.
Provide simple, credible, accurate, consistent information on time.
Give people meaningful actions they can take and offer a range of responses.
Do not dismiss the anxiety or tell people they should not be afraid.
Do not discuss blame, responsibility, liability, or cause before the situation is contained; avoid speculating about a deliberate cause.
source_case.rationale: The vignette involves an unfolding community illness event with public anxiety, early uncertainty, and questions about cause and blame. That triggers the crisis communication rules: communicate with CERC principles, provide timely accurate information, offer actionable steps, and respond with empathy and respect. Because the patient and community are worried, the response should not minimize fear or dismiss outrage. Because the source is not yet confirmed, discussions of blame or a deliberate cause should be avoided.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea over the past three days. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has received a preliminary update from public health that the illnesses appear to be from a self-limited norovirus cluster, and local officials are still finalizing the source and scope of the illness.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still involves a school outbreak with anxious questions about contamination and a possible deliberate cause, but the investigation has already produced a preliminary non-criminal explanation.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Speculate that the cafeteria food was deliberately poisoned and discuss possible blame
Address liability or responsibility before the investigation is complete
Invoke bioterrorism language in the initial counseling
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for early speculation about blame or deliberate cause because there is already a preliminary public health impression of a routine norovirus cluster, not an unresolved suspicious event. The safer interpretation is to acknowledge uncertainty, avoid assigning blame, and defer to public health updates while still providing calm, factual guidance.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule against discussing cause, blame, or bioterrorism is triggered when the source is unclear and the event may be feared as intentional. Here, the key mismatch is that preliminary findings already suggest a non-deliberate outbreak, so the case no longer fits the same early-crisis speculation scenario.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 34-year-old elementary school teacher comes to urgent care after hearing that several students in her district developed sudden vomiting and diarrhea within the same day. She is visibly anxious and says parents are “panicking” on social media, asking whether the school cafeteria food was contaminated and whether this could be a deliberate poisoning. She has no fever or abdominal tenderness and is mainly worried about what to tell her family and coworkers. In the waiting room, she repeatedly asks for confirmation that the outbreak is “probably nothing serious” and wants to know the exact cause immediately. The clinic has confirmed that the same students attended a school picnic and that local officials have already identified a common food source; the investigation is now closed and a press release has been issued with the final cause.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like the same anxious outbreak visit, but the outbreak has already been contained and the cause is no longer unknown.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the visit like an unfolding emergency communication scenario
Avoid discussing the now-confirmed source and final cause
Focus on crisis messaging as if the event were still under active investigation
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is no longer the early, uncertain phase that requires withholding discussion of cause or using initial crisis communication tactics in the same way. Because the source has been confirmed and the investigation is closed, the safer interpretation is routine counseling with the finalized public health information rather than active speculation management.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original recommendation applies to an ongoing, unresolved event with uncertainty about cause and scope. Here, the crucial difference is that public health has already identified the source and issued a final statement, so the early-crisis guidance about avoiding cause speculation is not the operative issue.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-negative
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient has latent tuberculosis infection and is HIV-infected
selected_rules.execute: do not generally offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: ATS and CDC now recommend that this regimen should generally not be offered to persons with LTBI for either HIV-negative or HIV-infected persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection is taking other liver-injury medications
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 1) are concurrently taking other medications associated with liver injury

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection drinks excessive alcohol
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 2) drink excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is discontinued during treatment

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has underlying liver disease
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 3) have underlying liver disease

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient with latent tuberculosis infection has a history of isoniazid-associated liver injury
selected_rules.execute: do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen
selected_rules.source_statement: this regimen should never be offered to patients who 4) have a history of INH-associated liver injury.

selected_rules.activation_condition: patient taking rifampin plus pyrazinamide has AST greater than five times upper limit of normal without symptoms
selected_rules.execute: discontinue rifampin plus pyrazinamide immediately and do not resume
selected_rules.source_statement: Use of RZ should be discontinued immediately and not resumed for any of the following findings: 1) AT greater than five times the upper limit of normal range in an asymptomatic person

source_case.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he recently cut down to 2 beers on weekends. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection, but he does take atorvastatin and has mildly elevated baseline liver enzymes from suspected fatty liver disease.
recommended_actions: Do not generally offer the rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen for latent tuberculosis infection.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the patient drinks excessive amounts of alcohol, even if alcohol use is reduced during treatment.
Do not offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because he has underlying liver disease.
source_case.rationale: This vignette triggers latent tuberculosis infection with HIV-negative status, excessive alcohol use, and underlying liver disease. Under the supplied rules, rifampin plus pyrazinamide should generally not be offered for LTBI, and it should never be offered in patients with excessive alcohol use or underlying liver disease.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats, and his chest radiograph is normal, consistent with latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, although he says he has reduced to a few drinks only on weekends since his test result. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection and no prior liver disease, but he does take atorvastatin and recently started isoniazid prophylaxis at an outside clinic before being referred for TB counseling.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This still looks like uncomplicated latent TB infection with the same alcohol history and medication review, but the added concurrent isoniazid exposure and current liver-injury risk make the rifampin-pyrazinamide question unsafe in a different way.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start rifampin plus pyrazinamide for latent TB because the chest radiograph is normal.
Reassure him that reduced weekend drinking makes the regimen acceptable.
Proceed despite the medication list because atorvastatin is the only relevant drug.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: The case is not guideline-concordant for rifampin plus pyrazinamide. Even though he has LTBI, the key mismatch is that he is already taking another liver-injury-associated medication, and he still has a history of excessive alcohol use. The safer interpretation is to avoid this regimen and choose an alternative LTBI treatment strategy.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rifampin plus pyrazinamide is generally not recommended for LTBI and should not be offered when there is concurrent use of other hepatotoxic medications or excessive alcohol use.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 42-year-old man is evaluated after a positive interferon-gamma release assay during work screening. He has no cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats. A chest radiograph is normal except for a calcified right apical granuloma, and the clinician documents latent tuberculosis infection. He reports drinking 4–6 beers nightly for years, though he says he has been abstinent for the past two weeks. He asks whether he can take the short-course rifampin plus pyrazinamide regimen because a coworker completed it. He has no known HIV infection and no known liver disease, but he recalls that a prior course of isoniazid for exposure prophylaxis was stopped years ago because of mild nausea, not hepatitis. Baseline AST and ALT are within normal limits.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: This preserves the same LTBI workup and alcohol history, but the prior isoniazid stop was for nausea rather than true liver injury, so the obvious-looking contraindication is not actually present; the main issue remains the alcohol exposure and the regimen itself.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Offer rifampin plus pyrazinamide because the prior isoniazid problem was only nausea.
Ignore the alcohol history since he has been abstinent for two weeks.
Use the short-course regimen because the chest radiograph is normal and he is HIV-negative.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still not guideline-concordant for rifampin plus pyrazinamide. The regimen should generally not be offered for LTBI, and his history of excessive alcohol use remains a reason to avoid it even if he recently stopped drinking. The prior isoniazid intolerance was not liver injury, so that detail should not be overcalled, but it does not make the regimen appropriate.
