challenging_examples.rationale: RZ is not recommended for LTBI and should not be used in patients with excessive alcohol intake; recent abstinence does not remove that concern.

selected_rules.activation_condition: heat exposure is prolonged or severe enough to cause body temperature above 38°C
selected_rules.execute: do not continue further exposures during the same day
selected_rules.source_statement: There is abundant evidence that the physiological strain of an exposure which raises body temperature above 38°C is such as to contraindicate further exposures during the same day; it may take hours for complete recovery.

selected_rules.activation_condition: employee exposure to heat in the workplace
selected_rules.execute: control heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard
selected_rules.source_statement: NIOSH recommends that employee exposure to heat in the workplace be controlled by requiring compliance with the work practice standard set forth in the following sections.

selected_rules.activation_condition: hot environmental conditions exceed WBGT 79°F
selected_rules.execute: apply the standard to all places of employment indoors and outdoors and to all employees except those required to wear impermeable protective clothing
selected_rules.source_statement: The provisions of this standard are applicable to all places of employment, indoors and outdoors, and to all employees except those who are required to wear impermeable protective clothing.

selected_rules.activation_condition: continuous exposure for one hour or intermittent exposure for two hours and WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women
selected_rules.execute: initiate one or a combination of the listed protective practices to keep body core temperature from exceeding 100.4°F
selected_rules.source_statement: When exposure of an employee is continuous for one hour or intermittent for a period of two hours and the time-weighted average WBGT exceeds 79°F for men or 76°F for women, then any one or combination of the following practices shall be initiated to insure that the employee's body care temperature does not exceed 100.4°F:

selected_rules.activation_condition: employee is unacclimatized
selected_rules.execute: acclimatize over 6 days starting at 50 percent of anticipated total work load and time exposure on day 1, then increase by 10 percent daily to 100 percent on day 6
selected_rules.source_statement: Unacclimatized employees shall be acclimatized over a period of 6 days. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total work load and time exposure on the first day, followed by daily 10 percent increments building up 100 percent total exposure on the sixth day.

selected_rules.activation_condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave
selected_rules.execute: use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule starting at 50 percent on day 1 and increasing by 20 percent daily to 100 percent on day 4
selected_rules.source_statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from nine or more consecutive calendar days of leave, shall undergo a four day acclimatization period. The acclimatization schedule shall begin with 50 percent of the anticipated total exposure on the first day, followed by daily 20 percent increments building up to 100 percent total exposure on the fourth day.

selected_rules.activation_condition: regular acclimatized employee returns after four consecutive days of illness
selected_rules.execute: obtain medical permission to return to work and use a 4-day reacclimatization schedule as above
selected_rules.source_statement: Regular acclimatized employees who return from four consecutive days of illness should have medical permission to return to the job, and should undergo a four day re-acclimatization period as defined in (2) above.

selected_rules.activation_condition: workers are exposed to hot jobs
selected_rules.execute: implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods
selected_rules.source_statement: A work and rest regimen shall be implemented to reduce the peaks of physiological strain and to improve recovery during rest periods.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.4°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
recommended_actions: Do not continue further heat exposures during the same day.
Control employee heat exposure by requiring compliance with the work practice standard.
Implement a work and rest regimen to reduce peaks of physiological strain and improve recovery during rest periods.
source_case.rationale: The worker had prolonged workplace heat exposure with a measured body temperature above 38°C, which triggers the recommendation to stop further exposures for the rest of the day. Because this is employee heat exposure in the workplace, heat exposure should be controlled through compliance with the work practice standard. Since workers are exposed to hot jobs, a work-and-rest regimen is recommended to reduce physiological strain and support recovery.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a non-air-conditioned loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 37.6°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot all day, and several coworkers were also complaining of the heat.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, symptoms, and hot-workplace context closely mirror a heat-stress case, but the measured temperature never exceeds the threshold that would contraindicate further exposure for the day.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Advise him not to return to work for the rest of the day because this is heat illness.
Document heat exposure and send him home immediately based on the headache and sweating alone.
Recommend stopping all further exposures as if the body temperature were above 38°C.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is still a hot-workplace exposure with symptoms concerning for heat stress, but it is not guideline-concordant to apply the 'no further exposures today' rule because the measured oral temperature is only 37.6°C. A safer interpretation is to continue monitoring, enforce rest and hydration, and assess for evolving heat illness rather than automatically invoking the temperature-based prohibition.
challenging_examples.rationale: The original rule requires body temperature above 38°C to contraindicate further exposures during the same day. Here the temperature is below that threshold, so the specific recommendation does not apply.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old warehouse worker presents to occupational health after a late-afternoon shift in a climate-controlled loading dock. He had been stacking boxes and moving pallets for about 90 minutes with only brief water breaks. On the site nurse’s check, his oral temperature is 38.5°C, and he reports marked fatigue, heavy sweating, and a throbbing headache. He says he feels better after resting indoors for 20 minutes, but the supervisor wants him to return to the dock to finish the shift. He is otherwise healthy and has no history of heat illness. The workplace had been unusually hot outside, but the dock itself remained air-conditioned and below the company’s heat-alert threshold.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The worker, symptoms, and elevated temperature still look like classic occupational heat strain, but the exposure occurred in a cooled work area rather than in a hot job environment requiring the workplace heat-control and work-rest regimen provisions.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Apply the workplace heat work-practice standard as if all employee heat exposure were uncontrolled hot-job exposure.
Institute a formal work-and-rest regimen specifically for hot jobs without confirming that the job site is actually hot.
Treat this as the same occupational heat-exposure scenario as an uncooled loading dock.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: Although he has heat-related symptoms and a temperature above 38°C, the vignette does not support the specific workplace hot-job provisions because the actual work area is climate-controlled and below the heat-alert threshold. The safer interpretation is to evaluate him clinically for heat illness and avoid assuming the hot-workplace standard or hot-job work-rest regimen automatically applies.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 and R008 are triggered by employee exposure to heat in the workplace and workers exposed to hot jobs. Here the dock is described as air-conditioned and below threshold, so the key environmental condition for those recommendations is not established.

selected_rules.activation_condition: occupational exposure to HIV with negligible risk
selected_rules.execute: do not give postexposure prophylaxis
selected_rules.source_statement: For exposures with negligible risk, PEP is not justified (Table 1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: exposure is not occupational exposure to HIV
selected_rules.execute: do not offer PEP
selected_rules.source_statement: Not offer-PEP should not be offered because these are not occupational exposures to HIV (1 ).

selected_rules.activation_condition: skin exposure to HIV without increased risk
selected_rules.execute: do not give PEP because drug toxicity outweighs benefit
selected_rules.source_statement: For skin exposures without increased risk, the risk for drug toxicity outweighs the benefit of PEP.

selected_rules.activation_condition: suspected heroin overdose with coma or severe respiratory distress
selected_rules.execute: give naloxone
selected_rules.source_statement: Naloxone remains the treatment of choice for coma and severe respiratory distress associated with possible drug overdose.

selected_rules.activation_condition: new case of scopolamine-containing heroin overdose
selected_rules.execute: report promptly to local poison-control center and health department
selected_rules.source_statement: new cases should be reported promptly to the local poison-control center and health department.

selected_rules.activation_condition: occupational exposure to HIV with highest risk for transmission
selected_rules.execute: recommend chemoprophylaxis
selected_rules.source_statement: Chemoprophylaxis should be recommended to exposed workers after occupational exposures associated with the highest risk for HIV transmission.

selected_rules.activation_condition: considering any HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen
selected_rules.execute: include zidovudine (ZDV)
selected_rules.source_statement: At present, ZDV should be considered for all PEP regimens because ZDV is the only agent for which data support the efficacy of PEP in the clinical setting.

selected_rules.activation_condition: HIV postexposure prophylaxis regimen is being selected
selected_rules.execute: usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV
selected_rules.source_statement: 3TC should usually be added to ZDV for increased antiretroviral activity and activity against many ZDV-resistant strains.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. This is an occupational exposure with a substantial risk of HIV transmission, rather than a negligible-risk contact or a nonoccupational exposure.
recommended_actions: Recommend HIV chemoprophylaxis after this high-risk occupational exposure.
Include zidovudine (ZDV) in the postexposure prophylaxis regimen.
Usually add lamivudine (3TC) to ZDV when selecting the regimen.
source_case.rationale: The vignette describes a high-risk occupational exposure to HIV, which triggers the recommendation for chemoprophylaxis. The selected rules also support including zidovudine in all PEP regimens and usually adding lamivudine to zidovudine to increase antiretroviral activity.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. The injury occurred while disposing of a phlebotomy needle after the blood draw was completed, and on exam the puncture is superficial with no visible blood on the device. This is an occupational exposure, but the source contact appears limited and low-volume rather than a clearly high-risk percutaneous inoculation.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, timing, and HIV-positive source patient are the same as a high-risk needlestick case, but the exposure details are downgraded to a superficial, low-volume percutaneous injury.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Start HIV postexposure chemoprophylaxis immediately as if this were a high-risk occupational needlestick.
Select a full PEP regimen including zidovudine and lamivudine without reassessing transmission risk.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for high-risk occupational HIV exposure because the injury is superficial and low-volume, making the transmission risk closer to negligible than to the 'highest risk' category. The safer interpretation is to reassess exposure severity and source factors before committing to PEP.
challenging_examples.rationale: R006 applies only to occupational exposures associated with the highest risk for HIV transmission. A superficial, low-volume puncture after needle disposal does not clearly meet that threshold, even though the patient and setting otherwise resemble a classic PEP case.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old emergency department nurse sustains a needlestick injury while drawing blood from a patient with known untreated HIV infection and a high viral load. The needle was visibly blood-filled and penetrated deeply into her finger before she was able to wash the area. She presents within 45 minutes of the exposure. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, and has no contraindications to antiretroviral medications. However, the source patient is her roommate and the blood draw occurred during a private home visit, not as part of an employment-related duty. The exposure itself is percutaneous and recent, but it did not arise from a workplace occupational activity.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly all clinical details match a classic occupational HIV needlestick exposure, but the key context has shifted from workplace exposure to a nonoccupational incident.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Recommend HIV chemoprophylaxis as for an occupational exposure.
Choose zidovudine-based PEP without first recognizing that this is not an occupational exposure.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This case is not guideline-concordant for occupational HIV postexposure prophylaxis because the exposure did not occur in the context of an occupational duty. The safer interpretation is that it is a nonoccupational exposure and should not be managed under the occupational PEP recommendation pathway.
challenging_examples.rationale: R002 is triggered when the exposure is not occupational. Despite the high-risk source and prompt presentation, the setting was a private home visit with no employment-related exposure, so the occupational recommendation does not apply.

selected_rules.activation_condition: routine prevention of hepatitis A in the United States
selected_rules.execute: Implement routine hepatitis A vaccination for children nationwide.
selected_rules.source_statement: All children should receive hepatitis A vaccine at age 1 year (i.e., 12-23 months).

selected_rules.activation_condition: state, county, or community already has a hepatitis A vaccination program for children aged 2-18 years
selected_rules.execute: Maintain the existing childhood hepatitis A vaccination program.
selected_rules.source_statement: States, counties, and communities with existing hepatitis A vaccination programs for children aged 2-18 years are encouraged to maintain these programs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person is traveling to or working in a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity and is susceptible
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate before departure or give immune globulin before departure; vaccine is preferred.
selected_rules.source_statement: All susceptible persons traveling to or working in countries that have high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity (Figure 4) should be vaccinated or receive IG before departure (Tables 1-4). Hepatitis A vaccination at the age-appropriate dose is preferred (Tables 2-4).

selected_rules.activation_condition: travel is planned and first vaccine dose can be given now
selected_rules.execute: Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
selected_rules.source_statement: The first dose of hepatitis A vaccine should be administered as soon as travel is considered.

selected_rules.activation_condition: traveler departs in less than 4 weeks and cannot or will not receive immune globulin
selected_rules.execute: Still give hepatitis A vaccine and inform the traveler protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
selected_rules.source_statement: Travelers departing in <4 weeks who do not or cannot receive IG should nonetheless receive hepatitis A vaccine and be informed that they might not be optimally protected from acquiring hepatitis A in the immediate future (i.e., subsequent 2-4 weeks).

selected_rules.activation_condition: traveler is allergic to a vaccine component or declines vaccine
selected_rules.execute: Give a single dose of immune globulin 0.02 mL/kg; if travel lasts more than 2 months, use 0.06 mL/kg and repeat if travel lasts more than 5 months.
selected_rules.source_statement: Travelers who are allergic to a vaccine component or who elect not to receive vaccine should receive a single dose of IG (0.02 mL/kg), which provides effective protection against hepatitis A for up to 3 months (Table 1). Travelers whose travel period is >2 months should be administered IG at 0.06 mL/kg; administration must be repeated if the travel period is >5 months (Table 1).

selected_rules.activation_condition: person is an adolescent or adult man who has sex with men
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
selected_rules.source_statement: MSM (both adolescents and adults) should be vaccinated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: person uses injection or noninjection illicit drugs
selected_rules.execute: Vaccinate against hepatitis A.
selected_rules.source_statement: Vaccination is recommended for users of injection and noninjection illicit drugs.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to rural Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like protection as quickly as possible and is willing to be vaccinated today.
recommended_actions: Vaccinate before departure because he is a susceptible traveler going to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity.
Give the first hepatitis A vaccine dose as soon as travel is considered.
Because departure is in less than 4 weeks, administer hepatitis A vaccine now and inform him that protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.
source_case.rationale: This traveler is susceptible and is going to a country with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity, so pre-departure hepatitis A vaccination is recommended. Since travel is already planned, the first dose should be given as soon as travel is considered. Because he is leaving in less than 4 weeks, he should still receive vaccine now and be told that immediate protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to Lima, Peru. He has no chronic medical problems and no vaccine allergies. He received a full hepatitis A vaccine series in childhood but cannot find the records. He says he is healthy, takes no regular medications, and asks whether he can do anything now to reduce his risk before leaving. Because departure is soon, he would like the fastest protection available and is willing to be vaccinated today if needed.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting, travel timeline, and request for rapid pre-departure protection are nearly identical to the source, but he is not actually susceptible because he already completed hepatitis A vaccination in childhood.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Administer a first hepatitis A vaccine dose immediately because travel is soon.
Recommend hepatitis A vaccination solely because he is traveling to Peru.
Advise that vaccine now is needed for immediate partial protection before departure.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant for the travel-prevention rule because he is not clearly susceptible; a prior completed hepatitis A series would mean he does not need pretravel hepatitis A vaccination for this indication. The safer interpretation is to verify immunization records rather than treating him as an unvaccinated traveler.
challenging_examples.rationale: The travel recommendation applies to susceptible persons. A documented prior completed series invalidates the assumption that he needs a new pre-departure dose, even though the travel context otherwise looks the same.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 29-year-old man comes to a primary care visit 3 weeks before a planned 10-day trip to Cusco, Peru. He has never received hepatitis A vaccine and does not recall prior infection. He is generally healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no vaccine allergies. He asks whether he can still do anything to reduce his risk before leaving and says he prefers the quickest option. On further review, he explains that he is leaving for a private resort stay with bottled water, prearranged meals, and no planned exposure to local community settings.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The patient still appears to be a susceptible traveler with imminent departure to Peru, but the itinerary is narrowly limited to a highly controlled resort setting rather than routine travel with meaningful exposure risk.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Give hepatitis A vaccine immediately because he is traveling to Peru soon.
Tell him protection may be incomplete for the next 2-4 weeks but vaccinate now anyway.
Treat him as needing pre-departure hepatitis A prophylaxis without further assessment.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This vignette is not a clean match for the travel indication because the itinerary is a low-exposure, controlled resort stay and does not clearly establish the kind of travel exposure that would trigger the recommendation. A safer interpretation is to assess actual exposure risk and itinerary details before assuming the pretravel hepatitis A rule applies.
challenging_examples.rationale: The source recommendation is for susceptible persons traveling to countries with high or intermediate hepatitis A endemicity. The travel destination is similar, but the exposure profile is materially different and makes the indication less straightforward.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults aged 65 years or older
selected_rules.execute: implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death
selected_rules.source_statement: Falls are the leading cause of injury deaths and disabilities among persons aged ≥65 years.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults at risk of falling
selected_rules.execute: use multifaceted fall prevention programs that combine exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction
selected_rules.source_statement: Research has established that effective fall intervention programs employ a multifaceted approach and incorporate both behavioral and environmental elements: exercises to improve strength and balance, environmental modifications, education about fall prevention, medication review and assessment to minimize side effects, and risk factor reduction.

selected_rules.activation_condition: community-dwelling older adults with fall risk factors
selected_rules.execute: provide behavioral instructions, targeted training, exercise to increase strength, and medication adjustments
selected_rules.source_statement: A total of 153 persons participated in an intervention that consisted of behavioral instructions and training to reduce specific risk factors ... exercise programs to increase strength, and medication adjustments.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults living in the community
selected_rules.execute: increase physical activity that improves strength, balance, and coordination
selected_rules.source_statement: Activities that improve strength, balance, and coordination can reduce the risk for falls and fall-related injuries among healthy and frail persons.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults living at home
selected_rules.execute: address home hazards and use home-visiting programs to identify and correct fall risks
selected_rules.source_statement: Because approximately 50%-60% of all falls among older adults occur at home, fall prevention programs should address home hazards that can contribute to falls.

selected_rules.activation_condition: older adults with chronic diseases or hip fracture risk factors
selected_rules.execute: maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss
selected_rules.source_statement: Medical approaches might include reducing fall risk factors (e.g., maximizing control of concomitant chronic diseases) and reducing hip fracture risk factors (e.g., counseling women aged ≥65 years against inappropriate weight loss).

selected_rules.activation_condition: nursing-home residents at high risk of falls
selected_rules.execute: implement intensive multifaceted interventions including environmental modifications, medication review, and attention to individual needs
selected_rules.source_statement: A randomized trial ... evaluated an intensive, multifaceted intervention of extensive environmental modifications ... medication review, and increased attention to individual resident needs.

selected_rules.activation_condition: women starting an exercise program
selected_rules.execute: start slowly at a level matched to current physical fitness and set realistic goals
selected_rules.source_statement: Women starting exercise programs should be realistic about their goals and start slowly at frequency, duration, and intensity levels commensurate with their current physical fitness condition.

source_case.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks.
recommended_actions: Implement fall prevention efforts to reduce injury, disability, and death.
Use a multifaceted fall prevention program that combines exercise, environmental modifications, education, medication review, and risk factor reduction.
Address home hazards and consider a home-visiting program to identify and correct fall risks.
Maximize control of concomitant chronic diseases and counsel against inappropriate weight loss if relevant.
source_case.rationale: She is an older adult aged 65 years or older, which triggers general fall prevention. She is also at risk of falling due to prior falls, unsteadiness, home hazards, and medications, supporting a multifaceted approach. Because she lives at home, home hazard assessment and correction are indicated. Her chronic disease burden supports attention to optimizing chronic disease control.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. At today’s visit, however, she says she has already moved to an assisted-living apartment with staff support and no longer manages her own home environment.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: Nearly identical fall-risk profile, age, medications, and unsteadiness, but she is no longer living at home, so home hazard assessment is not directly indicated in the same way.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Order a home visiting program to identify and remove loose rugs and lighting hazards
Treat this as a standard at-home fall-prevention referral
Document home environmental modification as the main intervention
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This remains an older adult with fall risk, so general fall-prevention measures still apply, but the specific home-hazard/home-visiting recommendation is not guideline-concordant because she is no longer living at home. Safer interpretation: focus on multifactorial fall prevention and assess the assisted-living environment through the facility’s own safety processes rather than a home visit.
challenging_examples.rationale: Rule R005 requires older adults living at home. The new living arrangement removes the key activation condition while preserving the rest of the fall-risk narrative.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: Mrs. L is a 78-year-old woman who lives independently in her own home. Over the past 6 months, she has had two near-falls while carrying laundry down the stairs and one minor fall in the kitchen, though she was not seriously injured. She has osteoarthritis and hypertension, takes a diuretic and a sedating sleep aid, and says she feels unsteady when getting up at night. Her daughter notes that the hallway is dimly lit and that loose rugs are present near the bedroom and bathroom. Mrs. L wants to stay active but has become less confident walking outside and has stopped attending her weekly neighborhood walks. She also reports a 14-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 8 months after starting a restrictive diet prescribed for a newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes program.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The case still looks like a classic older adult fall-prevention visit, with chronic disease, unsteadiness, and home hazards, but the weight loss is intentional and part of diabetes management rather than inappropriate weight loss.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Counsel her to stop the diet because of fracture risk
Apply the chronic-disease/weight-loss recommendation as written
Recommend weight gain solely to reduce falls
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: She remains appropriate for general fall-prevention counseling, but the specific instruction to counsel against inappropriate weight loss is not clearly applicable here because the weight loss is intentional and tied to diabetes management. The safer interpretation is to review whether the diet is overly restrictive or causing frailty, while also optimizing diabetes control and overall nutrition rather than automatically discouraging weight loss.
challenging_examples.rationale: R006 applies to women aged 65 years or older when counseling against inappropriate weight loss is relevant. This vignette shifts the weight loss to a purposeful treatment plan, making the recommendation non-concordant as stated.

selected_rules.activation_condition: planning school food environment
selected_rules.execute: Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
selected_rules.source_statement: In addition, schools should discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars (e.g., candy, fried chips, and soda) on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.

selected_rules.activation_condition: teachers use food in classroom management
selected_rules.execute: Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
selected_rules.source_statement: Schools should discourage teachers from using food for disciplining or rewarding students.

selected_rules.activation_condition: school-based nutrition education is being planned or implemented
selected_rules.execute: Use a comprehensive school health program to promote healthy eating.
selected_rules.source_statement: School-based programs can play an important role in promoting lifelong healthy eating.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
selected_rules.source_statement: Policy: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
selected_rules.source_statement: A school nutrition policy should be a brief document that incorporates input from all relevant constituents of the school community: students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
selected_rules.source_statement: The policy should meet local needs and be adapted to the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
selected_rules.source_statement: The policy plan should include means of obtaining follow-up input from all parties and means of revising the plan as needed.

selected_rules.activation_condition: developing school nutrition policy
selected_rules.execute: Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
selected_rules.source_statement: A nutrition advisory committee or a nutrition subcommittee of the school health advisory council having student members can develop and promulgate a coordinated school nutrition policy.

source_case.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating without ignoring local food traditions or the needs of different student groups.
recommended_actions: Adopt a coordinated school nutrition policy that promotes healthy eating through classroom lessons and a supportive school environment.
Include input from students, teachers, coaches, staff, administrators, food service personnel, nurses, counselors, public health professionals, and parents.
Adapt the policy to local needs and the health concerns, food preferences, and dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups.
Include follow-up input and a process for revising the policy as needed.
Use a nutrition advisory committee or nutrition subcommittee with student members to develop and promulgate the policy.
Discourage the sale of foods high in fat, sodium, and added sugars on school grounds and as part of fundraising activities.
Discourage teachers from using food to reward or discipline students.
source_case.rationale: The vignette centers on developing a school nutrition policy, which triggers the policy-related recommendations. It also includes planning a school food environment and teacher use of food for classroom management, triggering the corresponding discouragement recommendations. No unsupported recommendations were added.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating, but the draft proposal would be limited to the middle school only and would not be shared with the school health advisory council because the district wants a quick rollout before the semester ends.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: The setting still appears to be a school nutrition policy meeting with the same stakeholders and concerns, but the process has been narrowed to exclude broad constituent input and student-member committee involvement.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Approve the draft as a coordinated school nutrition policy without further stakeholder review.
Use the existing administrators and nurse alone to finalize the policy and promulgate it immediately.
Skip the nutrition advisory committee because the timeline is short.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the policy process intentionally omits input from key school constituents and does not use a nutrition advisory committee or subcommittee with student members. The safer interpretation is that the district should pause final adoption and obtain broader stakeholder participation before promulgating the policy.
challenging_examples.rationale: The vignette preserves the school nutrition policy workflow, but the required inclusive input and student-member advisory structure are missing, invalidating the corresponding recommendations.

challenging_examples.patient_vignette: A 15-year-old student is brought to a school health planning meeting after the district notices inconsistent food practices across campuses. The middle school is drafting a new nutrition policy because vending machines, classroom celebrations, and fundraisers currently vary by teacher and club. Parents have asked for healthier options, while students want foods that fit cultural preferences and after-school sports schedules. The school nurse reports that some teachers use candy as rewards, and the PTA is considering a fundraiser built around soda and chips. Administrators want a policy that can be rolled out this semester but also updated after feedback from families, staff, and students. The team is looking for a practical plan that supports healthy eating, but the current draft sets a one-size-fits-all menu and explicitly says it will not be revised after implementation because the district plans to keep the language identical across all campuses for at least three years.
challenging_examples.deceptive_similarity: It remains a realistic school nutrition policy discussion with the same concerns about foods, rewards, and fundraisers, but the draft ignores local cultural and socioeconomic tailoring and removes any mechanism for follow-up revision.
likely_but_incorrect_actions: Treat the draft as an acceptable district nutrition policy and implement it unchanged.
Assume the same policy language can be copied to all campuses without modification.
Finalize the plan now and defer any feedback or revision until several years later.
challenging_examples.corrected_assessment: This is not guideline-concordant because the policy is not adapted to local needs or the dietary practices of different ethnic and socioeconomic groups, and it lacks follow-up input and a revision process. The safer interpretation is that the district should revise the draft to incorporate local tailoring and a mechanism for future feedback before adoption.
challenging_examples.rationale: The case still looks like a standard school nutrition policy effort, but the absence of local adaptation and revision planning breaks two key recommendations.
